dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with creditReconcileDelta null, a transparency note rather than a defect for a certificate; six 3-credit courses are shown, so which courses are required versus optional and the total credit count are not transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate's total credits and identify the required core (e.g., CE 610/611/616) versus elective legal/management courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The six courses carry clear, specific descriptions of managerial, scheduling, estimating, and legal content, and the page is readable; however, it lacks a credit total, a required/elective breakdown, and outcomes, and EM 631 Legal Aspects in Environmental Engineering sits slightly oddly beside the construction-focused EM 632.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a certificate overview with credit total and required/elective structure, and confirm whether EM 631 (environmental law) or EM 632 (construction law) is the intended legal requirement.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The content is solid, employable construction-management practice, cost estimating (CE 616), scheduling and resource leveling (CE 611), construction law (EM 632), and management science (EM 602), but it misses the field's modern digital and delivery shifts (BIM/VDC, lean construction, sustainability/LEED, construction safety, and integrated project delivery).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a BIM/virtual-design-and-construction elective and a lean/sustainable-construction module so the certificate reflects current delivery practice, not only traditional estimating and scheduling.

descriptionQuality.justification: The six descriptions are present and specific (CE 611's network scheduling and time-cost analysis, CE 616's full range of estimating methods and computerized takeoff, EM 602's linear programming and queueing, EM 632's contract and third-party-liability content), giving strong, informative course-level detail.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and internally consistent: CE 611 and CE 616 both clearly require CE 610, and EM 602 states 'undergraduate calculus and probability and statistics'; the nine dangling references are undergraduate background rather than internal inconsistencies.

missingTopics.topic: BIM and virtual design and construction
missingTopics.rationale: The certificate covers estimating and scheduling (CE 616, CE 611) but no BIM/VDC, now central to construction coordination and quantity takeoff.
Lean methods (Last Planner, pull planning) are standard practice and would extend CE 611's planning-and-control scope.
No safety/OSHA or risk-of-injury course appears, a core competency for construction managers.
Sustainable delivery and green-building certification are expected of modern construction managers but are absent from the course set.

emergingTrends.rationale: Model-based coordination and 4D/5D scheduling are reshaping the field and would modernize CE 611 and CE 616.

emergingTrends.trend: Data analytics and AI in project controls
emergingTrends.rationale: Predictive cost/schedule analytics build on EM 602's quantitative base and are a growing management skill.
IPD and lean contracting are changing how projects are organized, extending the EM 632 construction-law content.
Low-carbon materials and resilient delivery are rising client demands the certificate could position for.

topSuggestions: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes describing project-management, estimating, and construction-law competencies.
State total credits and the required (CE 610/611/616) versus elective structure so the completion path is transparent.
Add a BIM/VDC elective and a lean/sustainable-construction module to reflect current delivery practice.
Add a construction-safety-management course to close a core professional gap.
Confirm the intended legal requirement (EM 632 construction law versus EM 631 environmental law) and add an integrating capstone.

data-mining-259.name: Data Mining

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 10-course pool forms a coherent data-mining pathway from data foundations (CS 631 Data Management System Design, IS 601 Web Systems Development) through core mining (CS 634 Data Mining, IS 665 Data Analytics for Info System) to applications (IS 634 Information Retrieval, IS 687 Transaction Mining and Fraud Detection, IS 688 Web Mining, MGMT 635 Data Mining and Analysis). The applied-mining courses build on analytics (IS 601 -> IS 665 -> IS 687/688, depth 2), giving a sensible progression for a focused credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate CS 634 (or IS 665) as an explicit required anchor and group the applications (IS 687, IS 688, IS 634) as electives so the intended path is visible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and appropriately shallow for a certificate: missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) and deepestPrereqChain=2 via the IS 601 -> IS 665 -> IS 687/IS 688 chain, providing real sequencing into the applied-mining courses without over-constraining entry.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit CS 634 (or statistics) prerequisite to the application courses so mining fundamentals precede fraud/web-mining electives.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No certificate-level learning outcomes are present in the data; only a course pool is exposed, which is a gap for a professional credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 outcomes (e.g., build classification/clustering models, mine web and transaction data, evaluate mining results) mapped to CS 634, IS 688, and IS 687.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null; for a graduate certificate this is a credit-transparency note rather than a hard defect, and all sampled courses are 3 credits, but the required total/number of courses is not published, so the footprint is only inferable.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate's total credits and required course count (e.g., 12 credits / four courses) to make the scope explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are substantive and the mining focus is legible, but requirementGroups=0 leaves no plan/overview, and several prerequisiteRaw fields contain description text rather than clean prerequisites (IS 631, IS 634, IS 665, IS 687 each run on into descriptive prose).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a certificate overview with required/elective grouping and clean the prerequisite fields that currently embed course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Classic data-mining currency is solid (CS 634 Data Mining, IS 634 Information Retrieval, IS 688 Web Mining, IS 687 Transaction Mining and Fraud Detection), but the emphasis leans traditional: there is little modern ML-based mining, no deep-learning or graph-mining coverage, and no big-data/streaming mining, so the credential reads slightly dated relative to current practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize the pool with an ML/deep-learning-based mining elective and a graph- or streaming-mining option to complement the classical CS 634 core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and informative (e.g., CS 634 details association/dependency analysis, classification, prediction, and clustering; IS 688 describes mining hyperlink structure, content, and usage logs for e-commerce and IR), with no thin or placeholder entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites exist and are internally consistent with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), and simple gates are clear (IS 688 'IS 665'), but several prerequisiteRaw fields are contaminated with description text (IS 631, IS 634, IS 665, IS 687), and CS 602's prerequisite field holds a description fragment rather than a prerequisite.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 634 covers classical association/clustering methods but no modern supervised/deep-learning mining course appears, a core expectation today.
With web mining (IS 688) present, the absence of graph/network-mining coursework is a notable gap for contemporary data mining.
The pool mines modest datasets; no course addresses mining at scale on distributed platforms, limiting relevance to large-data settings.
IS 634 Information Retrieval touches text, but a dedicated text-mining/NLP course would reflect how unstructured data is mined now.

emergingTrends.rationale: Adding a neural-methods mining elective would modernize the classical CS 634 core toward current representation-learning practice.
Extending IS 688 Web Mining with GNN-based network analysis would align the certificate with a fast-growing mining subfield.
Fraud detection (IS 687) is increasingly real-time; a streaming-mining module would keep that application current.
Transaction and web mining raise privacy concerns; a governance/privacy component would reflect modern professional expectations.

topSuggestions: Publish the certificate's total credits and required course count for credit transparency.
Add a modern ML/deep-learning-based mining elective and a graph- or streaming-mining option.
Designate an explicit required anchor (CS 634) with grouped electives, and publish learning outcomes.
Clean the prerequisite fields that currently embed course descriptions (IS 631, IS 634, IS 665, IS 687).
Fix CS 602's mis-mapped prerequisite field so prerequisite clarity is consistent.

data-science-for-chemical-and-materials-.name: Data Science for Chemical and Materials Engineers

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: For a focused graduate certificate the seven referenced courses form a coherent, well-layered menu: a statistics foundation (MATH 661 Applied Statistics, MATH 662 Probability Distributions, MATH 678 Statistical Methods in Data Science), data mining (CS 634), applied machine learning (MTEN 633), a domain data-science course (MTEN 631), and data communication/visualization (CHE 632). The pieces build logically from statistics to ML to domain application, though as a course menu (requirementGroups 0) the specific required subset is not shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required-versus-elective structure (e.g., which four of the seven courses constitute the certificate) so the intended path through the statistics-to-ML progression is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and sensibly shallow for a certificate: missingCourseCount is 0 and the deepest chain is 1 (MATH 678 builds on MATH 661/663), with MATH 662 gating on MATH 341/333 and the MTEN/CHE courses gating on a relevant B.S. degree. No dangling references and no over-constraining appear.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit course-code prerequisites to CS 634 Data Mining (currently null) so the statistics-to-mining dependency matches the clarity of the MATH sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are stated in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what competencies (e.g., building predictive models on process/materials data) the certificate certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 learning outcomes tied to concrete skills such as statistical modeling, machine-learning workflow, and engineering-data visualization, mapped to MATH 678, MTEN 633, and CHE 632.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a graduate certificate the null statedTotalCredits and null creditReconcileDelta are a transparency note rather than a defect; the constituent courses are uniformly 3 credits, so a typical four-course/12-credit credential is implied but not stated. The credential is appropriately focused for its scope.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate's total credits and the exact number of courses required (e.g., 12 credits / four courses from the listed menu) so credit expectations are transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific (e.g., MTEN 633 'builds upon foundational knowledge in mathematical and statistical tools... to apply machine learning approaches for solving problems in Engineering'), and titles clearly convey scope. The page lacks a stated total-credit figure and an explicit required-course list, which a certificate page should carry.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credential summary block (total credits, required courses, target audience) above the course list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: This is a distinctly current credential: it pairs modern data-science method courses (CS 634 Data Mining, MTEN 633 Machine Learning, MATH 678 Statistical Methods in Data Science) with domain application to chemical and materials engineering (MTEN 631) and data communication (CHE 632), directly matching industry demand for engineers who can model process and materials data. The main currency gap is the absence of explicit programming and deep-learning content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or reference a scientific-programming/Python component and a deep-learning module so graduates can implement, not just conceptually apply, modern ML pipelines.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and audience-specific rather than placeholder: CHE 632 details theoretical and practical aspects of communicating engineering data, and MTEN 631 specifies data-science techniques for chemical/materials problems. Excerpts are truncated but clearly full entries exist for all seven courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0): the MATH sequence uses explicit course codes (MATH 678 requires MATH 661/663; MATH 662 requires MATH 341/333) and the MTEN/CHE courses state a required B.S. background. Only CS 634's null prerequisite slightly reduces consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The menu covers statistics, mining, and ML conceptually but no course explicitly teaches Python/NumPy/scikit-learn implementation, the practical backbone of applying these methods to CHE/MSE data.
MTEN 633 covers general machine learning, but there is no dedicated deep-learning course (CNNs for microstructure images, graph neural networks for molecules) now central to materials informatics.

missingTopics.topic: Design of experiments and Bayesian optimization
missingTopics.rationale: MATH 661/662 cover classical statistics, yet DOE and Bayesian optimization for experiment-efficient materials/process discovery are absent despite being core to engineering data science.
For engineering decisions, quantifying model uncertainty and building surrogate models of simulations is essential and not visibly covered by the current statistics-to-ML menu.
CS 634 teaches data mining generally, but working with materials informatics repositories (e.g., Materials Project) and cleaning noisy sensor/process data is a distinct skill not represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: Embedding conservation laws into ML models is transforming chemical/materials modeling; MTEN 633 is the natural course to add physics-informed neural-network content.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative models for molecular and materials design
emergingTrends.rationale: Diffusion and generative models now propose novel molecules and microstructures, a forward direction the domain course MTEN 631 could incorporate.

emergingTrends.trend: Foundation models and LLMs for scientific literature/data
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM-assisted extraction of properties from literature and code generation for analysis is entering research workflows and complements CHE 632's data-communication focus.
Closed-loop optimization of experiments couples ML with automation and is a high-growth application area for engineers trained by this certificate.

emergingTrends.trend: Explainable and trustworthy AI for engineering decisions
emergingTrends.rationale: As models drive process and design decisions, interpretability and validation are increasingly required, extending the statistical rigor of the MATH sequence into ML.

topSuggestions: Publish the certificate's total credits and required-course structure (e.g., which four of the seven courses are required) to replace the null statedTotalCredits.
Add an explicit scientific-programming (Python) component so graduates can implement the ML/data-mining methods, not only apply them conceptually.
State 3-4 learning outcomes tied to statistical modeling, ML workflow, and engineering-data visualization, mapped to MATH 678, MTEN 633, and CHE 632.
Introduce deep-learning content (CNNs for microstructure, graph neural networks for molecules) into or alongside MTEN 633 to cover the field's current frontier.
Add course-code prerequisites to CS 634 Data Mining to match the clarity of the MATH sequence.

data-visualization-260.name: Data Visualization

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The page is mis-scoped: referencedCourses=410 and every sampled course is an unrelated biology offering (BIOL 593-BIOL 790C: anatomy, ecology, neurophysiology, dissertation research), with no data-visualization course visible at all. For what should be a focused visualization certificate, no coherent visualization pathway can be discerned from the parsed content.
dimensions.suggestion: Re-scope the page to the actual visualization course set (e.g., DS 650 / IS 650 Data Visualization and a short curated elective list) so a coherent credential is shown instead of the full graduate catalog.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is unreliable because of the mis-scope: deepestPrereqChain=5 is meaningless when it is computed over 410 unrelated courses, and missingCourseCount=45 (dangling references including BIOL 590/591, CHEM 502/590, CS 113, CS 666, EE 657, ENG 541) reflects a broken, over-broad course pull rather than a designed prerequisite structure.
dimensions.suggestion: After re-scoping to real visualization courses, rebuild the prerequisite view; the current 45 dangling references should disappear once the correct three-to-four courses are listed.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes are present, and the underlying content is not even the correct program, so nothing evaluable about a visualization credential appears on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 visualization outcomes (e.g., apply perceptual encoding principles, build interactive/web visualizations, design dashboards) once the correct courses are attached.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The credit data is incoherent: statedTotalCredits=3.0 is implausibly small for a certificate, yet the page references 410 courses with summedTermCredits=null and creditReconcileDelta=null, so there is no meaningful or transparent credit footprint to evaluate.
dimensions.suggestion: Set the correct certificate total (typically ~12 credits / four courses) and attach only the credits of the real visualization courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: This is a catalog-integrity failure: a 'Data Visualization' certificate page lists hundreds of biology and chemistry courses (BIOL 601 Computational Biology, BIOL 646 Endocrinology, dissertation-research placeholders), one with an empty description (BIOL 593), giving a reader no usable view of the credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the underlying data source/parse so the page renders only the visualization certificate's courses, then add an overview and plan.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Currency in data visualization cannot be assessed because no visualization courses are present; the displayed biology/chemistry content is irrelevant to the field the certificate names.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the page with actual visualization courses (perception, D3/interactive viz, dashboarding) so its relevance to current practice can be judged.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present for many of the (wrong) biology courses and are individually substantive, but at least one is empty (BIOL 593 Graduate Co-Op Work Exp IV) and, critically, none describe the visualization content the certificate is supposed to convey, so description quality for the actual program cannot be validated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite clarity is undermined by the mis-scope: missingCourseCount=45 dangling references and a spurious depth-5 chain arise from pulling the entire graduate catalog, so the prerequisites shown do not belong to a data-visualization credential.

missingTopics.rationale: A visualization certificate must teach human perception and encoding channels; the page shows no such course (e.g., a DS 650 Data Visualization anchor is absent from the sample).
No coursework on interactive/web visualization appears, yet it is the core practical skill of the field.
Professional visualization work centers on dashboard tools, none of which are represented in the parsed content.
Maps and high-dimensional/embedding visualization are standard topics missing from the biology-dominated course set.

emergingTrends.rationale: Natural-language-to-chart tooling is reshaping the field and should feature in a modern visualization certificate once it is correctly scoped.
Visualizing model behavior is a growing demand area that a data-science visualization credential should target.
Live operational dashboards are now standard; the certificate should include streaming-visualization content.
Accessibility standards and immersive analytics are emerging directions the credential should position for.

topSuggestions: Fix the catalog data/parse: the page currently pulls ~410 unrelated biology/chemistry courses instead of the visualization certificate's courses.
Attach the real visualization course set (e.g., DS 650 / IS 650 Data Visualization plus curated electives) and correct statedTotalCredits (~12).
Rebuild the prerequisite view after re-scoping so the 45 dangling references are eliminated.
Publish visualization-specific learning outcomes (perceptual encoding, interactive viz, dashboarding).
Add missing core visualization topics: web/interactive (D3), BI dashboarding, and geospatial/high-dimensional visualization.

digital-arts-essentials-175.name: Digital Arts Essentials

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The certificate assembles a well-rounded digital-arts foundation across two requirement groups: a 2D/3D and time-based core (Graphic Design and Color Theory DD 601, 3D Modelling DD 602, Video and Animation DD 603) plus a two-course art/design history sequence (DD 610-611), complemented by History of Games (DD 631) and Storyboarding (DD 622). Breadth and a light progression (DD 622 at depth 1) are appropriate, though the mix leans heavily on survey history for a hands-on 'essentials' credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebalance slightly toward studio practice, e.g., make one history course optional in favor of a contemporary tools/creative-coding elective, so making outweighs surveying.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and self-contained: deepestPrereqChain is 1, missingCourseCount is 0, and DD 622's prerequisites (DD 601, DD 602) are both included within the certificate, so students meet them internally.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the graph self-contained as courses are added, and state the DD 601-602 to DD 622 dependency explicitly so students sequence correctly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes are published; a foundation certificate especially benefits from stating the concrete competencies (2D/3D design, motion, art-historical literacy) a completer gains.
dimensions.suggestion: Add three to five outcomes describing what a graduate can produce and analyze across 2D, 3D, and time-based media.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The credit structure reconciles cleanly (creditReconcileDelta 0; the two groups of 15 and 12 credits sum to the stated 27), giving better transparency than the sibling animation certificate. At 27 credits it is large for a 'certificate', functioning more like a full concentration, which is worth signaling to students.
dimensions.suggestion: Clarify how the two identically sized-purpose groups combine (all required vs. select-from) and note that 27 credits is a substantial, concentration-scale commitment.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are specific and informative, but the two requirement groups carry the identical uninformative heading 'What are the Required Courses?' and course titles include draft notes such as '(new course proposal forthcoming)', making the structure harder to read than it should be.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the two groups distinct, descriptive titles (e.g., 'Foundation Core' and 'Media and Context') and strip the proposal annotations from the final catalog copy.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The foundation is solid and transferable (design, color, 3D, video/animation, plus historical grounding), but it is comparatively dated in emphasis, two survey-history courses and no contemporary practice such as motion graphics, creative coding, generative tools, or web/interactive design.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a contemporary-media elective (motion graphics or creative coding) so the 'essentials' reflect current digital-arts practice, not only historical grounding.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., color theory applied to 2D composition, 3D modelling as describing space/shape/form in digital media, history surveys with a global development focus), clearly full entries rather than placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most foundation courses correctly carry no prerequisite, and the one sequenced course, DD 622, states explicit, internally satisfiable code prerequisites (DD 601, DD 602), so prerequisite information is clear and consistent.

missingTopics.rationale: A modern digital-arts foundation should include a Processing/p5.js/TouchDesigner-style course; the current set has no programmatic or generative making.
Video and Animation is present, but there is no dedicated motion-graphics/compositing course, a staple of contemporary digital-arts employment.
Graphic Design and Color Theory covers composition and color, but typographic craft, foundational to design practice, is not explicitly represented.
With no interactive or web-based course, the certificate omits a primary medium in which digital art is produced and distributed today.

missingTopics.topic: Digital painting, illustration, and concept art
missingTopics.rationale: Concept-art and digital-illustration skills bridge fine art and production pipelines and would strengthen the studio side of this foundation.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI in art and design
emergingTrends.rationale: Text-to-image and AI-assisted design tools are transforming digital-arts practice; a foundation certificate should engage them critically alongside color and composition.
AR/VR and immersive installation are fast-growing venues for digital art that the 3D and video strengths here could extend into.
Game engines are increasingly used as art-making and installation tools, a natural complement to the existing History of Games course.
Art driven by data and code is a prominent contemporary movement that the certificate's history-heavy framing does not yet address.
Evolving models for distributing and attributing digital work are reshaping artists' careers and belong in a current professional foundation.

topSuggestions: Add a contemporary studio elective (creative coding, motion graphics, or generative art) to modernize the largely historical emphasis.
Give the two requirement groups distinct, descriptive titles and remove draft/proposal annotations from titles.
Publish concrete completion outcomes across 2D, 3D, and time-based media.
Clarify the required-versus-select structure and flag the 27-credit, concentration-scale commitment.
Consider making one of the two history surveys optional to free room for current-practice content.

environmental-engineering-494.name: ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The nine referenced courses form a coherent environmental-engineering core spanning water/wastewater treatment (ENE 664 Physical and Chemical Treatment, ENE 665 Biological Treatment), remediation and waste (ENE 662 Site Remediation, CE 647 Geotechnical Aspects of Solid Waste), supporting science (ENE 661 Environmental Microbiology, ENE 630 Physical Processes of Environmental Systems), and assessment/regulation (ENE 671 Environmental Impact Analysis, ENE 672 Stormwater Management, EM 631 Legal Aspects), with some internal sequencing (ENE 662 from EM 631; ENE 664 from ENE 663).
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into visible tracks (treatment, remediation, assessment) and designate a required core so the coherent content also reads as a structured completion path.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This certificate has the cleanest reference health in the set: missingCourseCount is 0, so no dangling references, and deepestPrereqChain is 1 with real internal gates (ENE 662 requires EM 631, ENE 664 requires ENE 663, CE 647 requires CE 341/341A), showing deliberate sequencing rather than a flat menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Make the treatment prerequisite explicit on the page (e.g., ensure ENE 663 is shown as the gate for ENE 664) so the intended treatment sequence is visible to students.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No certificate-level learning outcomes are present, so a prospective environmental professional cannot see the competencies (treatment-process design, remediation, impact assessment) the credential certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes describing the treatment, remediation, and environmental-assessment skills a completer gains.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with creditReconcileDelta null, a transparency note rather than a defect for a certificate; nine 3-credit courses are shown, so the total and the required-versus-elective split are not transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate's total credits and identify the required treatment/assessment core versus electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Presentation is weakened by data-cleanliness issues: the program name is rendered in all caps ('ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING'), ENE 630 and ENE 661 have empty descriptions, and ENE 665 and ENE 671 excerpts begin with stray fragments ('Pre or (May be taken concurrently.)' and 'Pre or A graduate course') that read as parsing artifacts rather than clean descriptions.
dimensions.suggestion: Normalize the program title to title case and repair the empty (ENE 630, ENE 661) and fragmented (ENE 665, ENE 671) course descriptions so the page reads cleanly.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core is solid and employable, physical/chemical and biological treatment, site remediation, environmental microbiology, and impact analysis, but it misses several current priorities: emerging contaminants (PFAS, microplastics), water reuse, air-quality/GHG, and environmental data analytics, and notably lacks the sustainability/life-cycle content (ENE 673) that the sibling climate certificate carries.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an emerging-contaminants/water-reuse elective and a sustainability/life-cycle-analysis course (e.g., ENE 673) so the certificate reflects current environmental-engineering practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Description quality is uneven: strong entries exist (CE 647's solid-waste geotechnics, ENE 662's start-to-finish remediation scope, ENE 664's treatment unit-operations list, ENE 672's stormwater BMP coverage), but ENE 630 and ENE 661 are empty and ENE 665/ENE 671 open with stray 'Pre or' fragments, so nearly half the courses are deficient.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are internally consistent with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0): ENE 662 clearly builds on EM 631, ENE 664 on ENE 663, and CE 647 on CE 341/341A; clarity is only reduced by the stray 'Pre or' fragments that muddle where a prerequisite statement begins.

missingTopics.rationale: Treatment courses (ENE 664/665) cover conventional processes but not trace/persistent contaminants, now central to environmental-engineering practice.
The set covers treatment and remediation but not potable reuse or nutrient/energy recovery, a major modern direction for the field.
The certificate is water/waste-centric with no air-quality or GHG-mitigation content, a notable coverage gap.
ENE 673 Sustainability and Life Cycle Analysis exists in the department but is absent here, leaving lifecycle thinking out of the environmental core.

emergingTrends.rationale: Destruction/removal of persistent contaminants is the field's fastest-growing area and would extend ENE 664/665.
Integrated potable-reuse systems are becoming standard practice, building on the existing treatment courses.
Real-time monitoring and data-driven process control are emerging skills absent from the current core.
Carbon and life-cycle accounting of treatment/remediation is a rising expectation the certificate could adopt via ENE 673.

topSuggestions: Repair the presentation defects: normalize the all-caps title and fix the empty (ENE 630, ENE 661) and fragmented (ENE 665, ENE 671) course descriptions.
Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes describing treatment, remediation, and assessment competencies.
State total credits and the required-versus-elective structure so the completion path is transparent.
Add an emerging-contaminants/water-reuse elective and a sustainability/life-cycle course (ENE 673) to modernize the core.
Group courses into visible treatment/remediation/assessment tracks with a designated required core.

environmental-science-and-engineering-32.name: Environmental Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a focused graduate certificate the menu of 17 courses coheres around environmental problem-solving, bridging engineering treatment (ENE 664 Physical/Chemical Treatment, ENE 665 Biological Treatment, ENE 662 Site Remediation), science (EVSC 610 Environmental Chemical Science, EVSC 612 Environmental Analysis, EVSC 616 Toxicology), and policy/impact (ENE 671 Environmental Impact Analysis, EM 631 Legal Aspects, EPS 622 Sustainable Politics). However with requirementGroups 0 and no core-vs-elective designation it reads as an eligible-course list rather than a designed pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate a small required spine (e.g., one treatment, one analysis, and one policy course) so the certificate presents a defined progression instead of an undifferentiated 17-course menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy for a certificate: missingCourseCount is 0 and deepestPrereqChain is only 1, with courses largely standalone; the two chained cases are ENE 662 (requires EM 631) and ENE 664 (requires ENE 663). CE 647 reaches back to undergraduate CE 341/341A, which is appropriate signposting rather than a dangling reference.
dimensions.suggestion: Confirm ENE 663 (the prerequisite of ENE 664) is offered on a cadence certificate students can reach, or flag ENE 664 as part of a two-course treatment sub-sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or competency statements are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what the certificate certifies beyond the course titles.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish three or four competency outcomes (e.g., interpret environmental analytical data, evaluate remediation options, apply environmental regulations) for the certificate.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is null, which for a graduate certificate is a transparency note rather than a defect; every listed course is 3 credits, implying a conventional ~4-course/12-credit credential. The focus is appropriate but the required credit count and number of courses are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits required (e.g., 12) and how many of the 17 listed courses a student must complete to earn the certificate.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most course descriptions are present, specific, and informative (e.g., ENE 664, ENE 672 Stormwater Management, EVSC 610), but two courses, ENE 630 Physical Processes of Environmental Systems and ENE 661 Environmental Microbiology, have blank descriptions, and there is no plan of study or stated credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Fill in the missing descriptions for ENE 630 and ENE 661 and add a short credit/course-count summary at the top of the certificate page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set reflects current environmental practice well: site remediation (ENE 662), stormwater BMPs (ENE 672), toxicology and risk (EVSC 616), environmental microbiology (EVSC 627), and energy/sustainability (EVSC 715). It is comparatively light on geospatial analytics and air-quality/climate content, both now central to the field.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a GIS / environmental-data-analytics option (the parent M.S. already offers CE 602 Geographic Information System) so graduates can work with spatial monitoring data.

descriptionQuality.justification: The majority of the 17 descriptions are substantive and specific (ENE 664 on coagulation/flocculation/filtration, EVSC 610 on pollutant transport and decomposition), but ENE 630 and ENE 661 have entirely empty description excerpts, dragging down an otherwise informative set.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), e.g., ENE 662 requires EM 631 and ENE 664 requires ENE 663; clarity is only slightly reduced by CE 647 and EVSC 627 pointing to undergraduate prerequisites described in the undergraduate catalog.

missingTopics.rationale: The menu is water-, waste-, and soil-heavy (ENE 664/665, CE 647, ENE 662) with no dedicated air-pollution or emissions-control course.
Quantitative spatial analysis underpins modern environmental monitoring, yet no GIS course appears in this certificate menu even though the department offers CE 602.
Only EVSC 715 Energy and Sustainability touches energy; there is no course on carbon accounting or climate mitigation, now core to environmental practice.
The treatment and remediation courses (ENE 664/665, ENE 662) are framed around conventional pollutants and do not address the PFAS/emerging-contaminant problems dominating current regulation.
Policy coverage is legal and political (EM 631, EPS 622) with no explicit equity or community-engagement content that agencies now expect.

emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor networks and ML-driven analytics are transforming water and air monitoring; an analytics elective would extend EVSC 612 Environmental Analysis.
Low-impact development is displacing gray infrastructure; ENE 672 Stormwater Management could be modernized toward green-infrastructure design.
LCA is now standard for sustainability work; EVSC 715 could be paired with the department's ENE 673 Sustainability and Life Cycle Analysis.
Regulatory focus on PFAS makes ENE 662 Site Remediation a natural place to add advanced treatment and destruction technologies.
Energy transition is reshaping environmental engineering; EVSC 715 Energy and Sustainability could expand toward grid, storage, and renewables.

topSuggestions: State the required credit total and number of courses so the certificate's scope is transparent (statedTotalCredits is currently null).
Designate a required core within the 17-course menu so the credential has a defined spine rather than an eligible-course list.
Fill in the missing course descriptions for ENE 630 and ENE 661.
Add a GIS / environmental-data-analytics option (e.g., CE 602) to close the geospatial gap.
Publish three to four competency-based learning outcomes for the certificate.

environmental-science-and-engineering-49.name: Environmental Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 17-course, all-3-credit menu forms a broad, coherent environmental spine spanning treatment (ENE 664 Physical/Chemical, ENE 665 Biological), remediation (ENE 662), environmental chemistry and analysis (EVSC 610, EVSC 612), toxicology (EVSC 616), stormwater (ENE 672), and policy/sustainability (EPS 622, EVSC 715). Breadth is appropriate for an elective-choice certificate, though it reads as a flat survey (deepestPrereqChain=1) and includes redundancy such as two Environmental Microbiology courses (ENE 661 and EVSC 627).
dimensions.suggestion: Group the menu into 2-3 named tracks (e.g., water/wastewater treatment, site remediation, environmental chemistry/policy) so students can assemble a coherent 12-credit focus rather than picking from an undifferentiated list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 and deepestPrereqChain=1, so the graph is clean with no dangling references and one sensible sequenced pair (ENE 662 requires EM 631, which is on the menu). However ENE 664 lists ENE 663 (Water Chemistry) as a prerequisite while ENE 663 is not part of this certificate's list, so a student choosing ENE 664 must reach outside the menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Add ENE 663 to the menu or annotate ENE 664 so its external prerequisite is transparent to certificate students.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see which environmental competencies the credential certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes stating the environmental analysis, treatment-design, and regulatory competencies a completer should demonstrate.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null with no parsed plan, which is expected for a graduate certificate; all 17 referenced courses are uniformly 3 credits, implying a clean ~12-credit / 4-course credential, but the total and any required-versus-elective split are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate total (e.g., 12 credits / 4 courses) and whether any course is required rather than a free elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., ENE 672 stormwater BMPs, EVSC 616 toxicology), but two courses, ENE 630 (Physical Processes of Env Syst) and ENE 661 (Environmental Microbiology), have empty description excerpts, and the program name is stored in all-caps, reducing polish.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the two missing course descriptions (ENE 630, ENE 661) and normalize the program title casing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The menu reflects current environmental practice, remediation (ENE 662), stormwater/BMP design (ENE 672), sustainability and energy (EPS 622, EVSC 715), and analytical chemistry (EVSC 612), mapping well to consulting and regulatory careers, though it is light on emerging-contaminant and data-analytics content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or cross-list an emerging-contaminants (PFAS) elective and an environmental data-analytics/GIS elective to sharpen currency.

descriptionQuality.justification: The majority of the 17 descriptions are specific and course-appropriate, but two (ENE 630, ENE 661) are empty, dragging the aggregate below strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in clear narrative form (e.g., EVSC 627 requires General Biology I/II) with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), but ENE 664's prerequisite ENE 663 sits outside the certificate list, a minor internal inconsistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The treatment and remediation courses (ENE 662, ENE 664) predate the current regulatory focus on PFAS and micropollutants central to today's environmental practice.
The menu covers water, waste, and remediation thoroughly but has no dedicated air-pollution or emissions-control course.
No course teaches spatial analysis or data-driven environmental monitoring, now standard tools alongside EVSC 612 Environmental Analysis.
EVSC 715 and EPS 622 introduce sustainability conceptually but no course teaches quantitative LCA/footprint methods.
Stormwater (ENE 672) is present but climate-driven flood and resilience planning is not, despite an adjacent NJIT certificate on the topic.

emergingTrends.rationale: Regulatory limits are tightening rapidly; ENE 664 physical/chemical treatment should be modernized to include advanced oxidation and adsorption for these compounds.
Sensor networks and machine learning are transforming environmental analysis (EVSC 612), a direction the certificate could position for.

emergingTrends.trend: Water reuse and circular resource recovery
emergingTrends.rationale: Biological and physical-chemical treatment (ENE 665, ENE 664) is moving toward resource recovery and potable reuse.
Stormwater and remediation practice increasingly design for climate extremes, extending ENE 672 and ENE 662.
EPS 622 sustainable politics/policy could grow to include equity-focused, data-supported environmental decision-making.

topSuggestions: Organize the 17-course menu into named tracks (treatment, remediation, chemistry/policy) so a coherent 12-credit focus is legible.
Publish certificate-level learning outcomes and state the total credits / course count.
Populate the empty descriptions for ENE 630 and ENE 661 and fix the all-caps program title.
Add emerging-contaminant (PFAS) and environmental data-analytics/GIS electives to modernize field coverage.
Clarify ENE 664's external prerequisite (ENE 663) for certificate students.

financial-technology-596.name: FINANCIAL TECHNOLOGY

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 11 referenced courses form a coherent FinTech menu with a real spine: FIN 600 (Corporate Finance I) feeds FIN 616 (Data-Driven Financial Modeling), which feeds FIN 620 (Advanced Financial Data Analytics), a genuine depth-2 chain, complemented by FIN 611 (Intro to Topics in FinTech), MGMT 635 (Data Mining), and finance electives (FIN 624/626/641). The set is well-aligned to a fintech credential, but with requirementGroups=0 the required-vs-elective split for the ~4-course certificate is not visible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the fixed required core (e.g., FIN 611 + FIN 616) versus the elective menu so students can see the intended pathway through the certificate.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 2 with missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references): FIN 600 to FIN 616 to FIN 620 is a sensible progression, FIN 611 gates on a prior programming course (Python/Java/MATLAB/C/C++/R), and FIN 624/626/641 all build on FIN 600. This is healthy gating for a certificate without over-constraining.
dimensions.suggestion: Formalize the prose programming requirement on FIN 611/FIN 620 and the 'instructor's approval' escape hatch into a standardized, catalog-visible prerequisite statement.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what competencies the certificate certifies beyond the individual course titles.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes (e.g., build data-driven financial models, apply machine learning to financial data, evaluate fintech platforms and workflows).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a certificate, statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null; all 11 courses are uniform 3.0-credit graduate courses, so the content is well-focused, but with no stated total and no required/elective split it is unclear how many courses (and which) constitute the credential.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement (e.g., 12 credits / four courses) and how many electives count toward it, so credit expectations are transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive (FIN 611, FIN 616, and FIN 620 each carry detailed, non-placeholder text), so the course list is legible, but degree is null, there is no plan-of-study structure, and no credit summary to orient a prospective student.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short structure section to the catalog page listing required and elective courses and the total credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The fintech spine is genuinely current: FIN 611 surveys present-day industry automation trends, FIN 620 teaches Python-based data-driven applications used by large financial institutions, and FIN 616/MGMT 635/FIN 641 cover modeling, data mining, and derivatives. Notable modern gaps remain around blockchain/DLT, algorithmic trading, and RegTech.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated blockchain/distributed-ledger and cryptoassets course, plus algorithmic-trading or RegTech electives, to reach the fintech professional frontier.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present, specific, and informative rather than thin or placeholder; FIN 611, FIN 616, and FIN 620 each describe concrete content (fintech automation trends, financial modeling techniques, Python-based data applications).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly where present (FIN 600 chains for FIN 616/624/626/641; a spelled-out programming requirement on FIN 611) with zero dangling references (missingCourseCount=0); minor softness comes from prose conditions like 'familiarity with at least one programming language' and 'instructor's approval'.

missingTopics.topic: Blockchain / distributed ledger technology and cryptoassets
missingTopics.rationale: No course among FIN 611-620 covers DLT, smart contracts, or crypto, a core fintech pillar.

missingTopics.topic: Algorithmic and high-frequency trading / market microstructure
missingTopics.rationale: FIN 641 covers derivatives and FIN 620 analytics, but systematic trading strategy and execution are not explicit.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning and AI for finance
missingTopics.rationale: MGMT 635 is generic data mining and FIN 620 is analytics; modern ML (deep learning, NLP on financial text) is not named.

missingTopics.topic: RegTech, compliance, and model risk governance
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses regulatory technology, AML/KYC automation, or model-risk governance central to fintech operations.

missingTopics.topic: Payments, digital banking, and open-banking infrastructure
missingTopics.rationale: The certificate lacks explicit coverage of payment rails, APIs/open banking, and neobank business models.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI / LLMs in financial services
emergingTrends.rationale: FIN 620's Python analytics course should incorporate LLM-based document analysis and copilots now reshaping finance.
Building on FIN 626/641, the program should position for on-chain finance and tokenized assets.
FIN 616/620 could modernize to include sentiment, geospatial, and streaming data used by contemporary fintech firms.

emergingTrends.trend: Explainable and responsible AI / model risk
emergingTrends.rationale: As data-driven models drive lending and trading, fairness, explainability, and governance are rising professional priorities.
MIS 645/MRKT 632 could address banking-as-a-service and the platformization of financial products.

topSuggestions: Add a dedicated blockchain/DLT and cryptoassets course, the single largest fintech content gap in the current menu.
Publish 3-5 program-level learning outcomes for the certificate so certified competencies are explicit.
State the total credit / number-of-courses requirement and the required-versus-elective split for transparency.
Modernize FIN 620 and MGMT 635 to explicitly cover machine learning and generative AI for finance.
Add RegTech and algorithmic-trading electives to reach the fintech professional frontier.

foundations-of-cybersecurity-220.name: Foundations of Cybersecurity

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The genuine security spine is sound and well-sequenced: cryptography (CS 608), systems security and privacy (CS 645), network protocol security (CS 646), and network management/security (CS 696), with deepestPrereqChain=2 (CS 656->CS 646->CS 696). However, of the 20 referenced courses the majority are non-security software-engineering, IS, UX, and analytics courses (CS 673/683/684/685, IS 601/650/657/661/663/676, MATH 661), so for a certificate named 'Foundations of Cybersecurity' the listed set dilutes the security focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten the certificate to a security-focused required core (CS 608, CS 645, CS 646/CS 696) plus a curated security-elective list, removing unrelated SE/IS courses from the credential's course inventory.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: deepestPrereqChain=2, missingCourseCount=0, and no dangling references. The security courses layer sensibly on networking foundations (CS 656 -> CS 646 -> CS 696).
dimensions.suggestion: Normalize prerequisite text where course-description prose has merged into the prereq field (notably CS 696 and IS 665) so requirements are unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the security competencies a graduate should demonstrate are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes mapped to a recognized framework (e.g., NICE/NIST work roles) covering cryptography, systems security, and network defense.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null, acceptable as a transparency note for a certificate; all courses are uniform 3.0 credits, so per-course credit is clear but the total (typically ~12) is undisclosed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and identify the required security core versus electives so the ~12-credit focus is visible.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and informative (CS 608 enumerates substitution/transposition ciphers; CS 645 lists access-control mechanisms), but requirementGroups=0 means no plan, no required/elective split, and no credit total, and the broad non-security course list can mislead a reader about scope.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a labeled plan showing the required security core and total credits, and scope the listed courses to those actually part of the certificate.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The security courses are relevant and current (applied cryptography in CS 608, systems privacy in CS 645, protocol and network security in CS 646/CS 696), but the set omits digital forensics/incident response, offensive security/penetration testing (CS 647 is absent here), cloud security, and security operations.
dimensions.suggestion: Add cloud/container security and a forensics or incident-response/SOC course to cover the defensive-operations skills employers now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and technical (CS 646 covers network attacks and protocol hardening; CS 696 details data-disclosure protection and message authenticity); none are thin, though many belong to non-security courses padding the list.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are internally consistent with no dangling references, but several fields mix description narrative into the prerequisite text (CS 696, IS 665, IS 601), reducing clarity about actual entry requirements.

missingTopics.rationale: No course in this certificate's list covers evidence acquisition, IR playbooks, or breach investigation, a foundational cybersecurity competency.
CS 684 addresses QA generally but there is no coverage of secure coding, OWASP-class vulnerabilities, or SAST/DAST tooling.
The certificate omits hands-on attacker tradecraft (the department's CS 647 Counter Hacking is not included), leaving the 'foundations' one-sided toward defense/theory.
No course addresses security policy, risk assessment, or regulatory frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001, GDPR) that every security professional must navigate.
CS 643 (cloud) is not paired with a security-focused treatment of IAM, misconfiguration, and workload protection in cloud environments.

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 645/CS 696 network-security material should be extended to identity-centric, perimeter-less designs now standard in enterprises.
Pairing CS 634 (data mining) with CS 645 would let students build anomaly/intrusion detectors, a fast-growing security practice.
CS 608's classical cryptography foundation is the natural entry point for NIST PQC algorithms migrating into production.
Container, CI/CD, and dependency threats are dominant attack surfaces the certificate does not yet address.
Building on CS 645, coverage of model attacks and differential privacy would target an active research frontier.

topSuggestions: Scope the certificate to a security-focused required core (CS 608, CS 645, CS 646/CS 696) plus curated security electives, removing unrelated SE/IS/UX courses from the listing.
Add a digital-forensics/incident-response course and an offensive-security option so the 'foundations' cover both attack and defense.
Add cloud/application-security coverage to reflect where breaches actually occur today.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes mapped to NICE/NIST work roles.
State the total credit requirement and the required-versus-elective split.

game-design-and-interactivity-essentials.name: Game Design and Interactivity Essentials

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 12-credit (choose-four-of-seven) pool is internally incoherent: three of the seven listed courses, Public and Private Development (ARCH 651), Land Remediation and Community Revitalization (ARCH 654), and Geographic Information Systems (ARCH 677), are real-estate and urban-planning courses that belong to the college's development certificates, not a game-design credential. Only History of Games (DD 631), Storyboarding (DD 622), Physical Computing/Interaction Design (DD 634), and Digital Audio (DD 624) are on-topic, and even those omit any actual game-design or engine studio.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the three ARCH real-estate/GIS courses and replace them with game-focused options (game design fundamentals, a game engine/programming course, a production studio) so the elective pool matches the certificate's title.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is light and largely clean (deepestPrereqChain 1, missingCourseCount 0), but DD 622's prerequisites (DD 601, DD 602) lie outside this certificate, creating a hidden external dependency for a student selecting the storyboarding path.
dimensions.suggestion: Make the certificate self-contained by adapting or noting the DD 601-602 prerequisite behind DD 622, and keep any added game courses within the credential's own sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes are stated, and the mismatched course pool makes it impossible to infer a coherent set of game-design competencies from the listing alone.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes naming the concrete game-design skills a completer gains (design a game system, prototype interactivity, produce game audio), which will also expose the off-topic courses as inconsistent with those outcomes.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The totals reconcile (creditReconcileDelta 0; statedTotalCredits 12 as a choose-four-of-seven set), so the credit arithmetic is transparent, but the balance is undermined because a student could satisfy a 'game design' certificate largely with real-estate and GIS courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten the pool so all selectable credits are genuinely game/interactivity content, preserving the clean 12-credit total while making it meaningful.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Individual course descriptions are specific, but the requirement group is titled as a question ('What are the Required Courses?'), the DD titles carry '(new course proposal forthcoming)' draft notes, and DD 634's title (Physical Computing/Interaction Design) does not match its description (about data visualization and website creation), which will confuse students choosing courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Finalize the catalog copy, remove proposal notes, and reconcile DD 634's title with its description so the interactivity content is clear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The on-topic courses touch real skills (interaction design/physical computing, game history, game audio, storyboarding), but the certificate has no game engine (Unity/Unreal), no game programming, and no systems/level-design or playtesting studio, so it does not prepare completers for actual game production. The inclusion of real-estate and GIS courses further dilutes any game-industry signal.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a game-engine and game-design-studio course to anchor the credential in current production practice and drop the unrelated development/GIS courses.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., History of Games as a guided exploration of traditional through electronic games; land-remediation via a triple-bottom-line lens), but DD 634's description describes data-visualization/website work rather than the physical-computing/interaction title, a real title-description mismatch.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: DD 622 states explicit code prerequisites (DD 601, DD 602) and the ARCH courses list none, but DD 634 uses the vague, typo-laden 'Basic background in in the use and application of digital media in design', which is not a measurable condition.

missingTopics.rationale: There is no Unity/Unreal or scripting (C#/C++) course, so completers cannot build a working game, the central competency the certificate name promises.

missingTopics.topic: Game design fundamentals (systems, mechanics, level design)
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has history and peripheral craft but no course on core game design, mechanics, systems, and level design, the discipline's foundation.
No integrative studio exists in which students design, build, and ship a playable prototype, which is how game skills are demonstrated to employers.
Player-centered testing and game UX are standard practice, but the certificate includes no playtesting or usability content specific to games.
Networked and multiplayer systems are core to modern interactive entertainment and are absent from the pool.

emergingTrends.rationale: Real-time engines dominate game and interactive production; the certificate needs an engine course to reflect how games are actually made.

emergingTrends.trend: Procedural content generation and game AI
emergingTrends.rationale: PCG and game AI are fast-growing techniques that a current interactivity credential should introduce, extending the physical-computing course.
Immersive and location-based games are a major growth area the certificate's interaction-design strength could build toward.
Ongoing operation, analytics, and monetization now define much of the industry and belong in a modern professional overview.
Accessibility is now a baseline expectation in game production and should be an explicit topic in a current game-design credential.

topSuggestions: Remove the three off-topic ARCH real-estate/GIS courses (ARCH 651, 654, 677) from the required pool.
Add a game-engine/game-programming course and a game-design-studio capstone so completers can build a playable game.
Fix the DD 634 title-versus-description mismatch and finalize catalog copy by removing draft/proposal notes.
Publish game-specific completion outcomes so the credential's competencies are explicit.
Make the certificate self-contained by resolving the external DD 601-602 prerequisite behind DD 622.

geotechnical-engineering-498.name: Geotechnical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The menu is a tightly focused geotechnical spine: soil properties (CE 641), shallow/deep foundations (CE 642, CE 643), engineering geology (CE 644), rock mechanics (CE 645), geosynthetics/soil improvement (CE 646), seepage (CE 648), and earthquake geotechnics (CE 742), with geo-environmental crossover (CE 647, ENE 660/662). It builds logically from characterization to design to specialty topics, appropriate for a 12-credit certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Flag CE 641 (Engineering Properties of Soils) as the recommended entry course so students sequence soil characterization before the foundation and specialty electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=1 is expected for an elective menu, but missingCourseCount=2 flags CE 341 and CE 341A (undergraduate soil mechanics) as dangling references cited by CE 646 and CE 647; they are explicitly pointed to the undergraduate catalog, so the gap is understood but remains unresolved in the graduate data.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the CE 341/CE 341A references by linking the undergraduate catalog entries or restating them as a single 'undergraduate soil mechanics' admission prerequisite.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, so the certificate does not state which geotechnical competencies a completer certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes covering soil characterization, foundation design, and geotechnical risk assessment.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null with no parsed plan, expected for a certificate; the 15 sampled courses are uniformly 3 credits, implying a clean ~12-credit credential, but the total and any required course are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate total (e.g., 12 credits / 4 courses) and whether any course is required versus a free elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are consistently specific and technical (e.g., CE 641 clay mineralogy and in-situ testing, CE 648 saturated/unsaturated flow with boundary-value solutions), and every sampled course has a populated excerpt; only a few titles are abbreviated, such as CE 646 'Geosynthetics & Soil Imp'.
dimensions.suggestion: Expand truncated titles such as CE 646 to their full form for readability.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Coverage is strong and practice-oriented, foundations, ground improvement (CE 646), engineering geology, rock mechanics, and seismic geotechnics (CE 742), all core to consulting, but it is more traditional than cutting-edge, with limited numerical-modeling and instrumentation content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a computational/finite-element geotechnics elective and a geotechnical instrumentation/monitoring course to reflect current design workflows.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions across the 15 sampled courses are specific, technical, and free of placeholders, clearly conveying scope (e.g., CE 643 computer-based shallow/deep foundation and retaining-structure design).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in clear narrative form (e.g., 'approved undergraduate course in soil mechanics within last five years'), but two dangling references (CE 341, CE 341A) counted in missingCourseCount reduce precision.

missingTopics.rationale: Design courses (CE 642, CE 643) reference computer applications but no course teaches modern FE tools (PLAXIS/FLAC) now standard in geotechnical practice.
NDT (CE 638) is present but there is no course on field instrumentation, sensors, and performance monitoring of geo-structures.
CE 648 covers saturated/unsaturated flow, but unsaturated soil behavior and its role in slope stability warrants dedicated coverage.
No dedicated slope-stability/embankment course appears despite its centrality to geotechnical risk.
CE 742 covers seismic wave propagation but liquefaction assessment and seismic soil-structure interaction could be strengthened.

emergingTrends.rationale: Site characterization and settlement prediction increasingly use ML; CE 641/CE 642 could integrate data-driven methods.
CE 646 geosynthetics/soil improvement is evolving toward low-carbon and climate-resilient techniques.
Offshore wind and solar are creating demand for specialized foundation design extending CE 642/CE 643.
CE 638 NDT and CE 644 engineering geology point toward digital, sensor-based subsurface characterization.
Seepage (CE 648) and seismic (CE 742) content connects to growing work on flood- and climate-induced ground hazards.

topSuggestions: Resolve the CE 341/CE 341A dangling prerequisites and state a single undergraduate soil-mechanics admission requirement.
Publish certificate learning outcomes and state total credits / course count.
Add a computational (finite-element) geotechnics elective to match current design practice.
Introduce geotechnical instrumentation/monitoring and slope-stability content.
Recommend CE 641 as the sequencing entry course and expand truncated titles.

hydrology-and-water-resources-engineerin.name: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 9-course menu has a solid water spine, hydrology (CE 621), applied hydrogeology (CE 618), open-channel flow (CE 620), and stormwater (ENE 672), but it dilutes with loosely related electives such as ENE 660 (Solid and Hazardous Waste) and a theory-heavy MATH 715 (Mathematical Fluid Dynamics I) whose PhD-level rigor is a sharp jump from the applied courses (deepestPrereqChain=0).
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten the menu around surface- and groundwater-resources courses and either justify or replace ENE 660 and MATH 715 with an applied watershed-modeling elective.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 with no dangling references, and prerequisites are stated as undergraduate expectations (fluid mechanics, chemistry); however deepestPrereqChain=0 means the graduate courses are entirely unsequenced, and MATH 715 lists no prerequisite despite being mathematically advanced.
dimensions.suggestion: Note recommended undergraduate preparation per topic and flag MATH 715's mathematical prerequisites so students self-select appropriately.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, so the certificate does not state the water-resources competencies it certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 certificate learning outcomes covering hydrologic analysis, hydraulic design, and water-quality assessment.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null with no parsed plan, expected for a certificate; the nine courses are uniformly 3 credits, supporting a ~12-credit / 4-course focus, but the total and any required course are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the certificate total (e.g., 12 credits / 4 courses) and any required-versus-elective distinction.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Core water courses have clear, specific descriptions (e.g., CE 621 flood/drought statistics and flood routing; CE 620 steady/unsteady open-channel flow), but two of nine courses, ENE 630 (Physical Processes of Env Syst) and ENE 661 (Environmental Microbiology), have empty descriptions, a notable 22% gap on a small menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the ENE 630 and ENE 661 descriptions so every option on this compact list is informative.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The fundamentals, hydrology, hydrogeology, open-channel flow, and stormwater, are current and employable, but the menu lacks the computational and climate dimensions that define contemporary water-resources practice (watershed/hydraulic modeling software, GIS, climate-driven flood risk).
dimensions.suggestion: Add an applied hydrologic/hydraulic modeling course (HEC-HMS/HEC-RAS or SWMM) and a climate-and-water elective.

descriptionQuality.justification: Present descriptions are specific and well written (CE 618 hydrogeology well design/pumping tests; CE 620 open-channel steady/unsteady flow), but two empty excerpts on a nine-course list pull the aggregate to adequate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly as undergraduate expectations with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), though several courses (ENE 630, ENE 672, MATH 715) list none, leaving preparation implicit.

missingTopics.rationale: CE 621 hydrology and CE 620 open-channel flow are taught fundamentally, but no course delivers HEC-HMS/HEC-RAS or SWMM modeling that defines professional practice.

missingTopics.topic: GIS and remote sensing for water resources
missingTopics.rationale: Watershed delineation and spatial hydrology now rely on GIS/remote sensing, absent from the menu.
No course addresses nonstationarity, changing precipitation extremes, or climate-adjusted flood frequency, central to modern water planning.
There is no reservoir-operations or water-allocation systems course to complement the physical-process electives.
ENE 672 stormwater touches BMPs but LID/green-infrastructure design deserves fuller treatment.

emergingTrends.rationale: Streamflow forecasting and rainfall-runoff modeling increasingly use machine learning, extending CE 621's statistical framing.
Stormwater (ENE 672) and hydrology (CE 621) practice is reorienting around climate-driven flood risk.
CE 618 hydrogeology and CE 621 hydrology point toward coupled surface-groundwater modeling now standard in water planning.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and real-time water monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor networks and real-time hydraulic modeling are transforming open-channel and stormwater operations.
Water chemistry (ENE 663) and resource pressures are driving reuse and sustainable-supply engineering the certificate could address.

topSuggestions: Add an applied hydrologic/hydraulic modeling course (HEC-HMS/HEC-RAS or SWMM) to match professional practice.
Tighten the menu around water resources and reconsider off-theme electives (ENE 660, MATH 715).
Publish learning outcomes and state total credits / course count.
Populate the empty ENE 630 and ENE 661 descriptions on this compact list.
Introduce GIS/remote-sensing and climate-and-water electives to modernize coverage.

information-security-243.name: Information Security

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The certificate forms a focused, coherent security cluster: cryptography (CS 608), network management and security (CS 696), computer security auditing (IS 681), transaction mining and fraud detection (IS 687), and wireless security (IT 620), with foundational IS courses (IS 513, IS 531, IS 601) providing entry. Visible depth-2 chains (CS 656 to CS 696; IS 665 to IS 687) show real progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate CS 608 (cryptography) and IS 681 (security auditing) as a required core to anchor the credential and signal a coherent security spine.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This is the cleanest structure in the set: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references) and deepestPrereqChain is 2, with sensible chains (CS 652/656/ECE 637/683 into CS 696; IS 665 into IS 687). The prerequisites resolve within the catalog and are appropriately shallow for a certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the assumed foundations explicitly (networking for CS 696, data mining IS 665 for IS 687) so entrants know the recommended on-ramp.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, a real gap for a security credential where prospective students (auditors, analysts) need to know the competencies certified.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as applying cryptographic methods, auditing and securing systems, and detecting transaction fraud.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null (expected for a certificate), and the pool is appropriately focused at nine referenced courses, but no total credits or required-course count is stated, so the credential's size is not transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits (e.g., 12) and the number of required versus elective courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The course list is clean with no dangling references and informative descriptions, but there is no program overview or learning-outcome statement and the courses are a flat list without a stated audience or focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program overview describing the security focus and target audience (security analysts, auditors, compliance professionals).

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Coverage of cryptography (CS 608), network security (CS 696), security auditing (IS 681), and forensic/fraud analytics (IS 687) reflects enduring, currently relevant security fundamentals, though the mix leans toward audit/compliance; cloud security, application/DevSecOps security, and SOC/threat-intelligence operations are the notable modern gaps.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a cloud-security and application-security (DevSecOps) elective and some security-operations/threat-intelligence content to balance the audit-heavy emphasis.
