dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page has parsed requirement groups (Core 12 cr / Elective 18 cr) with legible course titles and present descriptions, but lacks stated learning outcomes and any track labeling on the large elective list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add learning outcomes and concentration-track headings to the elective section for scannability.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective pool is highly current: CS 643 Cloud Computing, CS/DS 677 Deep Learning, DS 680 NLP, CS/DS 644 Big Data, CS 750 High Performance Computing, a full security suite (CS 645/646/647/708/755) and CS 756 mobile/sensor networks reflect contemporary practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit MLOps/data-engineering and generative-AI/LLM electives to cover the production-ML and foundation-model gaps.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., CS 643 naming AWS/Google platforms, CS 647 'learning by doing' offensive/defensive labs, CS 703 continuous optimization), with no thin entries observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: With missingCourseCount=0 and explicit chains (CS 611 requires CS 610/435 or doctoral status; CS 668 requires CS 610 and CS 650), prerequisites are largely unambiguous; only phrases like 'completion of bridge requirements' remain vague.

missingTopics.rationale: CS/DS 675/677 teach modeling but no course covers deployment, serving, monitoring or lifecycle of ML systems.
CS/DS 644 Big Data exists, but modern batch/stream pipeline tooling (Spark/Kafka) and analytics engineering are not represented.
Theory is strong (CS 611 complexity, CS 665/667 algorithms) yet software correctness/verification is absent from the SE electives.
An increasingly standard graduate CS topic that does not appear among the 75 referenced courses.
The security suite is deep but differential privacy and federated learning are not covered alongside CS 708.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI / LLMs and agentic systems
emergingTrends.rationale: DS 680 NLP and CS/DS 677 Deep Learning provide a base to add a dedicated foundation-models/LLM course.
Complements the strong ML electives with the engineering and governance skills employers now expect.
CS 643 Cloud Computing could extend to containers/Kubernetes and serverless architectures.
Bridges the security track (CS 708) and ML track with federated learning and differential privacy.
Connects the languages (CS 635) and SE electives to LLM-assisted software development.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes on the catalog page.
Organize the ~70 electives into named concentration tracks (AI/ML, Security, Systems, Data, SE).
Add a generative-AI/LLM course and an MLOps/data-engineering elective to close production-ML gaps.
Make a capstone project or thesis (CS 700B/701B) explicitly required rather than optional within electives.
Standardize 'bridge requirements' into explicit prerequisite course lists.

m-s-in-computing-and-business-212.name: M.S. in Computing and Business

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 30-credit degree blends a CS foundation (CS 505/506/610, plus CS 631/634/652/656) with a business menu weighted toward finance (FIN 600->624/626/634/641/650) and management (MGMT 630/635/650, MRKT 620, HRM 601); however requirementGroups=0 means no parsed core/elective split, and the two halves connect only through the YWCC 691 capstone.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a required computing core plus a business core and an integrative analytics course so the computing and business halves reinforce each other rather than sitting side by side.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=2 with sensible finance ladders (FIN 600 as gateway to five 600-level electives), but missingCourseCount=2 flags dangling undergraduate references CS 280 and CS 332 that resolve to nothing in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the CS 280 and CS 332 dangling references by mapping them to current course codes or removing them from prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data for this interdisciplinary degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes spanning both domains (e.g., build data-driven business solutions, evaluate technology investments).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=30 is declared but creditReconcileDelta=null and requirementGroups=0 leave the computing-versus-business credit split invisible, which is a transparency concern for a dual-discipline program.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study showing how the 30 credits divide between computing, business and the capstone (YWCC 691).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The 27 sample courses carry present, readable descriptions, but the page has no requirement groups, no outcomes, and at least one parsing artifact (IS 634 prerequisite text merged into the description).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study table and clean the merged prerequisite/description text.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: There is decent analytics currency (MGMT 635 Data Mining and Analysis, MGMT 630 Decision Analysis, CS 634 Data Mining, CS 652 Cognitive Cloud Networking, MIS 625 e-commerce), but no modern BI/visualization, applied ML-for-business, or fintech course connects the heavy finance electives to computing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a business-analytics/BI course and a fintech elective that ties the FIN sequence to computational methods.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific across both domains (e.g., FIN 641 option valuation and forward/futures pricing, MGMT 635 framing research questions on large databases, CS 652 cloud application delivery), with no placeholder text observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Finance prerequisites are unambiguous (FIN 600 gates the FIN electives), but missingCourseCount=2 (CS 280, CS 332) introduces dangling references and one IS 634 entry mixes prerequisite text into its description.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 634 and MGMT 635 cover data mining but no course delivers dashboards/BI or data storytelling for decision-makers.
Predictive analytics beyond classical data mining is absent despite the CS/business overlap.
No course bridges technical delivery and business strategy through product/PM practice.
Six FIN electives exist but none connect derivatives/portfolio theory (FIN 641/650) to computing or algorithmic methods.
MGMT 650 Knowledge Management and MIS 625 e-commerce touch strategy, but IT governance/architecture is missing.

emergingTrends.rationale: LLM copilots and workflow automation are reshaping enterprise operations the program's management courses should address.
Extends MGMT 630/635 toward the modern data stack that businesses now build on.
Would give the deep FIN elective set (FIN 626/641/650) a computational, forward-looking dimension.
CS 652 Cognitive Cloud Networking pairs naturally with platform/SaaS business-model analysis.
Complements HRM 601 and MGMT courses as firms adopt AI under regulatory scrutiny.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes for the dual-discipline degree.
Structure the program into a computing core, a business core and an integrative analytics/capstone requirement.
Resolve the dangling CS 280 and CS 332 prerequisite references.
Add ML-for-business / BI and a fintech elective to modernize and connect the two halves.
Publish a plan of study clarifying the computing-versus-business credit balance.

m-s-in-critical-infrastructure-systems-4.name: M.S. in Critical Infrastructure Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 37 referenced courses are genuinely organized around infrastructure systems: domain anchors like CE 671 (Performance and Risk Analysis of Infrastructure Systems), CE 672 (Security Management of Critical Infrastructure), CE 615 (Infrastructure Remediation) and MIP 675 (Infrastructure Planning) are supported by enabling-technology electives in geospatial (CE 602 GIS), data (CS 631/632, MGMT 635), networking/power (ECE 610, ECE 637, ECE 683), systems/reliability (IE 605, IE 621, IE 651), emergency response (IS 613/614), and transportation (TRAN 755 ITS). It is deliberately interdisciplinary and coheres around critical-infrastructure protection, though the breadth risks diffuseness without an anchoring core.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a required core (e.g., CE 671 risk analysis + CE 672 security + one systems/analytics course) so the wide interdisciplinary elective menu is anchored to a shared foundation.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1 and missingCourseCount is just 1 (CE 701) out of 37 referenced courses, an excellent ratio; sensible chains appear (CS 631 -> CS 632, CE 610 -> CE 611/616, ENE 663 -> ENE 662, TRAN 752 -> TRAN 755). The graph is shallow but clean and appropriate for a course-based interdisciplinary M.S.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the single dangling CE 701 reference and consider one deeper analytics prerequisite chain so the systems electives build on a shared methods base.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, which is a real finding for a distinctive interdisciplinary degree whose value depends on articulating cross-domain competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the risk-assessment, security, and systems-analysis capabilities that unify the program's diverse electives.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan is parsed (requirementGroups=0), so for a full M.S. degree neither the total nor the core/elective split is transparent; the very large 37-course elective pool makes an explicit structure especially important.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and specify how many are required core versus electives so students can navigate the broad menu.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are rich and informative across the 37 courses (e.g., CE 672 on vulnerability assessment and network modeling; ECE 683 on cloud/IoT security), but the absence of a plan of study, requirement groups, and a credit total leaves the page as an impressive inventory rather than a guided program.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study layout with labeled requirement groups and a credit summary to convert the inventory into a navigable curriculum.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The currency is exemplary: CE 672 (critical-infrastructure security), ECE 683 (Cloud and IoT Networking and Security), ECE 642 (communications through 5G and beyond), CE 671 (infrastructure risk/asset management), TRAN 755 (Intelligent Transportation Systems), and MGMT 635 (Data Mining) map directly onto today's infrastructure-protection, cyber-physical, and analytics priorities.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit cyber-physical / operational-technology (OT) security and infrastructure-resilience-to-climate offering to close the one gap between IT security and physical-asset resilience.

descriptionQuality.justification: Across 37 courses the description excerpts are consistently substantive and specific (e.g., CE 671 on integrated asset management, ECE 683 on cloud/IoT security fundamentals, IS 614 on command-and-control models), indicating strong, non-placeholder descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and are internally consistent, with sensible chains (CS 631 -> CS 632, TRAN 752 -> TRAN 755) and only a single dangling reference (CE 701, missingCourseCount=1) across a large course set.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has strong IT-network security (ECE 683, ECE 637) and physical-security (CE 672) coverage but no course explicitly bridging them at the operational-technology/SCADA layer.
Risk and asset management (CE 671) is covered, but climate-driven resilience and recovery of lifeline systems is not explicitly represented.
With power (ECE 610), transport (TRAN), and networking electives present, an explicit cross-sector interdependency/cascading-failure course is a natural missing capstone.
EM 771 covers operations cost, but public-infrastructure financing/PPP models, central to infrastructure decision-making, are underrepresented.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for infrastructure monitoring and anomaly detection
emergingTrends.rationale: The data electives (CS 782 pattern recognition, MGMT 635 data mining) position the program to formalize ML-based sensing and predictive maintenance of critical assets.

emergingTrends.trend: Smart grids and distributed energy resilience
emergingTrends.rationale: ECE 610 power analysis is a foundation the program can extend toward smart-grid resilience, a top national-infrastructure priority.
ECE 683 and ECE 642 already position the degree at the IoT/5G edge that is reshaping infrastructure sensing and control.
GIS (CE 602) and simulation (IE 651) provide the ingredients to build a digital-twin strand for asset and risk management.

topSuggestions: State the total credits and a required-core versus elective structure to anchor the 37-course interdisciplinary menu.
Define an anchoring required core (CE 671 risk + CE 672 security + a systems/analytics course) so breadth resolves into a shared foundation.
Publish program learning outcomes articulating the cross-domain risk, security, and systems competencies.
Add a cyber-physical/OT-security and infrastructure-resilience offering to bridge IT security and physical-asset resilience.
Resolve the single dangling CE 701 reference and add a plan-of-study layout with labeled requirement groups.

m-s-in-cyber-security-and-privacy-213.name: M.S. in Cyber Security and Privacy

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A tightly themed 15-credit five-course core (CS 608 Cryptography, CS 645 Security & Privacy, CS 646 Network Protocols Security, CS 647 Counter Hacking, CS 656 Internet Protocols) anchors a coherent security elective set (forensics IS 680/681/682, cloud/IoT security ECE 683, wireless CS 755, law/policy MGMT 688/691); deepestPrereqChain=3 (CS 656->646->647; ->708) gives a genuine foundations-to-offense progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required privacy-specific course and sequence the offensive/defensive electives into a labeled progression, since 'Privacy' in the title currently rests almost entirely on CS 708.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: metrics show deepestPrereqChain=3, missingCourseCount=0 and no dangling references across 40 referenced courses, with real ladders such as CS 656->CS 646->CS 647 and CS 608/645/696->CS 708.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten vague gates like 'completion of bridge requirements' (CS 633) into explicit prerequisite courses.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the provided data.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes covering both security engineering and privacy competencies (e.g., threat modeling, applied cryptography, privacy-by-design).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=30 with a parsed 15-credit core implies a balanced 15/15 core-elective split, but summedTermCredits=15 and creditReconcileDelta=-15 show the elective half (and any required capstone/thesis) is not represented in the plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the 15 elective credits and the capstone/thesis requirement so the plan reconciles to the full 30 credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page parses and labels the core group and course descriptions are present, but the group name carries raw bullet text ('• Five Core Courses required for all students') and titles retain footnote markers ('&1', '&2', '&3'), and no outcomes are stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the footnote/bullet markers, format the requirement-group heading, and add learning outcomes.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly current: CS 647 Counter Hacking, CS 708 Advanced Data Security and Privacy, CS 755 wireless security, ECE 683 Cloud and IoT Networking and Security, IS 682 forensic auditing and MGMT 688/691 cyber law/ethics reflect contemporary security practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a privacy-engineering course (differential privacy/PETs) and a cloud-security/DevSecOps elective to cover the two most notable modern gaps.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are detailed and practice-oriented (e.g., CS 647 hands-on offensive/defensive labs, CS 708 security/privacy of large-scale data, IS 682 computer-forensics evidence collection), with no thin entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: missingCourseCount=0 and prerequisites are mostly explicit (CS 708 requires CS 608/645/696; CS 647 requires CS 645/646/696/ECE 638), though some entries (IS 665) fold prerequisite prose into the description and a few gate only on 'bridge requirements'.

missingTopics.rationale: 'Privacy' is in the degree title yet only CS 708 addresses it; there is no dedicated course on differential privacy, PETs or GDPR/CCPA compliance.
CS 643 Cloud Computing and ECE 683 IoT security exist but no course covers secure cloud architecture, containers or CI/CD security.
CS 684 Testing/QA is present but there is no dedicated appsec course covering SAST/DAST and secure coding.

missingTopics.topic: Security operations, threat intel & incident response
missingTopics.rationale: Forensics (IS 682) is covered, but SOC/blue-team operations, SIEM and incident response are absent.
CS 634 Data Mining exists but no course addresses attacks on and defenses for ML systems.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for security and adversarial ML
emergingTrends.rationale: Pairs the data-mining elective (CS 634) with LLM-assisted attack/defense, a fast-moving security frontier.
Federated learning and homomorphic encryption directly extend the 'Privacy' mandate barely covered by CS 708.
Builds on ECE 683 Cloud/IoT security toward the zero-trust architectures enterprises are adopting.
CS 608 Cryptography and Security should incorporate NIST post-quantum standards as classical schemes are deprecated.
ECE 683 IoT networking is a natural anchor for expanding into supply-chain and operational-technology threats.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes covering both security and privacy competencies.
Add a dedicated privacy-engineering course so the curriculum matches the 'and Privacy' in the degree title.
Add cloud-security/DevSecOps and adversarial-ML/AI-security electives.
Publish the full 30-credit plan (elective 15 credits plus capstone/thesis) so credits reconcile.
Modernize CS 608 Cryptography with post-quantum cryptography content.

m-s-in-data-science-computational-track-.name: M.S. in Data Science - Computational Track

dimensions.justification: No curriculum can be assessed: requirementGroups=[], referencedCourses=0, and sampleCourses is empty, so the page exposes no courses, sequence, or core/elective structure at all. Only the 39-credit total is stated. There is no evidence of coherence or progression to evaluate.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the actual plan of study (core, computational electives, capstone) so the track's course sequence is visible and can be assessed.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is empty (deepestPrereqChain=0, missingCourseCount=0 only because no courses are referenced). The clean metrics are vacuous rather than healthy: with zero courses there is no scaffolding, so nothing can be judged as well- or ill-constructed.
dimensions.suggestion: List the track's courses with their prerequisites so a real prerequisite structure exists on the page.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or even a program description are present in the data, leaving nothing for a prospective student to evaluate.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish measurable learning outcomes for the computational track (e.g., scalable algorithm design, distributed data processing, statistical modeling).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total of 39 credits reconciles trivially (summedTermCredits=39, creditReconcileDelta=0.0) only because there are no parsed terms to contradict it. No core/elective/capstone credit split is visible, so balance and transparency cannot be confirmed despite the clean delta.
dimensions.suggestion: Break the 39 credits into labeled core, computational-elective, and capstone/thesis allocations so the distribution is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity

dimensions.suggestion: Author a complete catalog page with an overview paragraph and a structured plan-of-study table before anything else.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Currency cannot be evaluated because no courses are listed; a 'computational track' implies systems/HPC/data-engineering emphasis, but none of that is demonstrated in the provided data.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the page with the track's actual computational courses (e.g., parallel computing, big data systems, ML at scale) so its industry currency can be judged.

descriptionQuality.justification: No course data is present (sampleCourses is empty), so description quality cannot be assessed; the absence itself is the finding for a catalog page that should list its courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: No courses and therefore no prerequisites are present in the data (referencedCourses=0), so prerequisite clarity cannot be evaluated; the page exposes no prerequisite information at all.

missingTopics.rationale: A 'computational track' should foreground parallel/distributed processing (e.g., DS 642 Applications of Parallel Computing, CS 735 High Performance Data Analytics), none of which appear on the page.
No coursework on data ingestion, storage, or ETL/streaming is visible, yet it is central to a computational data-science degree.
Core ML (e.g., DS 675 Machine Learning, DS 677 Deep Learning) is absent from the listed content, an essential expectation for the degree.
No statistics/inference course is shown, leaving the quantitative backbone of the track undocumented.

emergingTrends.trend: ML systems and MLOps at scale

A computational specialization increasingly requires accelerator literacy that the current empty page cannot demonstrate.

The limiter is that, with no course list parsed, the distribution across required core, track electives, and capstone is not visible.

dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description, a course list, and a term-by-term plan of study to accompany the credit total.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Currency cannot be assessed because no courses were parsed; for a high-demand field like data science the absence of any visible machine-learning, data-engineering, or programming content is a real limitation of the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the actual course list so modern data-science content (machine learning, data engineering, cloud/big-data tools) is visible and assessable.

descriptionQuality.justification: No courses were parsed (referencedCourses 0, empty sampleCourses), so there are no course descriptions to assess; the absence is a serious catalog-completeness problem for a 39-credit degree.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: With zero parsed courses there are no prerequisites to evaluate; missingCourseCount 0 reflects that nothing was captured rather than well-formed prerequisites.

missingTopics.rationale: Predictive modeling is the core of any data-science M.S.; no such course is visible in the parsed data for this statistics track.

Fairness, interpretability, and privacy-preserving methods are growing regulatory and industry priorities.

m-s-in-digital-design-165.name: M.S. in Digital Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 17 courses read as a coherent coursework M.S.: the same foundations (Graphic Design/Color, 3D Modelling, Video/Animation) and history survey feed the Extended Reality Design Studio (DD 650) and an Independent Project Studio (DD 657) capstone, with a broad elective pool across animation, game, UI/UX, digital audio, and physical computing plus cross-listed IS courses. It is deliberately shallower than the MFA (it omits the two-course track studios 651-656), so progression is sound but less deep, and no plan groups were parsed to show elective rules.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study clarifying which courses are core versus elective and how many elective credits are required beyond DD 650 and DD 657.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is clean (missingCourseCount 0) with a sensible spine (601/602/603 to 650 to 657) at deepestPrereqChain 2, appropriate for a coursework M.S. However most electives are flat (depth 0) and several use the vague prose prerequisite 'Basic background in in the use and application of digital media in design,' so the structure is shallow and partly informal.
dimensions.suggestion: Encode concrete prerequisites (e.g., DD 601/602) on the elective courses and fix the doubled 'in in' typo so the graph reflects real readiness expectations.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data, leaving the intended competencies of the degree unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 M.S. learning outcomes spanning digital-design craft, interaction/UX methods, and an independent project deliverable.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with no parsed plan, so the total (an M.S. is typically ~30 credits) and the balance between 3-credit electives and the 6-credit DD 650/DD 657 studios are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State total credits and the required core/elective/studio split so students can see how the 3- and 6-credit courses combine.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are specific and consistent across the 17 courses and titles are clear, but the page lacks a plan of study, total credits, and labeled terms, so core-versus-elective status must be inferred.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table and credit summary distinguishing required core from the elective pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The degree is strongly current: an XR studio, UI/UX and physical-computing/interaction electives, and cross-listed IS courses in User Experience Design and lean-startup Customer Discovery tie creative skills to product practice, a marketable combination for a professionally oriented M.S.
dimensions.suggestion: Name the specific tools/engines used and add explicit AI-assisted design and data/interaction-analytics content to reinforce currency.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative, e.g., DD 650's framing of XR across UI/UX, gaming, and animation and IS 661's articulation of UX as product design, with no placeholder entries among the sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Core-spine prerequisites (DD 650 requires 601/602/603; DD 657 requires 650) are stated clearly with no dangling references, but multiple electives rely on the vague, typo-laden 'Basic background in in the use...' prose prerequisite, reducing overall clarity and consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: A coursework M.S. benefits from an explicit research/UX-methods course; only the IS courses touch discovery, and no formal methods course is visible.

missingTopics.topic: Real-time game engines and interactive prototyping
missingTopics.rationale: With the XR studio and UI/UX focus, an explicit engine/prototyping course (Unity/Unreal, Figma prototyping) would ground the interactive work students are expected to produce.
No course addresses generative-AI image/video/3D tools now embedded in digital-design workflows.
Physical Computing touches data communication, but there is no dedicated data-visualization or usage-analytics course to inform evidence-based design.
Beyond the independent project studio, there is no explicit portfolio/professional-practice course to package graduates for the job market.

emergingTrends.rationale: Generative tools are reshaping production and ideation; the foundations and XR studio should incorporate critical, hands-on use.
The DD 650 XR studio should track current headset/spatial-computing ecosystems as they enter mainstream product design.
UI/UX and IS courses should foreground accessibility standards, now a baseline expectation for digital products.
Designing interfaces for AI-driven products is an emerging specialty the UX-oriented electives could address.
Customer Discovery already leans evidence-based; extending to interaction analytics would align the degree with data-driven product practice.

topSuggestions: State total credits and a plan of study distinguishing required core from the elective pool, replacing the null statedTotalCredits.
Publish M.S. program learning outcomes for digital-design craft, UX methods, and the independent project.
Encode concrete prerequisites on electives and fix the 'in in' typo to firm up the prerequisite graph.
Add a design/UX research-methods course and an explicit real-time engine/prototyping course.
Integrate generative-AI and interaction-analytics content into the XR studio and UX electives to stay current.

m-s-in-electrical-engineering-519.name: M.S. in Electrical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 45 referenced courses span the classic EE breadth areas - systems and control (ECE 601/605/660/661/664), power (ECE 610-619), communications and DSP (ECE 640/642/644/645/673), devices and VLSI (ECE 657/658/758), and applied ML (ECE 754) - which is coherent for a breadth M.S., but with requirementGroups 0 there is no visible required core or concentration and progression appears only in short chains (ECE 601 to ECE 660 to ECE 661; ECE 658 to ECE 758).
dimensions.suggestion: Define concentration tracks (e.g., power/energy, communications/signals, devices/VLSI, controls) with a required-core plus track-elective rule instead of a single undifferentiated list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 2, appropriate for a graduate electives program, but missingCourseCount is 6 (ECE 232, 321, 333, 361, 362, 372) - the highest among the ECE M.S. programs - all undergraduate ECE codes referenced as background, and ECE 613's prerequisite field again contains description text rather than a clean prerequisite.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate the six ECE 2xx/3xx references as undergraduate-preparation expectations and clean the ECE 613 prerequisite string.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, a real omission for a breadth EE degree where students choose among very different subfields.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program outcomes and, ideally, per-track outcomes so students in power vs. communications vs. devices see distinct competency targets.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable (requirementGroups 0), so the standard ~30-credit load and the balance between coursework and the ECE 700B project / ECE 701B thesis are not shown.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the required breadth-vs-depth and project/thesis distribution.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and specific for most of the 34 sampled courses, but ECE 613 and ECE 641 are blank and there is no plan of study or labeled sequencing, so structure must be inferred.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study/track table and complete the missing ECE 613 and ECE 641 descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Coverage is current across subfields: renewable/photovoltaic energy (ECE 618), smart-grid and smart-city sensing (ECE 619), power electronics (ECE 616), 5G communications (ECE 642/644/645), and statistical machine learning (ECE 754), reflecting where EE practice is moving.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a wide-bandgap (SiC/GaN) power-electronics module and an ML-for-signal-processing elective to keep the strongest tracks at the frontier.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are generally informative and subfield-specific (e.g., ECE 616 on thyristor and inverter drives, ECE 673 on random processes), but they are truncated excerpts and ECE 613/641 are empty placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite clarity is the weakest signal here: six undergraduate references (ECE 232/321/333/361/362/372) read as dangling, ECE 613's prerequisite string is contaminated with description prose, and while many electives legitimately carry null prerequisites the overall set is noisy.

missingTopics.rationale: The sampled EE list has no RF/microwave or antenna-design course, a core EE competency for wireless and radar systems despite heavy 5G emphasis in ECE 642/644/645.
VLSI here (ECE 658/758) is digital-focused; there is no analog/mixed-signal or RFIC design course, a persistent industry hiring need.
ECE 616 covers classical power electronics but lacks modern EV-drivetrain, wide-bandgap-device, and grid-tie-inverter content driving the electrification market.
ECE 754 teaches statistical ML generically; a course applying deep learning to signals/communications/imaging would connect it to the strong DSP/comms courses (ECE 640/673).
Beyond ECE 626 optoelectronics, there is no integrated-photonics or fiber-optic-systems course, a growing area for data-center and sensing applications.

emergingTrends.rationale: These devices are transforming power electronics and EV/renewable converters; ECE 616 and ECE 657 should incorporate wide-bandgap device and converter design.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML embedded in communications and signal processing
emergingTrends.rationale: Learned receivers, spectrum sensing, and physical-layer ML are reshaping comms; ECE 642/644 and ECE 754 could be linked into an ML-for-EE thread.
The energy transition is making inverter-dominated grids central; ECE 610-619 should address grid-forming inverters and DER integration.
Wireless research is moving past 5G; the ECE 644/645 sequence should preview mmWave/THz propagation and massive-MIMO front ends.
A research EE M.S. should expose students to quantum devices/sensing as an emerging device-physics frontier building on ECE 657.

topSuggestions: Introduce named concentration tracks with a required core to give the broad ECE elective list a coherent structure.
Publish program (and per-track) learning outcomes and the total credit requirement, closing the null statedTotalCredits/outcomes gaps.
Add RF/microwave and analog/mixed-signal IC design courses to fill two conspicuous EE competency gaps.
Modernize power electronics (ECE 616) with wide-bandgap devices and electrification/EV applications.
Improve prerequisite hygiene: annotate the six undergraduate ECE references and clean ECE 613's contaminated prerequisite string.

m-s-in-engineering-management-551.name: M.S. in Engineering Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 28 referenced courses cover the engineering-management body of knowledge coherently: management science (EM 602), a project-management sequence (EM 636 to EM 637), quality engineering (IE 672, IE 673, EM 674), logistics and supply chain (EM 640, IE 659, EM 641), cost and economics (IE 618, EM 691), organizational behavior (HRM 601), and manufacturing systems (MNE 601 to 602 to 654). Depth-2 chains such as EM 641 (requiring EM 602, EM 640, and EM 674) show genuine sequencing, though with requirementGroups=0 the core-versus-elective split is not parsed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study distinguishing required core courses from the large elective pool so the intended 30-credit path through this broad catalog is legible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This program has the healthiest graph of the group: deepestPrereqChain is 2, missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and real prerequisites appear (EM 636 to EM 637; MNE 601 to MNE 602/654; EM 641 requiring EM 602/640/674). That depth is sensible for a 30-credit master's without over-constraining a working-professional audience.
dimensions.suggestion: Verify the prose prerequisites that point to undergraduate courses (e.g., IE 621's 'IE 331, IE 466') resolve cleanly for applicants entering from other B.S. backgrounds.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see the leadership, decision-analysis, and operations competencies the degree targets.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 program outcomes (e.g., apply management-science methods to engineering decisions, lead technical projects, deploy quality systems) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30, a transparent and standard master's total, but creditReconcileDelta is null and requirementGroups=0, so the internal split among required core, breadth, and electives is not shown - with 28 nearly-all-elective courses visible, the balance between mandated and chosen credits is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: State how the 30 credits divide (e.g., a required core of N credits plus electives) so students can see the mandated-versus-elective balance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The 28 courses carry detailed, informative descriptions, but the page has no parsed plan of study, no labeled core/elective grouping, and no stated outcomes, so a rich course list is presented without the structure that would let a student assemble a coherent program.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into a labeled plan of study (core, concentration tracks, electives) to convert the flat list into a navigable structure.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Coverage of quality (IE 672/673, EM 674), project management (EM 636/637), supply chain (IE 659, EM 640), and cost economics (IE 618, EM 691) reflects durable EM practice, but several emphases read dated - IE 661 'Man-Machine Systems' and the older MIS 645/EM 655 information-systems framing - and there is no dedicated data-analytics, Agile, or sustainability course.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a modern business-analytics elective and refresh the information-systems and human-factors courses to reflect current data-driven and Agile practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions across the 28 courses are consistently detailed and specific - EM 602 lists linear programming, transportation/assignment, Markov chains, and queueing; IE 659 enumerates supply-chain design and control; MNE 601 names robotics, PLCs, and lean - with no thin or placeholder entries evident.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in prose and are internally consistent with missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references); the main softness is imprecise conditions such as 'undergraduate calculus and probability and statistics' (EM 602) and 'computer programming experience' (EM 655) rather than named courses.

missingTopics.rationale: The information-systems coverage (MIS 645, EM 655) predates the analytics era; no modern data-analytics or business-intelligence course supports data-driven engineering management.
The project sequence EM 636 to EM 637 is classical waterfall/earned-value; Agile, Scrum, and hybrid delivery now dominate technical project work and are absent.
No course addresses sustainability or circular-economy operations, an expected competency for contemporary engineering managers.
IE 618 and EM 691 cover cost estimating, but modern financial-decision and risk-analysis methods for capital projects are underweighted.
MNE 601/602 cover computerized/flexible manufacturing, but IoT, digital-thread, and Industry 4.0 management integration is not explicit.

emergingTrends.rationale: Extending management science (EM 602) and simulation (IE 621/IE 651) with machine-learning decision tools reflects where analytical EM practice is heading.
MNE 601/602's automation content positions the program to teach IoT-enabled, data-connected operations management now central to manufacturing firms.
Modernizing EM 636/637 toward Agile and hybrid delivery aligns the project strand with prevailing technical-project practice.
IE 659 and EM 640 can evolve toward digital supply networks and post-disruption resilience, a top priority for operations leaders.
Embedding ESG and life-cycle thinking into the quality and logistics courses matches rising regulatory and investor expectations.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study that separates required core from the large elective pool so the 30-credit path is legible.
Publish 4-5 program-level learning outcomes describing the management, decision-analysis, and quality competencies the degree develops.
Add a modern business-analytics elective and modernize the project sequence (EM 636/637) toward Agile/hybrid delivery.
State how the 30 credits divide between mandated core and electives to fix the opaque credit balance.
Refresh dated emphases (IE 661 man-machine systems, MIS 645/EM 655 information systems) and add sustainability/ESG content.

m-s-in-engineering-science-571.name: M.S. in Engineering Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The page exposes no curriculum: referencedCourses=0, requirementGroups=0, and an empty sampleCourses list, so no course sequence or core/elective structure can be evaluated. As a flexible interdisciplinary M.S. this breadth is expected, but with nothing shown, coherence and progression cannot be demonstrated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study: a small interdisciplinary core plus curated elective clusters (with a thesis or project option) so students can see how a coherent 30-credit path is assembled.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: referencedCourses=0 and deepestPrereqChain=0 mean there is no prerequisite graph to assess; missingCourseCount=0 is trivially true with no listed courses and is not a strength.
dimensions.suggestion: List representative required and elective graduate courses with their prerequisites so a minimal, checkable prerequisite structure exists on the page.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes, objectives, or program description are present in the data, leaving applicants with no stated competencies for this interdisciplinary master's.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish M.S.-level outcomes (e.g., interdisciplinary problem formulation, advanced analysis/modeling, and a capstone/thesis deliverable).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=30 is present and matches the standard master's expectation, giving credit transparency; however summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null and no distribution across core/electives/thesis is shown, so balance within the 30 credits cannot be verified.
dimensions.suggestion: Break the 30 credits into core, elective, and thesis/project components so the internal distribution is transparent, not just the total.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Apart from a 30-credit total the page is empty: no description, no courses, and no requirement groups, so it reads as a stub rather than a catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Author a program description explaining the interdisciplinary intent, participating departments, and how a student customizes the 30-credit path.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: With no courses or description present, currency relative to any engineering field cannot be assessed from the page; the interdisciplinary framing is promising but unsupported by visible content.
dimensions.suggestion: Describe example concentration areas (e.g., data/AI for engineering, systems engineering, sustainability) and the courses that populate them so relevance is evident.

descriptionQuality.justification: No courses are attached to the program (sampleCourses is empty), so there are no course descriptions to assess.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: With referencedCourses=0 there are no prerequisites to evaluate for clarity or consistency; the absence of course data is itself the finding.

missingTopics.topic: Engineering data analytics and computational methods
missingTopics.rationale: An interdisciplinary engineering M.S. should offer a shared quantitative/data-methods core; none is visible and it would anchor otherwise disparate electives.
A cross-cutting systems-thinking course is the natural spine for an 'Engineering Science' degree yet no such component is shown.
A master's-level methods and capstone requirement is expected but absent from the page, leaving the culminating experience undefined.
A modern interdisciplinary engineering degree is expected to address sustainability; it could be a defined elective cluster.

emergingTrends.rationale: A flexible engineering-science M.S. is well positioned to offer an applied-ML elective track that serves students from multiple engineering backgrounds.
Model-based systems engineering and simulation are increasingly cross-disciplinary skills the program could formalize.
Funding and industry increasingly reward convergence across engineering, computing, and life sciences, matching this degree's interdisciplinary premise if concentrations are defined.
Interdisciplinary master's programs increasingly let students stack graduate certificates into the degree, a structure this program could advertise explicitly.

topSuggestions: Author a complete catalog page: program description, interdisciplinary intent, and participating departments (currently only a 30-credit total is shown).
Publish a plan of study that breaks the 30 credits into core, elective clusters, and a thesis/capstone.
List representative required and elective courses with prerequisites so structure and progression are visible.
Publish M.S.-level learning outcomes for an interdisciplinary engineering degree.
Define example concentration areas (data/AI, systems engineering, sustainability) to make relevance concrete.

m-s-in-environmental-engineering-482.name: M.S. in Environmental Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 30 referenced courses form a coherent environmental-engineering spine with a real treatment sequence (ENE 663 Water Chemistry -> ENE 664 Physical and Chemical Treatment, plus ENE 665 Biological Treatment and ENE 666 Analysis of Receiving Waters), a water-resources cluster (CE 618 Hydrogeology, CE 620 Open Channel, CE 621 Hydrology, CE 623 Groundwater), and waste/remediation coverage (ENE 660, CE 647, ENE 662). Progression is visible and the disciplinary coverage is strong; the main weakness is that the sequencing is implicit rather than laid out as a plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit sequencing guidance (e.g., ENE 663 before the treatment electives) and group courses into water-quality/treatment, water-resources, and waste/remediation strands so the coherent structure is visible to students.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1 with sensible chains (ENE 663 -> ENE 664, CE 601+MATH 661 -> CE 605), but missingCourseCount is 10 (CE 320/321/322, CHEM 126, MATH 222, MECH 234/236, CS 101, ENE 700/701) - mostly undergraduate-background references dangling in a graduate-only catalog, though ENE 700/701 are genuine graduate gaps.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the dangling ENE 700/701 references and link the undergraduate prerequisite codes to the undergraduate catalog so the 10 missing-course flags clear.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, so the environmental competencies (treatment design, contaminant transport, impact assessment) the degree certifies are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing treatment-process design, contaminant-fate, and environmental-assessment capabilities.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null for a full M.S. degree and no plan is parsed (requirementGroups=0), so the total and the core/elective/thesis split are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and how it divides among required core, electives, and thesis/project options.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most description excerpts are specific and useful (e.g., ENE 664 listing coagulation, flocculation, filtration, disinfection), but ENE 661 (Environmental Microbiology) has an empty description, and there is no plan of study or credit total - concrete presentation defects for the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Fill in the empty ENE 661 (Environmental Microbiology) description and add a plan of study with a credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program covers classical environmental engineering strongly - water/wastewater treatment (ENE 663/664/665), groundwater/hydrogeology (CE 618/623), remediation (ENE 662), stormwater (ENE 672), and chemodynamics (ENE 720) - but the emphasis is somewhat traditional: emerging contaminants (PFAS), air-quality/emissions, climate adaptation, and sustainability/LCA are not visible among the 30 courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Add contemporary electives on emerging contaminants (PFAS, microplastics), climate resilience, and sustainability/life-cycle assessment to modernize the currency.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most of the 30 courses carry specific, informative descriptions (e.g., ENE 664 physical/chemical unit operations; ENE 720 chemodynamics across air-water and soil interfaces), but ENE 661 (Environmental Microbiology) has an empty description and CE 702/ENE 702 special-topics entries are necessarily thin, pulling the aggregate to adequate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in prose and internally consistent where present (ENE 663 -> ENE 664; CE 601+MATH 661 -> CE 605), but 10 dangling references (missingCourseCount=10), including graduate ENE 700/701, reduce prerequisite clarity.

missingTopics.rationale: Treatment courses (ENE 663/664/665) address conventional pollutants but not per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances or microplastics, now central to environmental practice and regulation.
The curriculum is water/waste-centric; air-pollution control and emissions monitoring are not represented among the 30 courses.
No LCA, green-infrastructure, or sustainability course appears, a significant omission for a modern environmental M.S.
Beyond CE 602 GIS and CE 605 remote sensing, there is no environmental data-analytics/modeling course to handle today's monitoring datasets.

emergingTrends.rationale: Regulatory pressure on PFAS is reshaping water treatment; the strong ENE 663/664 treatment spine is the natural place to add advanced-oxidation/adsorption content.

emergingTrends.trend: Climate adaptation and resilient water systems
emergingTrends.rationale: The hydrology/stormwater courses (CE 621, ENE 672) provide a hook to add climate-driven flood and water-security resilience.
Remote sensing (CE 605) already touches neural networks; ML for contaminant-fate and water-quality prediction is a growing research instrument.
The treatment and biological-process courses position the degree to add potable-reuse and nutrient/energy-recovery content that defines modern water engineering.

topSuggestions: Add emerging-contaminant (PFAS/microplastics), climate-resilience, and sustainability/LCA electives to modernize a traditional water/waste emphasis.
Fill in the empty ENE 661 description and publish a plan of study with a credit summary.
State total credits and the core/elective/thesis split to fix the null credit transparency.
Resolve the dangling ENE 700/701 references and link undergraduate prerequisite codes to clear the 10 missing-course flags.
Publish program learning outcomes and explicit course-sequencing guidance for the treatment and water-resources strands.

m-s-in-environmental-science-316.name: M.S. in Environmental Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: With 28 referenced courses the program shows strong, coherent interdisciplinary breadth spanning environmental chemistry (EVSC 610, 612), engineering treatment (ENE 664, 665, 672), risk and toxicology (EVSC 614, 616), microbiology (EVSC 627), policy and economics (EPS 612, 614, 622), sustainability/LCA (ENE 673), and GIS (CE 602), all anchored by EVSC 600 Seminar and independent-study vehicles. The main weakness is that requirementGroups is 0, so the required core versus the elective menu is not delineated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study that fixes a required core (e.g., environmental chemistry, analysis, and risk assessment) and organizes the remaining courses into policy, engineering, and sustainability elective clusters.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1 with missingCourseCount 0, and the chains that exist are sensible (ENE 664 builds on ENE 663 Water Chemistry; ENE 662 on EM 631; EVSC 711 Advanced Environmental Analysis on EVSC 612), giving credible sequencing without over-constraining an interdisciplinary cohort drawn from varied backgrounds.
dimensions.suggestion: Add one or two additional advanced prerequisites (e.g., a toxicology-to-risk-assessment link) so the graph better reflects genuine depth in the analytical track.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present, so intended competencies (analytical methods, regulatory literacy, risk quantification) are left implicit despite a rich course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes tied to the analytical, regulatory, and problem-solving strands the electives already support.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with no parsed plan, so although the elective pool is deep, the total (an environmental-science M.S. is typically ~30 credits) and the balance among core, policy, engineering, and research credits are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and how they divide among required core, elective clusters, and thesis/independent-study credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are consistently present and specific (e.g., ENE 673 Sustainability and Life Cycle Analysis, EVSC 614 Quantitative Environmental Risk Assessment) and the breadth is impressive, but the absence of a labeled plan of study and any credit total leaves the page a course list rather than a legible program map.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study section with labeled core and elective groupings and a credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is highly job-relevant, covering remediation, waste and stormwater management, risk assessment, industrial hygiene (IE 615), and sustainability/LCA that map directly to environmental consulting and regulatory careers; the main gap is limited quantitative environmental data science and climate-modeling content beyond GIS.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an environmental data-science/geospatial-analytics elective and a climate-science course to modernize the analytical offering.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across chemistry, engineering, and policy courses (e.g., EVSC 612's chain-of-custody analytical workflow, EPS 614's economy-environment framing); the set is largely free of thin or placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly, with real intra-program links (ENE 663 to ENE 664, EVSC 612 to EVSC 711) and no dangling references or contradictory conditions.

missingTopics.topic: Environmental data science and geospatial analytics
missingTopics.rationale: CE 602 GIS is the only computational course; modern practice requires spatial statistics, remote-sensing analysis, and programming for environmental data.
EVSC 615 Global Environmental Problems touches global change but no course delivers quantitative climate modeling or greenhouse-gas accounting.
The toxicology and analysis courses predate the PFAS/microplastics agenda now central to regulatory and consulting work.
The list is heavily engineering- and chemistry-weighted with little ecological-systems or biodiversity content expected of a full environmental-science M.S.
Beyond EVSC 614's risk concepts, there is no dedicated quantitative modeling or environmental-statistics course to support rigorous data interpretation.

emergingTrends.rationale: Consulting and public-sector demand is shifting toward adaptation; EPS 622 and ENE 672 provide a foundation to build a resilience strand.
Regulatory momentum on PFAS makes this a near-term priority that EVSC 616 and the ENE treatment courses could be extended to cover.

emergingTrends.trend: Sensor networks and environmental machine learning
emergingTrends.rationale: Low-cost sensing plus ML for pollution monitoring is reshaping field practice and would extend the CE 602 GIS strand.
ENE 673's life-cycle framing positions the program to formalize circular-economy and materials-recovery content.
Policy and regulatory work increasingly foregrounds distributional impacts, a natural extension of the EPS policy cluster.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study that separates a required core from labeled policy, engineering, and sustainability elective clusters.
State total credits and the core/elective/research credit split to close the transparency gap.
Add an environmental data-science/geospatial-analytics elective to modernize the quantitative side beyond GIS.
Introduce climate-science and emerging-contaminant (PFAS) content to align with current regulatory priorities.
Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes tied to analytical, regulatory, and problem-solving competencies.

m-s-in-healthcare-systems-management-553.name: M.S. in Healthcare Systems Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 13 referenced courses form a coherent industrial-engineering/operations toolkit - management science (EM 602), advanced statistics (IE 604), systems simulation (IE 621), operations research (IE 650), quality (IE 672, IE 673), and a project-management pair (EM 636 to EM 637) - but almost none is healthcare-specific, so a degree titled 'Healthcare Systems Management' lacks a visible domain spine (no healthcare operations, informatics, or policy course among the sample). The methods cohere; the healthcare framing does not.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required healthcare-domain core (healthcare operations, health informatics, healthcare policy/economics) so the degree's title matches its curriculum.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1 (EM 636 to EM 637; MIS 648 requiring MIS 645) with missingCourseCount=0, so the graph is clean but shallow - most courses are standalone depth-0 electives, which under-constrains a coherent master's progression. There are no dangling references.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a required methods-then-application sequence (e.g., statistics/OR before a healthcare-analytics capstone) to give the program real depth beyond a single two-course chain.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present, which is especially costly here because outcomes are the only place the healthcare mission could be asserted given the generically industrial course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 outcomes explicitly framing healthcare-management competencies (e.g., optimize patient-flow operations, apply analytics to clinical/administrative data, evaluate health-policy tradeoffs).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30, a transparent standard master's total, but creditReconcileDelta is null and requirementGroups=0, so the split among core, healthcare electives, and general electives is not shown - and with 13 mostly-generic courses visible, the balance toward domain content cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the credit split and designate how many credits must come from healthcare-specific coursework versus the IE/EM methods core.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The 13 descriptions are clear and specific, but the page has no parsed plan of study, no stated outcomes, several 'Restriction:' prefaces (IE 682, IE 699), and MIS 648's prerequisite MIS 645 is not itself among the listed courses, leaving a small navigational gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a labeled plan of study and ensure prerequisite courses like MIS 645 are visible or cross-referenced on the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: This is the program's weakest dimension: despite a title in one of the fastest-growing management fields, the curriculum relies on generic industrial courses (IE 672/673 industrial quality, IE 682 industrial safety, EM 602 management science) with no healthcare informatics, health analytics, hospital operations, health policy, or telehealth content, so it reads dated relative to contemporary healthcare-management practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce healthcare-specific electives (health informatics/EHR analytics, hospital operations, value-based care) so the degree reflects current healthcare-delivery practice rather than generic IE methods.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are clear and specific - EM 602 enumerates its optimization and stochastic methods, IE 604 details experimental design, MIS 648 describes decision-support model management - with no thin entries; the shortfall is topical (industrial rather than healthcare) rather than descriptive quality.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated and internally consistent with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), but several point to undergraduate courses ('IE 331') and MIS 648 requires MIS 645, which does not itself appear in the listed sample, creating a minor traceability gap.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has systems simulation (IE 621) and OR (IE 650) but no course applying them to hospital/clinic operations, the core of healthcare systems management.

missingTopics.topic: Health informatics and clinical data systems
missingTopics.rationale: Only the generic MIS 648 decision-support course appears; there is no EHR, clinical-data, or health-IT course despite the field's dependence on informatics.
IE 672/IE 673 teach industrial quality/TQM, but healthcare-specific quality and patient-safety methods (e.g., adverse-event analysis, accreditation) are absent.
No course covers payment models, reimbursement, or health policy, which govern how healthcare systems actually operate.
IE 604 and IE 650 supply the statistical/OR base, but no course targets predictive modeling for clinical or population-health outcomes.

emergingTrends.trend: Healthcare data analytics and clinical AI
emergingTrends.rationale: Extending IE 604 statistics and MIS 648 decision support toward clinical decision-support and predictive analytics matches where healthcare management is heading.
Post-pandemic telehealth has reshaped delivery operations; a digital-health operations course would modernize the generic operations content.
Adapting IE 673 TQM into lean healthcare and value-based-care improvement aligns the quality strand with current reimbursement models.
