m-s-in-materials-science-and-engineering.name: M.S. in Materials Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The program presents a broad interdisciplinary menu built on a clear MTEN core (610 Foundations of Materials Science, 611 Diffusion and Solid-State Kinetics, 612 Thermodynamics of Materials, 613 Characterization) surrounded by extensive electives spanning every materials class: metals/corrosion (CHE 654, ME 626 fracture), polymers (CHE 683/684/781/782, ME 678/679), ceramics/nanomaterials (CHE 619, CHEM 748), electronic/optical materials (ECE 657/659/626), mechanics (ME 620/621/675), and computation (CHE 775 Molecular Simulations, MTEN 631 Data Science, 633 Machine Learning). Coverage is exemplary, but with 40 cross-listed courses, requirementGroups 0, and a few loosely related entries (CE 641 Soils), the required core versus elective breadth is not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study fixing a required MTEN core and organizing the electives into materials-class tracks (metals, polymers, ceramics/nano, electronic, computational) so the large menu is navigable.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is broadly healthy: missingCourseCount 0 and deepestPrereqChain 2, with sensible coded chains (ME 620 to 621; ECE 657 to 659; CHE 624 to 709/710/714/722). One clear inconsistency is that MTEN 611 lists its prerequisite as 'MTSE 602', using a legacy/foreign subject code rather than the program's MTEN prefix, and prerequisites otherwise mix coded graduate courses with prose undergraduate background.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the MTEN 611 prerequisite from the legacy 'MTSE 602' code to the current MTEN subject code and audit for other legacy-code prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data, a real gap for an M.S. catalog page that a curriculum committee and prospective students would expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes (e.g., structure-property-processing analysis, materials characterization, computational/data-driven materials design) mapped to the MTEN core.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups were parsed, so with 40 electives available neither the required-core credit count nor the total load is published; an M.S. in MSE is typically about 30 credits and that structure is opaque here.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 30-credit total, how many credits must come from the MTEN core versus electives, and the thesis/project options (MTEN 700B/701B).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for essentially all 40 courses, with rich technical detail (e.g., CHE 722 additive manufacturing, MTEN 613 characterization theory). However several entries carry parsing artifacts where prerequisite text bled into the description ('Prerequisites or The course...' in CHE 684; 'Pre or The course...' in CHE 781/782) and MTEN 701B lists a cross-department corequisite (CHE 791); there is also no plan of study or credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the truncated prerequisite/description artifacts (CHE 684, 781, 782), correct the MTEN 701B corequisite, and add a plan-of-study table with a credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The elective set is notably current: additive manufacturing (CHE 722), nanomaterials and nano-characterization (CHE 619, CHEM 748), semiconductor devices and fabrication (ECE 657/659), molecular simulation (CHE 775), and, distinctively, dedicated Data Science (MTEN 631) and Machine Learning (MTEN 633) courses that align the program with the Materials Genome Initiative and integrated computational materials engineering. Gaps are mainly in energy-storage materials and materials sustainability/recycling.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an energy-materials (batteries/photovoltaics) elective and a materials-sustainability/circular-economy elective to complement the strong computational offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific across the metals, polymer, ceramic, electronic, mechanics, and computational electives; only a handful (CHE 684, 781, 782) have leading prerequisite-bleed artifacts, so aggregate quality is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites mix coded graduate courses with prose undergraduate background and have no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), but the legacy 'MTSE 602' code on MTEN 611 and the 'Pre or'/'Prerequisites or' artifacts on CHE 684/781/782 reduce clarity.

missingTopics.topic: Energy-storage and conversion materials (batteries, fuel cells, photovoltaics)
missingTopics.rationale: Despite strong metals, polymer, and electronic-materials coverage, no dedicated energy-materials course appears, a major contemporary materials subfield.

missingTopics.topic: Materials sustainability, recycling, and life-cycle assessment
missingTopics.rationale: With deep polymer (CHE 781/782) and metals content there is no circular-economy or recycling course, now central to materials practice.
Biomaterials courses (BME 651/672/680) are borrowed in, but there is no soft-matter/hydrogels course integrating with the polymer track.

missingTopics.topic: Electronic-structure and multiscale computational materials (DFT, ICME)
missingTopics.rationale: CHE 775 offers molecular simulation, but no density-functional-theory or integrated multiscale course completes the computational stack.
MTEN 613 covers characterization broadly, but advanced synchrotron, in-situ/operando, and atom-probe methods are not offered as an elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: MTEN 631/633 already position the program well; extending toward inverse/generative design and autonomous experimentation would keep it at the frontier.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing of metals and ceramics
emergingTrends.rationale: CHE 722 could expand into metals/ceramics AM and process-microstructure-property linkages that industry increasingly demands.
The ECE/CHEM nanomaterials courses could add graphene/2D and quantum-materials content that is reshaping electronic materials.
The polymer track (CHE 781/782) could incorporate biopolymers, degradable plastics, and recycling to match sustainability demand.
Pairing the data-science courses with autonomous/high-throughput experimentation reflects where materials discovery is heading.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study: a required MTEN core, a 30-credit total, and electives organized into materials-class tracks.
State program learning outcomes mapped to the MTEN core.
Fix data-quality issues: the MTSE 602 to MTEN prerequisite on MTEN 611 and the prerequisite-bleed artifacts on CHE 684/781/782.
Add energy-materials and materials-sustainability/recycling electives to close the main content gaps.
Build on the strong Data Science/Machine Learning courses toward materials informatics, inverse design, and autonomous experimentation.

m-s-in-mechanical-engineering-559.name: M.S. in Mechanical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Only a single course is surfaced, ME 791 Mechanical Engineering Colloquium (0 credits), while the two other references (ME 700, ME 701 project/thesis codes) are missing; the taught ME core (thermofluids, solid mechanics, dynamics and controls, design) is entirely invisible so no progression can be traced.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the graduate ME core and specialization electives plus the project/thesis vehicle so a coherent thermal/mechanical/dynamics-and-controls sequence is visible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and missingCourseCount is 2 (ME 700 and ME 701 are dangling); the one visible course, the colloquium, carries only a clear 'graduate standing and major in mechanical engineering' restriction, so there is no substantive prerequisite graph.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the ME 700/ME 701 project and thesis references and record prerequisites on the taught courses so a real structure appears.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present; a lone colloquium listing gives a prospective research student nothing on which to judge the ME competencies the degree builds.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes describing the analytical, computational, and experimental capabilities of an ME M.S. graduate.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan is parsed (creditReconcileDelta null), so neither the total (an ME master's is typically ~30 credits) nor any core/elective/thesis split is transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and how it divides among required core, electives, and project/thesis credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page shows one zero-credit colloquium, a null total, no plan, and two broken course references, so it cannot orient a prospective student to the degree's content. The single description present is readable but non-representative.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the catalog entry with the full ME course list, a plan of study, and a stated credit total.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: With only the colloquium visible, currency cannot be assessed; mechanical engineering is highly relevant, but the page demonstrates none of the modern thermal, mechatronics, or computational-mechanics content the discipline expects.
dimensions.suggestion: Add and display contemporary electives (mechatronics/controls, computational fluid/solid mechanics, energy systems) so the degree's currency is demonstrable.

descriptionQuality.justification: Only one description is present (ME 791 colloquium), which is clear about the seminar's format and requirement but is a single non-representative sample that cannot establish description quality.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The one visible course states a sensible 'graduate standing and major' restriction, but two of the three references (ME 700, ME 701) are dangling, so prerequisite clarity across the degree cannot be confirmed.

missingTopics.rationale: A thermal-sciences core is expected of an ME master's but is absent from the surfaced page.

missingTopics.topic: Solid mechanics and finite element analysis
missingTopics.rationale: Continuum/computational solid mechanics is foundational graduate ME content not shown.
Modern ME programs require controls and mechatronics; none is visible here.
Simulation-driven engineering is central to contemporary ME yet unrepresented.
The ME 700/ME 701 project and thesis codes are dangling, leaving the culminating research experience undocumented.

emergingTrends.rationale: Simulation-first mechanical design and digital replicas are reshaping ME research and practice.
Integration of sensing, actuation, and control is a leading ME direction warranting a dedicated course.
Design-for-additive and architected materials are transforming mechanical design.
Electrification, batteries, and thermal management are high-growth ME areas the degree should address.
Data-driven surrogate models and physics-informed ML are entering computational mechanics research.

topSuggestions: Rebuild the catalog page with the department's real graduate ME core and electives, since a single colloquium cannot represent the degree.
State the total credit requirement and the core/elective/thesis split (currently null).
Resolve the dangling ME 700/ME 701 project and thesis references.
Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes for ME analytical, computational, and experimental competencies.
Add modern electives (mechatronics/controls, computational mechanics, energy systems) and display them to demonstrate currency.

m-s-in-occupational-safety-and-health-en.name: M.S. in Occupational Safety and Health Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 23 surfaced courses cover the OSHE domain comprehensively and logically: hazard recognition and control (IE 615 Industrial Hygiene, IE 614 Safety Engineering Methods), systems safety (IE 685), ergonomics and human factors (IE 661/664/665, IE 669), toxicology and risk (EVSC 616, EVSC 614), legal aspects (EM 633, IE 608), and a culminating project/independent-research path (IE 700B, IE 725). Short prerequisite chains such as IE 614 into IE 675 show intended progression from foundations to specialization.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the pool explicitly into required core versus elective concentrations (safety, industrial hygiene, ergonomics) so the intended pathway through the 36 credits is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1, so gating exists (e.g., IE 675 requires IE 614; IE 665 requires IE 355 or IE 699) but is shallow for a 36-credit professional degree; missingCourseCount is 2 (IE 681 and IE 700 dangling), which are minor code issues against an otherwise clean set.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the IE 681 and IE 700 references and deepen a couple of chains (e.g., statistics into risk-assessment and systems-safety courses) so sequencing is clearer.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data; despite a rich course pool, the page states no explicit competencies (hazard anticipation, exposure assessment, safety-program management) that graduates should attain.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes aligned to recognized OSH competency frameworks (e.g., anticipate, recognize, evaluate, and control workplace hazards).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 36, transparently stated and appropriately above the 30-credit norm for this professionally oriented, NIOSH-affiliated degree (note IE 682 references a trainee scholarship), but creditReconcileDelta is null and no core/elective credit split is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study specifying how the 36 credits divide among required core, technical electives, and the project/research requirement.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The entry is rich and readable: 23 courses with substantive, specific descriptions and stated credits, and clean titles across IE, EVSC, EM, BME, HRM, and ME prefixes. The main gap is the absence of a parsed plan of study or labeled core/elective terms (requirementGroups is 0).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a visible plan-of-study structure (core vs. elective groupings and any recommended sequence) to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum closely tracks contemporary OSH practice: industrial hygiene (IE 615), systems safety (IE 685), risk assessment (EVSC 614), toxicology (EVSC 616), HAZWOPER (EVSC 603), cognitive engineering (IE 662), and legal/product-liability topics (EM 633, IE 608), reflecting the multidisciplinary reality of the profession. Notable gaps are formal process-safety management, safety data analytics, and Total Worker Health.
dimensions.suggestion: Add electives in process safety management and safety data analytics to close the most visible currency gaps.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are consistently specific and informative, for example IE 615 detailing recognition/evaluation/control of noise, heat, chemical, and radiation exposures, and IE 685 covering system-safety planning and management; the pool is largely free of thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are frequently stated but as prose that leans on undergraduate references (e.g., 'IE 355 or equivalent', 'one year of college physics and one semester of college chemistry or biology'), and a few are vague ('human factors engineering'); two dangling references (IE 681, IE 700) slightly reduce consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: Chemical/process safety and hazard analysis (PHA, LOPA) is a core professional area only partially touched by IE 685 and IE 614.
No course applies statistics/ML to incident and exposure data despite IE 604 being available in the department.
Quantifying exposure-disease relationships complements the toxicology (EVSC 616) and risk (EVSC 614) courses but is not represented.
A high-hazard, heavily regulated sector is not addressed as a dedicated course in the visible list.
Beyond IE 614's fire-protection mention, a dedicated fire-safety/emergency-preparedness course is missing.

emergingTrends.trend: Wearable sensors and real-time exposure monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: IoT-based dosimetry and physiological monitoring are transforming industrial hygiene practice and extend IE 615.
Machine-learning models for incident prediction and leading-indicator analytics are entering safety management.

emergingTrends.trend: Total Worker Health and psychosocial hazards
emergingTrends.rationale: NIOSH's integration of well-being, stress, and ergonomics broadens the field beyond physical hazards, extending IE 661/664.
Safe deployment of collaborative robots (cobots) is a growing occupational-safety concern relevant to the ergonomics strand.
Designing out hazards early is a rising professional emphasis that builds directly on IE 675 Safety in Facility and Product Design.

topSuggestions: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes tied to recognized OSH competencies (anticipate, recognize, evaluate, control).
Group the 23-course pool into explicit required-core and elective concentrations and show the 36-credit plan of study.
Add a process safety management elective and a safety data-analytics elective to close the clearest currency gaps.
Resolve the dangling IE 681 and IE 700 references and standardize prerequisite wording.
Introduce modern content on wearable exposure monitoring and predictive safety analytics.

m-s-in-pharmaceutical-chemistry-318.name: M.S. in Pharmaceutical Chemistry

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 45-course inventory reveals a coherent professional-science-master's design: a pharmaceutical spine of drug design and action (CHEM 777, 716, 719), pharmaceutical analysis (CHEM 714), computational modeling (CHEM 737), and nanomaterials (CHEM 748), a chemistry/biology foundation (CHEM 605, 673; BIOL 605, 606), a deliberate business/management elective set (ECON 610, FIN 600, MGMT 620/640/650, EM 636), and applied capstones (CHEM 595 Practicum, CHEM 700B Master's Project). The one weakness is that requirementGroups is 0, so the required-versus-elective boundary of this otherwise well-structured program is not shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study that fixes the required pharmaceutical core and the professional-skills elective block so the PSM structure is explicit rather than inferred from the course list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1 and missingCourseCount is 0, with a sensible foundational link (BIOL 606 Applied Bioprocessing builds on BIOL 605); the graph is appropriately light for a professional master's that admits students from varied science backgrounds and gates chiefly on undergraduate organic chemistry.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit prerequisite from CHEM 777 Principles of Pharmaceutical Chemistry into the advanced drug-delivery/design electives so the professional sequence's depth is visible.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present, which is a notable omission for a professional master's whose value proposition (industry-ready technical plus business competencies) would benefit most from explicit outcomes.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 PSM learning outcomes spanning drug-development science, analytical/regulatory skills, and professional/business competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30, a transparent and standard M.S. total, and the course mix visibly balances technical pharmaceutical science against a professional/business block plus an applied practicum and project; creditReconcileDelta is null only because no term-by-term plan is parsed, not because of an inconsistency.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the credit split (required pharmaceutical core, professional electives, practicum/project) within the 30-credit total to make the balance explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most descriptions are clear and program-specific (e.g., CHEM 719 Drug Delivery Systems, CHEM 777 pharmacodynamics/pharmacokinetics), and the stated 30-credit total helps, but several entries have prerequisite text bleeding into the description field (CHEM 716 and CHEM 719 show run-on 'Strong background in organic chemistry...' strings) and no plan of study is displayed.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the CHEM 716/719 records so prerequisites and descriptions are cleanly separated, and add a labeled plan of study.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program is strongly aligned with the New Jersey pharmaceutical corridor, covering computer-aided drug design (CHEM 716), delivery systems, pharmaceutical analysis, nanomaterials, and a cell-and-gene-therapy practicum (CHEM 595), plus regulatory-adjacent business courses; the main gaps are a dedicated regulatory-affairs/cGMP-quality course and formal biologics/biopharmaceutics content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a regulatory affairs and cGMP/quality-by-design course and a biologics/biopharmaceutics elective to complete the industry skill set.

descriptionQuality.justification: The pharmaceutical and science courses are described specifically and informatively (CHEM 714's survey of pharmaceutical instrumental analysis, CHEM 777's coverage of pharmacodynamics and molecular targets); only the generic 'Special Topics' entries (CHEM 601, 702) and the management electives are less program-specific.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear and free of dangling references, but the data quality is uneven: CHEM 716 and CHEM 719 have prerequisite strings that run into course-description text, muddying what is actually required.

missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses FDA/ICH regulatory pathways or GMP quality systems, essential knowledge for pharmaceutical-industry roles the degree targets.
The core is small-molecule-centric (CHEM 605, 777); protein/antibody therapeutics and their formulation are a large and growing share of the pipeline that is underrepresented.
CHEM 777 introduces PK/PD conceptually, but there is no dedicated quantitative ADMET or PBPK modeling course now standard in drug development.
The program covers discovery and delivery but not process development, crystallization, or manufacturing scale-up needed for CMC roles.
Beyond CHEM 737 modeling, there is no data-science course for handling screening, omics, or chemical-library data central to modern discovery.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML and generative models in drug discovery
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine-learning-driven molecular generation and property prediction are transforming discovery; CHEM 716 and CHEM 737 are natural anchors to modernize.
The CHEM 595 cell-and-gene-therapy practicum and CHEM 748 nanomaterials position the program to formalize advanced-modality delivery as a strand.

emergingTrends.trend: Continuous manufacturing and process analytical technology
emergingTrends.rationale: Industry is shifting to continuous, PAT-monitored production, a CMC-facing trend the analysis courses could be extended toward.

emergingTrends.trend: Targeted protein degradation (PROTACs) and new modalities
emergingTrends.rationale: Novel modalities beyond classic small molecules are reshaping medicinal chemistry that CHEM 777/716 should introduce.
Growth in biologics and biosimilars makes analytical characterization of large molecules an increasingly demanded competency.

topSuggestions: Add a regulatory-affairs and cGMP/quality-by-design course, the most consequential gap for a pharmaceutical PSM's employability.
Introduce biologics/biopharmaceutics and PK/ADMET-modeling content to balance the small-molecule-centric core.
Publish the plan of study and the credit split within the stated 30 credits (pharmaceutical core, professional electives, practicum/project).
Clean up the CHEM 716 and CHEM 719 records so prerequisites and descriptions are properly separated.
Publish 3-5 PSM learning outcomes spanning technical, analytical, and professional/business competencies.

m-s-in-pharmaceutical-engineering-453.name: M.S. in Pharmaceutical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The program is a coherent hybrid of chemical-engineering fundamentals (CHE 611 Thermodynamics, 612 Kinetics, 624 Transport, 603 Separations, 714 Particle Technology) and a dedicated pharmaceutical-engineering spine: PHEN 601 Principles feeds PHEN 603 Unit Operations, which together gate PHEN 602 Facility Design and PHEN 605 Packaging, complemented by PHEN 604 Validation/Regulatory and PHEN 618 Pharmacokinetics and Drug Delivery. The PHEN sequence builds sensibly; the main limitation is that requirementGroups is 0, so required core versus electives is not delineated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study showing the required ChemE-fundamentals plus PHEN core and any electives or tracks (e.g., drug-product versus bioprocessing).

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is sensible with deepestPrereqChain 1 and missingCourseCount 0: coded PHEN chains (PHEN 601/603 gate 602 and 605) and CHE 714 building on CHE 624, plus CHE 603's explicit list of six undergraduate prerequisites (CHE 342/349/363/364/367/471) that defines the bridge for entrants. Depth is modest, as expected for a professional M.S.
dimensions.suggestion: Consider an advanced second-level elective (e.g., building on PHEN 618 drug delivery) so the program shows progression beyond a single prerequisite level.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are published, a real gap for a professionally oriented M.S. whose graduates enter regulated pharmaceutical manufacturing.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes (e.g., design and scale pharmaceutical unit operations, apply cGMP/validation, analyze pharmacokinetics) mapped to the PHEN and CHE courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups exist, so although the 11 visible 3-credit courses imply a roughly 30-credit degree, neither the total nor the required-core-versus-elective split is stated; the bridge-program credits referenced in PHEN 602/605 further complicate the picture without a published structure.
dimensions.suggestion: State the roughly 30-credit total and the required-core versus elective split, and clarify the bridge-program credits for non-ChemE entrants.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present, specific, and informative for all 11 courses, including detailed PHEN entries on FDA processes (PHEN 601, 604), facility design (PHEN 602), and packaging (PHEN 605). The page nonetheless lacks a plan of study, labeled terms, and a stated credit total, so structure must be inferred from prerequisites.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table and a credit summary, and clearly describe the bridge-program pathway referenced in PHEN 602/605.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum targets the pharmaceutical-manufacturing industry directly and currently: unit operations (PHEN 603), cGMP facility design (PHEN 602), validation and regulatory affairs (PHEN 604), packaging technology (PHEN 605), pharmacokinetics and drug delivery (PHEN 618), and particle technology (CHE 714). This is highly employable, especially for New Jersey's pharma corridor. The main gaps are continuous manufacturing/PAT-QbD and biologics/biopharmaceutical processing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit continuous-manufacturing and Quality-by-Design/PAT content and a biologics/biopharmaceutical-processing course to match where pharmaceutical manufacturing is heading.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific across both the CHE fundamentals and the PHEN specialization (e.g., PHEN 604 on the Federal Code of Regulations, PHEN 603 on scaling liquid/dispersed-phase processes), with no placeholder entries.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are mostly clear with coded PHEN chains and CHE 603's explicit undergraduate list, and no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0); clarity is only slightly reduced by conditional prose ('completion of the bridge program for students who are required to take it').

missingTopics.topic: Biopharmaceuticals and bioprocessing (upstream/downstream, cell culture, purification)
missingTopics.rationale: The program is oriented to small-molecule and solid/liquid dosage processing (PHEN 603/605, CHE 714); biologics now dominate the pipeline yet have no dedicated course.

missingTopics.topic: Continuous manufacturing and Process Analytical Technology/Quality-by-Design
missingTopics.rationale: PHEN 603 and 604 cover batch unit operations and validation but not continuous manufacturing, PAT, or QbD, which the FDA actively encourages.
CHE 714 (particle technology) and PHEN 603 (liquids/dispersions) exist, but there is no dedicated solid-dosage formulation and design course.

missingTopics.topic: Pharmaceutical data science and process modeling
missingTopics.rationale: No course applies data analytics, modeling, or digital-manufacturing methods to pharmaceutical processes, an increasingly expected competency.
Facility design (PHEN 602) and packaging (PHEN 605) are covered, but dedicated sterile/aseptic processing and fill-finish operations are absent.

emergingTrends.rationale: FDA-encouraged continuous processing and real-time quality assurance are reshaping pharma manufacturing and should modernize PHEN 603/604.
The fastest-growing pharmaceutical segment calls for a bioprocessing course covering upstream/downstream biologics operations.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML and digital twins for pharmaceutical processes
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven process development, control, and digital twins are emerging skills that would extend the program's transport/unit-operations base.

emergingTrends.trend: Advanced and nanoparticle drug delivery (lipid nanoparticles, mRNA, long-acting injectables)
emergingTrends.rationale: PHEN 618 could be extended toward modern delivery platforms that now anchor the industry pipeline.
Solvent reduction and sustainable process design are growing regulatory and industry priorities relevant to the unit-operations curriculum.

topSuggestions: Publish the plan of study with the credit total (about 30), required core versus electives, and the bridge-program pathway for non-ChemE entrants.
State program learning outcomes mapped to the PHEN and CHE courses.
Add a biologics/bioprocessing course (upstream/downstream) to complement the small-molecule and solid/liquid-dose focus.
Introduce continuous-manufacturing and PAT/Quality-by-Design content, modernizing PHEN 603/604.
Add a pharmaceutical data-science/process-modeling course and extend PHEN 618 toward advanced/nanoparticle drug delivery.

m-s-in-pharmaceutical-systems-management.name: M.S. in Pharmaceutical Systems Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 16 courses form a coherent blend of pharmaceutical-engineering fundamentals (PHEN 601 Principles of Pharmaceutical Engineering, PHEN 602 Facility Design, PHEN 604 Validation and Regulatory, PHEN 605 Packaging) with a management/systems core (EM 636/637 project management and control, EM 602 Management Science, IE 673 TQM, IE 659 Supply Chain Engineering, IE 618 cost/production economics). Prerequisite chains such as PHEN 601 into PHEN 602/605 and EM 636 into EM 637 give the plan clear internal progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Distinguish a required pharma+management core from electives so the intended path through the 30 credits, rather than a flat pool, is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is clean and sensible: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references) and deepestPrereqChain is 1, with logical gates like EM 637 requiring EM 636 (or MGMT 641) and PHEN 602/605 requiring PHEN 601/603 plus named undergraduate courses. Depth is modest but appropriate for a professional master's with many parallel electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Consider one or two deeper chains (e.g., a statistics/management-science prerequisite ahead of supply-chain and quality courses) to reinforce sequencing.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present; despite a strong, well-referenced course pool, the page states no explicit competencies (regulatory compliance, pharma operations, supply-chain and quality management) graduates should attain.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes describing the regulatory, operations, and management capabilities a pharmaceutical-systems-management graduate develops.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30, transparent and standard for the degree, and the 16-course pool comfortably exceeds the ~10 courses needed, implying genuine elective choice; however, creditReconcileDelta is null and no explicit core/elective credit split is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study specifying how the 30 credits divide among required core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The entry is clear and complete: 16 courses with specific descriptions, stated credits, and clean cross-references (missingCourseCount 0) spanning PHEN, EM, IE, HRM, and MNE prefixes. The chief gap is the absence of a parsed plan of study or labeled core/elective grouping (requirementGroups 0).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a visible plan-of-study structure (core vs. elective groupings) to complement the already-strong descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program maps directly onto pharmaceutical-industry needs: GMP validation and regulation (PHEN 604), facility and packaging engineering (PHEN 602/605), supply-chain engineering (IE 659), total quality management (IE 673), and project management (EM 636/637). Gaps relative to the current frontier include Quality by Design/PAT, continuous manufacturing, and biologics/advanced-therapy operations.
dimensions.suggestion: Add electives on Quality by Design/Process Analytical Technology and continuous/biologics manufacturing to track the industry's current direction.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative, for example PHEN 604 covering the Code of Federal Regulations and its industry impact, IE 659 detailing supply-chain design and logistics networks, and EM 602 enumerating LP, queueing, and Markov methods; the pool contains no thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and are internally consistent with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), including explicit chains such as EM 637 requiring 'EM 636 or MGMT 641' and PHEN 602 listing PHEN 601, PHEN 603 plus named undergraduate courses.

missingTopics.topic: Quality by Design (QbD) and Process Analytical Technology (PAT)
missingTopics.rationale: Modern FDA-aligned development and control frameworks are not explicitly covered despite strong validation (PHEN 604) and quality (IE 673) content.
No dedicated statistics/analytics course appears (IE 604 exists in the department) even though data-driven process control is central to pharma operations.
The visible courses emphasize small-molecule facilities and packaging; biologics/cell-and-gene manufacturing is a major and growing segment not represented.
Health-economics and reimbursement content would strengthen the 'management' side beyond project and cost economics.
Track-and-trace/DSCSA compliance is a specific pharma supply-chain requirement not surfaced alongside IE 659.

emergingTrends.rationale: Regulators now encourage continuous processing and digitalized plants, extending PHEN 602 facility design toward smart pharma manufacturing.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML in drug development and manufacturing
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine learning for process optimization, deviation detection, and R&D is rapidly entering the industry.
Temperature-controlled distribution for biologics and cell/gene therapies is reshaping pharma supply chains (IE 659).

emergingTrends.trend: Computer Software Assurance (CSA) and modern validation
emergingTrends.rationale: Risk-based CSA is replacing traditional CSV, a direct evolution of the PHEN 604 validation content.
Green chemistry, solvent recovery, and energy-efficient facilities are a rising industry priority the facility-design course could incorporate.

topSuggestions: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes describing regulatory, operations, and management competencies.
Show an explicit plan of study distinguishing required core from electives across the 30 credits.
Add electives on Quality by Design/PAT and continuous or biologics manufacturing to track the industry frontier.
Introduce a pharmaceutical data-analytics course to strengthen data-driven process control.
Add serialization/supply-chain-security content alongside IE 659 Supply Chain Engineering.

m-s-in-power-and-energy-systems-524.name: M.S. in Power and Energy Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 32 referenced courses form a strong, coherent power/energy spine: steady-state analysis (ECE 610) leading to transients (ECE 611) and protection (ECE 613), plus power electronics (ECE 616), economic control/dispatch (ECE 617), renewables/photovoltaics (ECE 618), smart-grid sensing (ECE 619), electromagnetic field theory (ECE 620), and power-system dynamics (ECE 656), sensibly complemented by cross-disciplinary energy electives (ME 607 thermodynamics, ME 610 heat transfer, ENE 671 environmental impact). Progression is clear (ECE 610 feeds ECE 611 and ECE 613).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a required power core (analysis, protection, electronics, dynamics) versus an energy-electives group so the strong latent structure is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health

ECE 613's prerequisite field again carries description text instead of a clean 'ECE 610 or equivalent'.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the ECE 613 prerequisite string and mark the five ECE 2xx/3xx codes as undergraduate preparation.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are stated in the data, leaving the power-engineering competencies (analysis, protection, economic operation, renewables integration) undocumented.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes covering power-system analysis, protection/dynamics, and renewable/grid-integration and map them to ECE 610/613/618/656.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance

dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and how they divide among required power courses, energy/interdisciplinary electives, and the project.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and specific for most of the 27 sampled courses (e.g., ECE 617 on economic dispatch and optimal power flow), but ECE 613 is blank and there is no plan of study or labeled sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table and fill the ECE 613 description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is well-aligned with the energy transition: photovoltaics and renewable systems (ECE 618), intelligent sensing for smart grid and smart city (ECE 619), power electronics (ECE 616), and economic/optimal power flow (ECE 617), with thermo/heat-transfer electives (ME 607/610) supporting energy-conversion breadth.
dimensions.suggestion: Add grid-scale energy-storage and EV-charging content so the renewables emphasis (ECE 618/619) extends to storage and electrified transportation.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are informative and domain-specific (e.g., ECE 656 on power-system dynamics/stability, ECE 618 on solar and storage), but stored as truncated excerpts with at least ECE 613 left blank.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are mostly clean codes with sensible power-domain layering (ECE 610 to ECE 611/613), but ECE 613's field is contaminated with description prose and five undergraduate references (ECE 232/321/333/361/372) read as dangling.

missingTopics.topic: Grid-scale energy storage and battery systems
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 618 mentions storage only in passing; a dedicated battery/energy-storage and its grid-integration course is essential to a modern power program.
The core (ECE 610-617) is transmission/bulk-system oriented with no microgrid or DER-integration course despite distribution-level electrification.
ECE 616 teaches classical thyristor/inverter power electronics but not the wide-bandgap devices now central to efficient converters and grid inverters.
ECE 617 covers economic dispatch but not restructured markets, LMP pricing, or ancillary-services markets that govern modern grid operation.
With smart-grid sensing (ECE 619) present, the absence of a grid-cybersecurity/resilience course is a notable gap given SCADA/OT threat exposure.

emergingTrends.rationale: As grids become inverter-dominated, stability with grid-forming inverters is a frontier topic that ECE 656 and ECE 616 should address.
Transportation electrification is reshaping distribution planning; ECE 616/617 could add EV-charging and V2G content.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for grid operations and forecasting
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine learning for load/renewables forecasting and state estimation is growing; ECE 619's sensing course could link to data-driven grid analytics.
Deep decarbonization couples power with heat/hydrogen; the ME 607/610 and ENE 671 electives position the program to add sector-coupling content.

emergingTrends.trend: Grid resilience under extreme weather and cyber threats
emergingTrends.rationale: Climate-driven outages and OT cyberattacks make resilience central; a resilience/cybersecurity course would modernize the protection track (ECE 613).

topSuggestions: Publish a required power core versus energy-electives structure with program learning outcomes for power/energy competencies.
State the total credit requirement and the core/elective/project distribution now hidden by null statedTotalCredits.
Add grid-scale energy-storage and microgrid/DER-integration courses to extend the renewables emphasis (ECE 618/619).
Introduce electricity-markets and power-system-cybersecurity/resilience courses to modernize the economic (ECE 617) and protection (ECE 613) tracks.
Fix data hygiene: clean and fill the ECE 613 prerequisite and description fields.

m-s-in-software-engineering-217.name: M.S. in Software Engineering

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are informative and specific (e.g., CS 690 team-based industrial-scale project, IS 663 process models and agile/lean principles, CS 685 software-architecture terms and methods), with no placeholder entries in the sample.

Bridges CS 675 and the SE core as software increasingly embeds machine-learning components.

m-s-in-telecommunications-525.name: M.S. in Telecommunications

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression

Progression is present (CS 656 to CS 652; ECE 673/683 to ECE 783).
dimensions.suggestion: State an explicit required core (comms theory, wireless, networks) plus electives so the coherent 5G-centric design is visible rather than inferred.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health

Clarity is hurt by CS 696's prerequisite field, which embeds course-description text after the 'CS 652 or CS 656 or ECE 637 or ECE 683' list.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the CS 696 prerequisite string and annotate the four ECE 3xx/4xx codes as undergraduate preparation.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program is highly current: the 5G-focused ECE 642/644/645 sequence, cognitive cloud networking (CS 652), cloud/IoT (ECE 683), network security (CS 696), and data mining (MGMT 635) reflect where telecommunications practice and research are heading.
dimensions.suggestion: Add optical/fiber-communications and Open-RAN content so the wireless-heavy 5G track (ECE 644/645) is balanced by transport-network and disaggregated-RAN topics.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and complete across all sampled courses (e.g., ECE 644 on wireless-channel characterization and the cellular concept, CS 652 on cloud-scale networking), with no blank entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites use course codes with sensible layering (ECE 783 requires ECE 673 and ECE 683), but CS 696 embeds description prose in its prerequisite field and four undergraduate references (ECE 321/333/353/421) read as dangling.

missingTopics.rationale: The sampled list is wireless/IP-heavy (ECE 642/644/645, ECE 783) with no optical/fiber-transport course, yet fiber is the backbone of all telecom traffic.
ECE 673 provides random-signal foundations but there is no information-theory/coding course underpinning capacity limits and modern error-correction (LDPC/polar).
The 5G courses (ECE 644/645) treat system/link design but not the RF/mmWave and antenna hardware essential to 5G/6G radio front ends.
CS 696 covers network security broadly, but there is no dedicated applied-crypto/telecom-security course for signaling, SIM/identity, and lawful-intercept concerns.

missingTopics.topic: Spectrum policy, regulation, and network economics
missingTopics.rationale: A telecommunications M.S. lacks a spectrum-management/regulatory-economics course, which shapes how real carrier networks are planned and operated.

emergingTrends.rationale: Wireless research is moving beyond 5G; the ECE 644/645 sequence should preview massive-MIMO, mmWave/THz, and 6G use cases.
Carriers are disaggregating radios into open, virtualized components; ECE 645/CS 652 could add O-RAN and RAN-virtualization content.
LEO mega-constellations are extending telecom coverage; the wireless track should introduce satellite/non-terrestrial network integration.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for wireless (spectrum sensing, channel estimation)
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine learning is entering the physical and MAC layers; MGMT 635 data mining and ECE 673 could feed an ML-for-wireless elective.
5G monetization depends on slicing and edge compute; ECE 645/683 should cover network slicing and MEC service placement.

topSuggestions: Add optical/fiber-communications and information-theory/coding courses to balance the wireless-heavy 5G spine.
Publish program learning outcomes tied to the ECE 642/644/645/783 sequence.
Formalize a required comms/networks core plus electives and add a credit breakdown under the stated 30 credits.
Introduce RF/mmWave front-end and Open-RAN content to modernize the 5G/6G radio track.
Clean the contaminated CS 696 prerequisite string and label the undergraduate ECE references.

management-essentials-593.name: Management Essentials

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: For a compact four-course certificate the selection is a clean cross-functional sampler with one course per major business function: ACCT 615 Management Accounting, FIN 600 Corporate Finance I, MIS 645 IT and Competitive Advantage, and MRKT 620 Global Marketing. That is coherent as a business primer, but a certificate titled Management Essentials notably omits a management/organizational-behavior or leadership course, so it reads more as business functions than management.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or swap in an organizational-behavior/leadership course (e.g., HRM 601) so the credential's core actually covers managing people, matching its title.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and missingCourseCount is 0, meaning all four courses are foundational, ungated, and reference-clean, exactly right for an introductory, stackable certificate aimed at professionals entering management study.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the flat structure but note any recommended ordering (e.g., ACCT 615 and FIN 600 early) for students new to business coursework.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes are stated, so what a completer should be able to do across the four functions is left to inference from course titles.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 outcomes describing the cross-functional business literacy (accounting, finance, IT, marketing) the certificate builds.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a certificate the null statedTotalCredits is a transparency note, not a defect; the four uniform 3-credit courses give a clean, balanced 12-credit credential with exactly one course per functional area.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 12-credit total explicitly and confirm all four courses are required versus a choose-from menu, to remove ambiguity.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: All four courses have clear, specific descriptions and consistent credits, but the entry lacks a total-credit statement, a purpose header, and outcomes, so it presents as a short list rather than a framed credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line purpose statement and credit total above the four courses.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The functional foundations are solid and MRKT 620 (global marketing) and MIS 645 (IT and competitive advantage) keep the set reasonably contemporary, but the four courses are generic and carry no analytics, digital, or data emphasis, so the credential feels foundational rather than current.
dimensions.suggestion: Refresh at least one slot toward a data/digital-literacy course so the certificate signals modern managerial skills alongside the classic functions.

descriptionQuality.justification: Each of the four courses carries a specific, informative description (ACCT 615 on accrual and managerial accounting for decision making, MIS 645 on how people, process, and technology make organizations effective), with no thin or placeholder entries.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: There are no prerequisites on any course and no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), unambiguous and internally consistent for a foundational certificate, correctly signaling open access.

missingTopics.rationale: A certificate named Management Essentials has no people-management or leadership course; the four slots cover accounting, finance, IT, and marketing but not managing teams.
No course addresses how work and processes are managed, a basic managerial function absent from the current four.
There is no strategy or general-management integrating course to connect the four functional views into managerial decision making.
The certificate includes no quantitative/analytics content, leaving completers without the data literacy expected of modern managers.
Core managerial skills of communication and ethical decision making are not represented among the four courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: Even an essentials certificate should expose managers to basic analytics; MIS 645 could be paired with a data-literacy module.
MIS 645 (IT and competitive advantage) positions the certificate to address how digital tools reshape each business function.
Adding an OB/leadership course would let the certificate cover managing distributed teams, now a baseline managerial skill.

master-of-architecture-161.name: Master of Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 20 referenced courses form a clear professional M.Arch spine: a six-step studio sequence (Studio I-IV plus Advanced Studios I-II, deepestPrereqChain 5), paralleled by two-course sequences in History, Construction, Environmental Control Systems, and Structures that feed the studios, capped by a Synthesis Seminar and Professional Practice I-II. Progression is well-ordered and the technical courses are timed to support studio work; the main weakness is that no plan-of-study groups were parsed (requirementGroups 0), so term-by-term balance and elective load are not visible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit term-by-term plan of study mapping each studio to its co-requisite technical courses so the coordinated sequencing that clearly exists in the prerequisites is legible on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and the studio chain reaches a sensible depth of 5 (501G to 502G to 503G to 504G to 505G to 506G) with technical prerequisites like Structures I to II and History I to II well-formed. The only blemish is ARCH 542G, whose prerequisiteRaw string has course-catalog description text bled into it, muddying an otherwise clean set.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the ARCH 542G prerequisite field so it reads only 'ARCH 541G' and audit for other cases where description text has contaminated the prerequisite parse.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data, which is a real gap for a first-professional, NAAB-accredited degree where accreditation is organized around explicit Student Performance / Program Criteria.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the NAAB Program Criteria the degree satisfies as stated learning outcomes and map each to the studios and technical courses that assess them.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta cannot be computed because no plan groups were parsed, so a prospective student cannot see the total credit load or how it splits among studio, technical, history, and professional-practice courses. For a full professional degree (typically ~90-110 credits, or ~60 for advanced standing) this credit opacity is a substantive transparency defect rather than a minor note.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the credit distribution across studio, building-technology, history/theory, and professional-practice components, and note any advanced-standing pathway.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present, specific, and informative for all 20 sampled courses, and titles clearly signal the sequence. However the page lacks a legible plan of study, a stated total credit count, and any labeled terms, so the reader must infer structure from prerequisites alone.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study table with labeled terms and a credit summary to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects solid contemporary professional emphases: sustainable design and passive/active environmental systems (ARCH 541G-544G), structures, landscape and urbanism, and two professional-practice courses covering legal/ethical obligations. It is comparatively light on computational design and BIM beyond the introductory Tools and Techniques courses, and shows no explicit digital-fabrication or building-performance-simulation content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or elevate a required BIM/computational-design course and a building-energy-modeling course so graduates enter practice fluent in the digital workflows firms now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific, articulating each course's scope (e.g., ECS I on passive systems for thermal/visual comfort, Structures I on gravity and lateral behavior) rather than placeholder text; excerpts are truncated but clearly full entries exist.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible sequencing and no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0); clarity is reduced only by ARCH 542G's prerequisite string absorbing description prose and by 'graduate level standing' being used where a specific gate might be clearer.

missingTopics.rationale: Beyond the introductory ARCH 500G/555G Tools and Techniques, there is no required course in Revit/BIM coordination or parametric modeling, now baseline skills for architectural practice and integrated project delivery.
ECS I-II teach principles of environmental systems but no course covers quantitative energy/daylight modeling (e.g., EnergyPlus, Ladybug) needed to hit code and net-zero targets.

Construction I-II and the Synthesis Seminar address assemblies conceptually, but there is no CNC/robotic fabrication or advanced construction-technology course connecting design to modern manufacturing.
The sequence ends at Advanced Studio II and a Synthesis Seminar with no visible research-methods or independent thesis vehicle, limiting scholarly and evidence-based design preparation.
With most carbon savings now coming from retrofitting rather than new construction, the absence of a dedicated adaptive-reuse/renovation studio or seminar is a notable gap.

emergingTrends.rationale: Embodied-carbon accounting and mass-timber/CLT construction are reshaping structural and material choices; Structures and Construction courses should incorporate life-cycle carbon and engineered-timber content.
Generative and AI-assisted design tools are entering studio and documentation workflows; the Tools and Techniques sequence should be modernized to address them critically.
Sea-level rise and extreme heat make resilient, passive-survivability design central; the ECS and studio sequence should foreground climate-adaptive strategies.
Post-pandemic emphasis on indoor environmental quality and occupant wellbeing (WELL, biophilia) is a growing client demand that the environmental-systems track could integrate.
Professional practice is increasingly measured on inclusive, community-engaged process; Professional Practice I-II could add participatory design and social-equity content.

topSuggestions: Publish the total credit count and a term-by-term plan of study showing studio/technical/history/practice distribution, replacing the current null statedTotalCredits.
State NAAB-aligned program learning outcomes and map them to the studio and technical sequence.
Add required BIM/computational-design and building-energy-modeling courses to close the biggest currency gap versus practice.
Introduce a design-research-methods course and thesis or capstone research vehicle after Advanced Studio II.
Integrate embodied-carbon, mass-timber, and adaptive-reuse content into the Structures, Construction, and studio sequences.
