missingTopics.rationale: Between Environment Design and the game track, there is no explicit technical-art/shader or procedural-generation course, a key employable skill bridging art and engineering.
No course addresses generative-AI tools for image, video, 3D, or animation, now integral to digital-design production pipelines.
For a terminal MFA there is no visible professional-practice, portfolio, or thesis-exhibition course to prepare graduates for the creative job market or academia.

missingTopics.topic: Motion capture and character animation pipeline
missingTopics.rationale: Character/Facial Modeling and Acting for Animators exist, but no course covers the mocap-to-rig-to-animation production pipeline that studios expect.

emergingTrends.rationale: AI image/video/3D generation is transforming design workflows; the foundations and studios should teach critical, hands-on use of these tools.
Game-engine-driven virtual production is reshaping animation and film; the animation and XR studios are positioned to adopt it.
The DD 650 XR gateway should evolve toward current spatial-computing platforms (e.g., Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest) as they mainstream.
The UI/UX track should foreground accessibility and inclusive-design standards, now a baseline expectation in product design.

emergingTrends.trend: Ethics of AI-generated and immersive media
emergingTrends.rationale: With generative and immersive media raising authorship and consent questions, the history/theory courses could add an ethics strand.

topSuggestions: Publish the plan of study and total credits, showing the shared core, the DD 650 gateway, the three studio tracks, and the capstone.
State MFA program learning outcomes covering creative research, technical mastery, and portfolio/exhibition readiness.
Replace the vague 'Basic background in in the use...' prose prerequisite with concrete course/portfolio gates and fix the 'in in' typo.
Add explicit real-time game-engine and AI-assisted content-creation content to keep the game/XR tracks current.
Add a professional-practice/portfolio (thesis-exhibition) course appropriate to a terminal MFA.

master-of-science-in-management-msm-587.name: Master of Science in Management (MSM)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 31 referenced courses form a broad but coherent management curriculum with functional pillars (finance FIN 600-641, information systems IS 631-688, marketing MRKT 620/645, HR HRM 601, project management EM 636/637 and MGMT 641) and a genuine analytics spine that builds FIN 600 -> FIN 616 -> FIN 620 -> MGMT 735 (Deep Learning in Business), giving real depth (deepestPrereqChain=3).
dimensions.suggestion: Define named concentrations (e.g., business analytics, fintech, project management) so students can navigate the wide elective set with a coherent sequence.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=3 with sensible gating (FIN 600->616->620->MGMT 735; MIS 645->648; IS 663->684) and missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) indicate a healthy, well-formed prerequisite graph that is neither under- nor over-constrained.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten a few vague conditions, MGMT 735 lists only 'advanced graduate standing' as an alternative, so a concrete course prerequisite would make the analytics ladder explicit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, so the managerial and analytical competencies the MSM develops are not stated despite a rich course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish MSM outcomes (e.g., data-driven decision-making, functional-area management, and leadership) mapped to the core courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=null leaves the degree total unstated (a master's normally publishes ~30 credits), and with requirementGroups=0 there is no visible core/elective split, so while the 31 uniform 3-credit courses are consistent, the overall credit structure is not transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and separate required core courses from electives so students see how the degree is composed.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present, specific, and readable (e.g., MGMT 735 Deep Learning in Business, DS 644 Introduction to Big Data), but the page is a flat list with no requirement groups, no core/elective labeling, and no stated total, so structure is thin relative to the strong content.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into core and elective/concentration blocks and add a program overview and credit total to make the page navigable.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Currency is excellent: the curriculum surfaces big data (DS 644), deep learning for business (MGMT 735), data mining (MGMT 635), fintech (FIN 611) and financial data analytics (FIN 620), web mining (IS 688), and digital marketing (MRKT 645), squarely reflecting contemporary technology-management practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Stay ahead of the curve by adding a generative-AI/LLM-for-business elective and a data-governance/AI-ethics course to complement the strong analytics offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across the pool (e.g., MGMT 735 details data-mining/ML for business applications; FIN 620 describes Python-based financial data applications), well above thin/placeholder quality.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are mostly clear with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), but several entries carry parsing bleed or embedded prose (IS 631 and IS 684 prereq fields run into descriptive sentences; IS 663 shows a truncated 'Pre or') and MGMT 735's 'advanced graduate standing' is vague, which reduces clarity.

missingTopics.topic: Generative AI and LLM applications for management
missingTopics.rationale: The program has strong ML content (MGMT 735, MGMT 635) but no course on generative-AI tooling now reshaping business workflows.

missingTopics.topic: Data governance, privacy, and AI ethics
missingTopics.rationale: With heavy analytics coverage (DS 644, IS 688, FIN 620), a governance/ethics course is a notable gap for responsible data-driven management.
Beyond IE 659, the core lacks a broad operations-management course, a standard management-master's pillar.
The pool is strong on functional areas and analytics but light on an integrative strategy capstone that ties them together.

emergingTrends.rationale: MGMT 735 and MGMT 635 position students in ML; extending to generative-AI copilots reflects how managers now analyze and act on data.
FIN 611/616/620 already form a fintech-analytics ladder that could be branded as a concentration as the sector grows.
MRKT 645 and IS 688 (web mining) reflect the shift to measurable digital marketing, a differentiator worth deepening.

emergingTrends.trend: AI ethics and responsible data governance
emergingTrends.rationale: As analytics permeates the curriculum, governance and ethics are becoming required managerial competencies the MSM should surface.

topSuggestions: Publish the total credits and a core-versus-elective/concentration structure (currently statedTotalCredits=null and requirementGroups=0).
Define named concentrations (analytics, fintech, project management) to guide students through the 31-course pool.
Add forward-looking electives: generative-AI/LLM for business and data-governance/AI-ethics.
Publish MSM learning outcomes mapped to the core courses.
Clean up a few prerequisite fields (IS 631, IS 663, IS 684, MGMT 735) where prereq text is garbled or vague.

master-of-urban-design-mud-162.name: Master of Urban Design (MUD)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The six referenced courses form a coherent post-professional urban-design set: two design studios (Urban Design Studio I and II), History and Theory of Urban Planning and Design, Public and Private Development, GIS, and Topics of Sustainable Urbanism, spanning design, theory, real-estate economics, spatial tools, and sustainability. The set is well-balanced for a focused MUD; the coherence is undercut only by the two studios not being prerequisite-linked, which hides the intended I-to-II progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Encode Urban Design Studio I as a prerequisite for Studio II and publish a short plan of study so the intended year-long studio arc is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and no course lists a prerequisite, so the graph is entirely flat even though Studio I logically precedes Studio II and Public and Private Development notes 'completion of core sequence' in its description. missingCourseCount is 0, so there are no dangling references, but the absence of any encoded sequencing is a modest structural weakness.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the Studio I to Studio II prerequisite and formalize the 'completion of core sequence' gate on ARCH 651 so the sequencing described in prose is captured in the prerequisite data.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are stated, so the specific urban-design competencies a graduate should demonstrate are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes covering urban-design synthesis, development feasibility, and spatial analysis, mapped to the studios and GIS course.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups were parsed, so the total (a MUD is typically ~30-36 credits, with the two 6-credit studios alone contributing 12) and the core/elective split are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State total credits and show how the two 6-credit studios plus the 3-credit seminars sum to the degree total.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The six courses are described clearly and specifically, and the titles convey a legible urban-design concentration, but the page lacks a plan of study, total credits, and labeled terms.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a compact plan-of-study table with a credit summary to complement the strong, concise course list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is notably current: GIS provides spatial-analysis skills, Public and Private Development covers real-estate finance and feasibility, and Topics of Sustainable Urbanism directly addresses cities' ~70% share of carbon emissions and resource use. This aligns well with contemporary urban-design practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a mobility/transportation-planning and an urban-analytics elective to cover two areas practice increasingly demands.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative, e.g., Sustainable Urbanism opens with concrete data on urban carbon emissions and resource use, and Public and Private Development enumerates needs assessment and real-estate appraisal; none read as placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: All prerequisite fields are null so entries are unambiguous and free of dangling references, but the studios and the 'completion of core sequence' expectation in ARCH 651 are not encoded, so the stated clarity comes at the cost of missing real sequencing.

missingTopics.rationale: A MUD that covers development economics and GIS but no mobility/transit course omits a core determinant of urban form; a transportation-and-urban-form seminar would fill it.
Public and Private Development addresses real-estate economics but not housing policy or affordability, central concerns of contemporary urban design.
Sustainable Urbanism addresses mitigation and resource use, but adaptation to flooding and heat (resilience planning) is a distinct, missing competency.
No course covers participatory process or equitable engagement, essential to legitimate urban-design practice.

missingTopics.topic: Urban analytics and spatial data science
missingTopics.rationale: GIS provides a foundation, but quantitative spatial analysis and data-driven decision-making are increasingly expected beyond introductory GIS.

emergingTrends.rationale: Green/blue infrastructure and resilience are reshaping urban design; the Sustainable Urbanism course is a natural home to expand toward adaptation and nature-based solutions.
City-scale modeling and digital twins are emerging planning instruments that the GIS course could evolve toward.
Proximity-based, mixed-use models are a leading contemporary paradigm the studios should engage.
Spatial-justice frameworks are increasingly central to urban design and would strengthen the theory and studio components.
Public and Private Development can modernize toward analytics-driven feasibility and market modeling now standard in development practice.

topSuggestions: State total credits and a plan of study showing how the two 6-credit studios and 3-credit seminars sum to the degree.
Encode the Studio I to Studio II prerequisite and the ARCH 651 core-sequence gate so real sequencing is captured.
Publish program learning outcomes for urban-design synthesis, development feasibility, and spatial analysis.
Add transportation/mobility and community-engagement electives to close the most conspicuous topical gaps.
Expand the sustainability strand toward climate-resilience and nature-based-solutions content.

mini-mba-597.name: Mini-MBA

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The four courses form a deliberate functional-breadth sampler covering accounting (ACCT 615), finance (FIN 600), information systems (MIS 645), and marketing (MRKT 632), which coheres as a mini-MBA survey for technical professionals. It notably omits a strategy/management and a leadership/organizational-behavior component that anchor most MBA cores, leaving the 'management' core of the credential thin.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a strategic-management or leadership/organizational-behavior course so the functional survey is anchored by an integrative management capstone.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and no course carries a prerequisite, which is appropriate and intentional for an open-entry breadth certificate aimed at professionals from any background; missingCourseCount=0 means no dangling references. The flat graph is a design feature here rather than a defect, though it means there is no sequencing.
dimensions.suggestion: Optionally publish a recommended order (foundations such as ACCT 615/FIN 600 before applied IT/marketing) even though hard prerequisites are unnecessary.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, so the page does not state what management competencies the mini-MBA develops.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes (e.g., interpret financial statements, evaluate a marketing strategy, assess IT for competitive advantage).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a certificate, statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null, but the design is cleanly balanced: exactly four uniform 3.0-credit courses, one per functional area, implying a focused 12-credit credential with all courses required. The only gap is that the total is not stated explicitly.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 12-credit total and that all four courses are required so the credit expectation is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: All four course descriptions are present and readable, and a four-course list is inherently legible, but degree is null with no program-level description, no stated credit total, and no plan grouping to frame the credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief program overview and a 12-credit total to the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The functional content is evergreen and marketable, with a fitting tech tilt for NJIT's engineering-management audience (MIS 645 on IT and competitive advantage, MRKT 632 on marketing for technology-based organizations), but the credential lacks contemporary management topics such as business analytics, digital transformation, and data-driven decision-making.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a course on business analytics for managers or digital transformation/AI in business to modernize the credential.

descriptionQuality.justification: All four descriptions are present, specific, and informative rather than placeholder: ACCT 615 lists concrete accounting topics, MIS 645 defines the information-systems field, and MRKT 632 frames technology marketing challenges.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: No prerequisites appear on any of the four courses, which is unambiguous and internally consistent with zero dangling references (missingCourseCount=0); the open-entry design is deliberate and clear, though it offers no sequencing to assess.

missingTopics.rationale: No dedicated strategy course anchors the functional sampler, leaving the integrative core of an MBA incomplete.
There is no course on managing people, teams, or organizational dynamics, a staple of MBA cores.
The four-course sampler omits operations, one of the core business functions.
MIS 645 covers IT broadly, but a hands-on analytics-for-managers course is absent.
No coverage of ethics, corporate governance, or ESG that a modern MBA-branded credential is expected to include.

emergingTrends.trend: AI and generative AI for business decision-making
emergingTrends.rationale: A mini-MBA for technical professionals should address how LLMs and AI reshape management, complementing MIS 645.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital transformation and platform business models
emergingTrends.rationale: MRKT 632 and MIS 645 could evolve to cover platformization and digital strategy explicitly.
Managers increasingly need analytics fluency; a short analytics module would modernize the credential.

emergingTrends.trend: Sustainability and ESG in business strategy
emergingTrends.rationale: ESG considerations are now central to corporate strategy and finance and warrant a place in the survey.
Technical professionals benefit from modern agile and product-management approaches to running teams and products.

topSuggestions: Add a strategic-management or leadership/organizational-behavior capstone to complete the MBA functional core, the biggest gap for an MBA-branded credential.
Publish 3-5 program-level learning outcomes for the certificate.
State the 12-credit total and the fixed four-course structure explicitly for transparency.
Add a business-analytics or AI-for-managers course to modernize the credential.
Introduce operations/supply-chain and ethics/ESG options to broaden functional coverage.

network-security-and-information-assuran.name: Network Security and Information Assurance

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The certificate coherently pairs network foundations (CS 656 Internet and higher-layer protocols, CS 652 cognitive cloud networking) with a strong information-assurance strand (IS 680 IS auditing, IS 681 computer security auditing, IS 682 forensic auditing, IS 687 transaction mining and fraud detection), with a visible CS 656 to CS 652 progression. It is well-themed for a focused credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit hands-on network-defense course (firewalls/IDS-IPS or penetration testing) so the strong assurance/audit side is balanced by operational network security.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references) and deepestPrereqChain is 2, with sensible chains (CS 656 to CS 652; IS 601 to IS 631; IS 665 to IS 687). The prerequisite graph resolves cleanly within the catalog and is appropriately shallow for a certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface CS 656 as the recommended networking entry point so students sequence the networking and assurance courses sensibly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, a genuine gap for an assurance credential whose graduates need a defined competency set (auditing, forensics, network defense).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as assessing network security posture, conducting IS and forensic audits, and evaluating assurance controls.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null (expected for a certificate) and the 12-course referenced pool is reasonably focused, but no total credits or required-course count is stated, leaving the credential's size unclear.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the number of required versus elective courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The list is clean with no dangling references and specific descriptions (IS 680 audit process, IS 682 forensic evidence handling, CS 652 cloud networking), but there is no program overview or outcomes and the courses are presented as a flat list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an overview clarifying the balance between network security and information assurance and the intended audience (auditors, assurance/forensics analysts).

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program is strong on assurance, audit, and forensics (IS 680/681/682/687) and includes a modern cloud-networking course (CS 652), but its network-security content is audit-centric; hands-on defense (IDS/IPS, firewalls, segmentation), penetration testing, SIEM/SOC operations, and zero-trust are the notable gaps.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an operational network-defense or penetration-testing elective and SIEM/SOC content to complement the audit-heavy assurance strand.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific, describing concrete content (CS 656 top-down TCP/IP protocol analysis; CS 652 cloud-scale networking; IS 680 IS audit process and IT infrastructure controls; IS 682 identification and collection of computer forensic evidence).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0); chains such as CS 652 requiring CS 656, IS 601 into IS 631, and IS 665 into IS 687 are explicit, though a few prereq fields still carry description prose.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 656/CS 652 cover protocols and cloud networking but there is no operational network-defense course.
The assurance strand is audit-oriented (IS 680/681/682) with no adversarial/pentest perspective to validate defenses.

missingTopics.topic: Security operations, SIEM, and detection engineering
missingTopics.rationale: No SOC/SIEM course exists to operationalize the monitoring implied by information assurance.
CS 652 introduces cloud networking, but identity-centric zero-trust and secure architecture are not addressed.
Network security assurance needs applied crypto-protocol coverage that the pool omits.

emergingTrends.rationale: Enterprise network security is moving to zero-trust and secure-access-service-edge models that CS 652/CS 656 should reflect.
CS 652's cloud-networking focus is a natural home for SDN/cloud-native security controls.
IS 687's fraud-analytics techniques extend directly to ML-based network intrusion detection.
Assurance now spans distributed 5G/IoT/edge environments absent from the current pool.
Secure communications assurance must plan for post-quantum migration of TLS/VPN/PKI.

topSuggestions: Add a hands-on network-defense or penetration-testing elective so operational security balances the audit-heavy assurance strand.
Publish program learning outcomes describing the assurance and network-security competencies earned.
State total credits and designate required versus elective courses.
Add SIEM/SOC and zero-trust architecture content to modernize the network-security side.
Surface CS 656 as the recommended networking entry point and clean prerequisite metadata.

neuroscience-303.name: Neuroscience

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The certificate has a strong neuroscience spine that even shows a rare progression for this college - cellular to computational: BIOL 640 Cellular Neurophysiology, BIOL 641 Systems Neuroscience, BIOL 635 to BIOL 636 (Intro then Advanced Computational Neuroscience), plus BME 661 Neural Engineering and imaging (BIOL 645, BME 668). However, of the 14 referenced courses several are off-theme (BIOL 621 Ecology, BIOL 668 Evolutionary Medicine, EVSC 627 Environmental Microbiology, BIOL 672 Computational Systems Biology), which appear pooled from the general graduate catalog and dilute the neuroscience focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Prune the off-theme electives (Ecology, Environmental Microbiology) and add molecular/behavioral neuroscience so the elective pool stays on-topic.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=1 (BIOL 635 to BIOL 636) with missingCourseCount=0 and no dangling references gives a small but real progression, appropriate for a certificate; the remaining courses are entered on graduate standing or instructor permission (e.g., BIOL 635 'Permission by instructor').
dimensions.suggestion: Advertise the BIOL 635 to BIOL 636 sequence as a recommended computational track so students see the intended order.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No certificate-level learning outcomes are stated in the data, so the intended competencies (e.g., cellular, systems, or computational neuroscience proficiency) are not published.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 outcomes spanning cellular (BIOL 640), systems (BIOL 641), and computational (BIOL 635/636) neuroscience skills.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a focused credential the neuroscience courses are uniformly 3 credits and coherent, but statedTotalCredits is null so the completion size is not transparent; consistent with graduate-certificate norms this is a transparency note, not a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and how many of the 14 listed courses a student must complete.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are detailed and method-oriented (BIOL 635 describes modeling of single neurons and small networks; BME 661 covers instrumentation and signal-processing to study brain function), but requirementGroups=0 and no credit total leave the certificate without a compact plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a requirements block that separates a neuroscience core from electives and states the total credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The computational-neuroscience ladder (BIOL 635/636), neural engineering (BME 661), and imaging (BIOL 645, BME 668) reflect current research and industry practice in neurotech and computational brain science, giving the certificate real currency.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brain-computer-interface or neural-data-analysis elective to extend the already-strong neural engineering thread.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and technical (BIOL 636 details modeling and computational analysis of neuronal networks with paper presentations and an independent project; BME 661 describes instrumentation and signal-processing algorithms), with no thin entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The one course-coded prerequisite (BIOL 635 for BIOL 636) is explicit and consistent with missingCourseCount=0, but most gates are prose ('Permission by instructor', 'Graduate student status'), which is clear but informal.

missingTopics.rationale: BIOL 640 covers ion channels and excitability but not molecular neurobiology (synaptic proteins, neurodevelopment); a molecular neuroscience course would deepen the cellular tier.
The certificate is strong on cellular/systems/computational neuroscience but has no behavioral or cognitive course linking neural activity to behavior.
No course addresses how drugs act on the nervous system, a standard neuroscience pillar and a natural complement to BIOL 640/641.
A dedicated neuroanatomy offering is absent; systems content (BIOL 641) would be reinforced by structural grounding.

emergingTrends.rationale: BIOL 636 and BME 661 could incorporate connectome-scale datasets and analysis, now central to systems neuroscience.
The computational track (BIOL 635/636) is well positioned to add modern machine-learning methods for decoding and modeling neural signals.
BME 661 Neural Engineering is the natural anchor to expand toward BCI, a fast-growing neurotech area.
Method courses (BIOL 645 imaging) could reference optogenetic and high-density recording techniques driving current experimental neuroscience.

topSuggestions: Tighten the elective pool by removing off-theme courses (Ecology, Environmental Microbiology, Evolutionary Medicine) and adding molecular/behavioral neuroscience.
State the total credits and required-versus-elective structure of the certificate.
Publish certificate-level learning outcomes across cellular, systems, and computational neuroscience.
Add neuropharmacology and behavioral/cognitive neuroscience to fill standard neuroscience pillars.
Extend the neural-engineering thread (BME 661) toward brain-computer interfaces and deep-learning neural-data analysis.

organismal-and-systems-biology-304.name: Organismal and Systems Biology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The seven referenced courses cover both halves of the certificate's title: organismal biology (BIOL 610 Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy, BIOL 612 Comparative Animal Physiology, BIOL 621 Ecology, BIOL 668 Evolutionary Medicine) and a systems thread (BIOL 641 Systems Neuroscience, BIOL 672 Computational Systems Biology), plus EVSC 627 Environmental Microbiology. The pieces are individually relevant, but the organismal and the computational-systems threads are not strongly integrated, so the set reads as somewhat eclectic rather than a single build.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an integrative capstone or clearly state the through-line (e.g., from organismal physiology to systems-level modeling) so the two threads connect.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=0 and missingCourseCount=0 with no dangling references, which is clean and appropriate for a short certificate; each course is entered on graduate standing or permission (e.g., BIOL 621 'Graduate student status or permission of the instructor', BIOL 672 'Permission by the instructor').
dimensions.suggestion: State any recommended ordering (e.g., organismal courses before Computational Systems Biology) even without hard prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No certificate-level learning outcomes appear in the data, leaving the intended organismal-plus-systems competencies unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes spanning organismal structure/function (BIOL 610/612) and systems-level analysis/modeling (BIOL 672).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a focused credential the seven courses are uniformly 3 credits and reasonably themed, but statedTotalCredits is null so the completion footprint is not transparent; per graduate-certificate norms this is a transparency note rather than a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and how many of the seven courses complete the certificate.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are informative (BIOL 672 describes modeling of chemical, metabolic, and genetic networks with an independent project; BIOL 610 explains vertebrate anatomical evolution in a functional context), but requirementGroups=0 and no stated total leave the certificate without a compact plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short requirements block grouping organismal and systems courses and stating total credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Comparative physiology, ecology, and computational systems biology (BIOL 672) are relevant, but the certificate is light on the genomics/omics data skills that now drive systems biology, limiting its currency for modern research and industry roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a genomics or bioinformatics elective so 'systems biology' includes the high-throughput data methods the field now relies on.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and substantive (BIOL 672 details network modeling and an independent computational project; BIOL 612 explains function from cellular to organism level), with no thin or placeholder entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are prose standing/permission gates ('Graduate student status or permission of the instructor', 'Permission by the instructor') that are internally consistent with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), though informal rather than course-coded.

missingTopics.rationale: BIOL 672 Computational Systems Biology models networks but no course supplies the omics data foundations that feed modern systems models; add a genomics course.
Beyond BIOL 672, there is no dedicated modeling/bioinformatics methods course to build the computational skills 'systems biology' implies.
The organismal courses (BIOL 610/612) and BIOL 668 could be complemented by a comparative-genomics course linking organismal traits to genome-level data.
Comparative physiology (BIOL 612) would be strengthened by explicit training in analyzing physiological/organismal datasets.

emergingTrends.rationale: BIOL 672 network modeling is the natural anchor to integrate genomic, proteomic, and metabolomic layers now standard in systems biology.

emergingTrends.trend: Dynamical and network modeling at scale
emergingTrends.rationale: BIOL 672 could expand toward large-scale dynamical models and simulation, a growing frontier of systems biology.
BIOL 621 Ecology and BIOL 612 physiology could address organismal responses to environmental change, a fast-growing organismal-biology direction.
Applying ML to physiological and network data would modernize both the organismal and systems threads of the certificate.

topSuggestions: Add a genomics/bioinformatics elective so the 'systems biology' half includes modern high-throughput data skills.
State the total credits and required-versus-elective structure of the certificate.
Publish certificate-level learning outcomes bridging organismal and systems biology.
Add an integrative capstone or an explicit through-line connecting organismal physiology to systems-level modeling (BIOL 672).
Modernize BIOL 621/612 toward eco-physiology and climate-change responses.

ph-d-in-applied-physics-379.name: Ph.D. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The referenced courses form a recognizable, well-ordered graduate physics core: PHYS 611 Advanced Classical Mechanics, PHYS 621 Classical Electrodynamics feeding PHYS 721 Classical Electrodynamics II, PHYS 641 Statistical Mechanics, and PHYS 731 Quantum Mechanics II gated on PHYS 631, capped by the PHYS 791 Doctoral Seminar. The I-to-II sequencing in E&M and quantum gives a logical build, though no applied-specialization track is visible on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a visible plan of study that carries the core into applied specialization tracks (condensed matter, photonics, quantum information) plus qualifying-exam and research milestones.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health

missingTopics.rationale: The visible core is entirely mechanics, E&M, statistical, and quantum theory; condensed matter is the backbone of applied physics and no such course is surfaced to complement PHYS 641 and PHYS 731.
Modern doctoral research is simulation-heavy, but no computational-physics course appears among the referenced courses to support the theory core.

An applied doctorate should include graduate experimental-methods/instrumentation training, which is not visible alongside the theory sequence.

emergingTrends.rationale: Quantum computing, sensing, and communication are the fastest-growing applied-physics directions and are a natural extension of the PHYS 631/731 quantum sequence.
Data-driven modeling and ML analysis of experimental and simulation data are now standard; a methods course would modernize the doctoral toolkit.
Engineered optical materials build directly on the classical-electrodynamics core (PHYS 621/721) and are a major research and device frontier.
Topological insulators and 2D quantum materials link the quantum and condensed-matter cores and are a defining current research area for applied physics.

topSuggestions: Publish the doctoral credit requirement and a plan of study (coursework vs research vs dissertation); statedTotalCredits is currently null.

Add a quantum information science course to extend and modernize the PHYS 631/731 quantum sequence.

ph-d-in-business-data-science-589.name: Ph.D in Business Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The program has a clear doctoral research spine (BDS 791 Doctoral Seminar, BDS 792B Pre-Doctoral Research, BDS 725/726 Independent Study I-II, plus dissertation) wrapped around a strong methodological core spanning CS 634 Data Mining, CS 732 Advanced Machine Learning, DS 675 Machine Learning, DS 677 Deep Learning, and ECE 744 Optimization for Data Engineering, cohering well as a business-plus-data-science doctorate. The main weakness is scale: with 87 referenced courses and requirementGroups=0, the required core is not delineated from a very large elective menu spanning CS, DS, ECE, ECON, EM, FIN, and IE.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study naming the required methods core, the business-domain breadth requirement, and the research/dissertation sequence, separating them from the large elective catalog so the doctoral pathway is legible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The deepestPrereqChain of 3 is healthy for a doctorate and shows genuine sequencing (FIN 600 to FIN 616 to FIN 620; CS 634 to CS 732), but missingCourseCount is 7 with dangling references including the dissertation code BDS 790A plus undergraduate codes (CS 280, MATH 211, MATH 333, MGMT 216, MGMT 492) and INTD 799 that do not resolve in the catalog. The undergraduate references are defensible as background, but a dangling dissertation code is a real catalog defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the seven dangling references, especially BDS 790A and INTD 799, and mark clearly which are undergraduate background assumptions versus in-program requirements.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or doctoral competencies are stated in the data, so the research capabilities the Ph.D. certifies (original research, methodological rigor, publication) are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish doctoral program outcomes describing expected competencies in research design, advanced data-science methods, and independent scholarship.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with no parsed plan and creditReconcileDelta uncomputable, so the coursework-versus-dissertation credit balance of a doctoral program (typically 75+ credits with a defined dissertation minimum) is not transparent; note BDS 791 correctly carries 0 credits as a seminar.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total doctoral credit requirement and the split among required coursework, qualifying/pre-doctoral research, and dissertation credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most courses carry rich, specific descriptions, but a few key doctoral entries are thin or empty (BDS 791 Doctoral Seminar has an empty description; ECE 788 Selected Topics is generic), and the absence of a parsed plan of study means the 87-course menu is presented without indicating which courses fulfill which doctoral requirement.
dimensions.suggestion: Fill in the empty/generic descriptions for the doctoral seminar and research courses and group the catalog into required-core versus elective sections.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The methods coverage is at the current data-science frontier: DS 677 Deep Learning, CS 732 Advanced Machine Learning, CS 782 Pattern Recognition, CS 644 Introduction to Big Data, ECE 744 Optimization for Data Engineering, and FIN 616/620 data-driven financial analytics give doctoral students genuinely marketable, contemporary skills paired with business context.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated NLP/large-language-model course and a causal-inference course so the doctorate covers the generative-AI and causal-ML directions now central to business research.

descriptionQuality.justification: The large majority of the 87 courses have detailed, specific descriptions (CS 644 on big-data storage-to-analytics pipelines, ECE 744 on optimization for data engineering, FIN 620 on production data-driven financial applications); only a handful are thin or empty (BDS 791, ECE 788), which slightly lowers an otherwise strong aggregate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit and sensibly conditioned (CS 732 after CS 634 or CS 670; the FIN 600 to 616 to 620 chain), but missingCourseCount=7 means several stated prerequisites and the dissertation reference point to codes absent from the catalog, creating ambiguity about what students must actually complete.

missingTopics.topic: Causal inference and econometrics for research
missingTopics.rationale: The methods core is prediction-heavy (CS 634, DS 675/677, CS 732) but lacks an explicit causal-inference/econometrics course, essential for publishable business-research claims beyond correlation.

missingTopics.topic: Doctoral research methods and scholarly writing
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond FIN 780 (financial-research methods) there is no general research-design and publication course, a core need for a Ph.D. whose value is original scholarship.

missingTopics.topic: Natural language processing and large language models
missingTopics.rationale: Coverage spans classical ML and deep learning but no dedicated NLP/LLM course, despite text and generative models now dominating business data-science research.
CS 644 and ECE 744 touch scale and optimization, but reproducible-research practice and productionizing models are absent, leaving a gap between research prototypes and deployable science.
A data-science doctorate should require training in algorithmic fairness, data privacy, and responsible-AI governance; no such course appears in the referenced set.

emergingTrends.rationale: Extending DS 677 Deep Learning toward transformer architectures and LLM fine-tuning would put the program on the dominant current research trajectory.
Combining the strong ML core with causal methods answers the business-research demand for interventional, decision-relevant conclusions.

emergingTrends.trend: Reproducible and open science / ML systems
emergingTrends.rationale: Doctoral output increasingly requires reproducible pipelines and shared artifacts; a course building on CS 644 would prepare students for this norm.
Data-privacy law and AI regulation are reshaping what business data scientists may build, making governance a research and career necessity.
High-stakes business decisions demand interpretable models; a course on explainability would complement the pattern-recognition and deep-learning offerings (CS 782, DS 677).

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study separating the required methods core, business-domain breadth, and research/dissertation sequence from the 87-course elective menu.
Resolve the seven dangling course references, prioritizing the dissertation code BDS 790A and INTD 799.
Add causal inference/econometrics and a general doctoral research-methods course to balance the prediction-heavy methods core.
Introduce an NLP/large-language-model course and an explainable/responsible-AI course to reach the current data-science frontier.
State total doctoral credits and the coursework-versus-dissertation split, and publish doctoral learning outcomes.

The core is well chosen and internally consistent, but no plan of study was parsed (requirementGroups 0) and no advanced electives, qualifying-exam sequence, or Transport II appear, so progression beyond the first-year core is not legible.

One dangling reference remains (missingCourseCount 1, CHE 790A), almost certainly a doctoral dissertation-research course absent from the parsed catalog.

CHE 791 is correctly shown as a 0-credit seminar, but the overall credit picture is opaque for a degree that typically runs on the order of 75 post-baccalaureate credits.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and its distribution across required core, electives, seminar, and pre-doctoral/dissertation research so the coursework-to-research balance is transparent.

However the page carries no plan of study, no stated total credits, and a dangling CHE 790A, so a reader must infer structure from a short course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study section and a credit summary, and clean the dangling course reference, to complement the strong individual course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects timeless, essential chemical-engineering fundamentals, and CHE 612 notably foregrounds 'small-scale pharmaceutical production,' aligning with New Jersey's pharma corridor. The visible curriculum is nonetheless light on modern computational and data-driven methods, molecular/multiscale simulation, and process-systems or sustainability content that now define frontier chemical-engineering research.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or surface a required or elective modern-methods course (e.g., molecular simulation or machine learning for chemical systems) so the doctoral core signals contemporary computational practice alongside the classical fundamentals.

descriptionQuality.justification: The academic core descriptions are substantive and specific rather than placeholder (CHE 626 details formulating models and deriving analytical and numerical solutions to steady/unsteady problems; CHE 611 covers thermodynamic functions applied to processes). The research/administrative entries (CHE 705, 791, 792) read more as registration rules than content, slightly lowering the aggregate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated but mostly as prose against undergraduate preparation (deepestPrereqChain 0), which is defensible for doctoral admission yet not unambiguous course-code gating; one dangling reference persists (missingCourseCount 1, CHE 790A), and several research courses gate on advisor permission rather than coursework.

missingTopics.rationale: The core stops at CHE 624 Transport Phenomena I and CHE 626 Mathematical Methods with no molecular dynamics, DFT, or multiscale-modeling course, now standard doctoral tooling for chemical-engineering research.

missingTopics.topic: Data science and machine learning for chemical systems
missingTopics.rationale: No course in statistical learning or ML for process/materials data appears, despite the department already offering MTEN 631/633 that a Ph.D. plan could cross-list.
Kinetics and reactor design (CHE 612) are present, but there is no visible optimization, control, or flowsheet-modeling course to connect unit fundamentals to whole-process design.
Only Transport I (CHE 624) is exposed; a doctoral core normally continues to a second transport course covering interphase and turbulent transport in depth.
No electrochemistry, catalysis-for-energy, or decarbonization course appears, topics central to current chemical-engineering research funding and hiring.

emergingTrends.rationale: Physics-informed and data-driven surrogates are reshaping reactor design and transport modeling; CHE 626 Mathematical Methods is the natural home to introduce them.
Chemical-engineering research is pivoting to carbon capture and electrified processes, which the thermodynamics/kinetics core (CHE 611/612) could anchor with an applied elective.
Given CHE 612's explicit pharmaceutical framing and the department's PHEN courses, continuous manufacturing and PAT are a natural doctoral research direction to formalize.
Bayesian-optimization-driven autonomous labs are accelerating catalysis and materials discovery and would strengthen the kinetics/reactor research area.
Chemical-engineering doctoral programs increasingly span cell-based and enzymatic processes; a bioreaction elective would broaden the reactor-design core toward this growth area.

topSuggestions: Publish a doctoral plan of study with total credits, the qualifying-exam milestone, an advanced-elective menu, and the dissertation-research sequence to replace the null statedTotalCredits and empty requirementGroups.
State explicit Ph.D. learning outcomes (research design, disciplinary breadth, scholarly communication) and map them to the core and dissertation milestones.
Resolve the dangling CHE 790A reference and cite specific gateway course codes where possible to make the prerequisite structure explicit.
Add a required or elective modern-methods course (molecular simulation and/or machine learning for chemical systems) to modernize the classical core.
Extend the exposed core to Transport Phenomena II and a process-systems/optimization course so progression beyond the first year is visible.

dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and the split between required/elective coursework and dissertation-research credits.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are detailed and research-oriented across chemistry, materials, and bioengineering (e.g., BME 653 micro/nanotechnologies for interfacing live cells, MTSE 724 transport of electrons and phonons); the set is largely free of placeholder text.

missingTopics.rationale: Beyond CHEM 737 modeling, no course covers ML for property/reaction prediction or cheminformatics, now a core doctoral research skill.

dimensions.suggestion: Organize the page into research areas/tracks with a compact required core, and remove the flat cross-departmental course dump.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool does contain modern, relevant content, CS 675 Machine Learning, FIN 611 FinTech, FIN 616/620 financial data analytics, and ACCT 640 big-data analytics, but it is scattered and framed around business/finance rather than engineering research thrusts, so currency exists but is not organized toward the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate the modern analytics/ML courses into a defined computational engineering-science methods track tied to named research areas.

descriptionQuality.justification: Where descriptions are present they are substantive (e.g., FIN 644 details credit-risk measurement and derivative pricing; CS 675 outlines theory and practice of machine learning), though a few entries are empty or show parsing bleed (BDS 791 has no description; BDS 726's excerpt runs into 'BDS 790A. Doctoral Diss').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Many prerequisites are explicit and well-formed (the FIN 641->644 chain, ACCT 615->640), but missingCourseCount=7 indicates dangling references and several prereq fields carry embedded prose or undergraduate-level codes, reducing overall clarity.

missingTopics.rationale: The doctorate lacks a visible discipline-specific core; a shared advanced-methods/modeling requirement would give the interdisciplinary pool an engineering identity distinct from the business courses shown.

missingTopics.topic: Advanced statistics and experimental design for research
missingTopics.rationale: Although MATH 605 (Stochastic Calculus) and finance analytics appear, a doctoral research-statistics/experimental-design requirement is not evident in the pool.

missingTopics.topic: Responsible conduct of research and scientific writing
missingTopics.rationale: No RCR or grant/scientific-writing component is visible, both are expected doctoral milestones.
For an engineering-science doctorate, formal training in scientific computing and HPC would better anchor the ML/analytics electives already present (CS 675, FIN 620).

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 675, ACCT 640, and FIN 620 already bring ML into the pool; organizing them into a computational research track positions doctoral students at the AI-for-engineering frontier.
The heavy analytics presence (big data, financial data analytics) should be paired with data-management and reproducibility practices now required by funders.

emergingTrends.trend: Convergence research across engineering and business analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: The unusual finance/engineering breadth could become a genuine strength if framed as convergence research rather than an unstructured catalog.
FIN 611/616/642-644 form a strong quantitative-finance spine that a well-defined engineering-science track could formalize as a computational-finance concentration.

topSuggestions: Correct the stated total credits (currently 3.0) and publish the coursework-versus-dissertation split.
Publish an engineering-science-specific plan of study so the surfaced course pool matches the degree rather than an aggregated business catalog.

Organize the ~99-course pool into research tracks with a compact required core and breadth requirements.
Publish Ph.D.-level learning outcomes and add explicit research-methods, RCR, and scientific-writing milestones.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity

dimensions.suggestion: Add a climate-science/modeling course and an environmental-data-science/geospatial-analytics elective to modernize the quantitative research toolkit.

descriptionQuality.justification: Where present, descriptions are specific and research-relevant (EVSC 711 advanced environmental analysis, EVSC 712 hazardous-substance management), but several referenced courses (EVSC 622, ENE 630, ENE 661) have empty description fields, weakening overall completeness.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite links that exist are clear and sensibly gated (ENE 663 to 664, EVSC 612 to 711), but the single dangling reference (EVSC 790A, missingCourseCount 1) and a cross-listed prerequisite style slightly reduce internal consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: EVSC 615 addresses global problems descriptively, but no course delivers quantitative climate or earth-system modeling expected of a doctoral program today.

missingTopics.topic: Environmental data science and geospatial ML
missingTopics.rationale: The program lacks a computational/geospatial-analytics course; doctoral environmental research increasingly depends on remote sensing, spatial statistics, and machine learning.
The toxicology, analysis, and treatment courses predate the PFAS/microplastics research agenda now central to the field and to funding calls.
The list is chemistry- and engineering-weighted with little ecological-systems or biodiversity research content for a comprehensive environmental doctorate.
Water, soil, and waste are well covered, but atmospheric chemistry and air-quality modeling are largely absent from the elective set.

emergingTrends.trend: Climate resilience, adaptation, and decarbonization research
emergingTrends.rationale: Funding and policy priorities are shifting toward adaptation and net-zero pathways that EPS 622, ENE 673, and EVSC 715 could anchor into a research strand.

emergingTrends.trend: PFAS and emerging-contaminant fate and remediation
emergingTrends.rationale: PFAS chemistry and treatment is a rapidly funded frontier that EVSC 622 bioremediation and the ENE treatment courses could be extended to address.

emergingTrends.trend: Environmental sensing networks and machine learning
emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor-plus-ML monitoring is transforming environmental measurement, a data-driven direction the analytical strand (EVSC 711) should incorporate.
ENE 673's life-cycle framing positions the program to formalize materials-recovery and circular-economy research.
Distributional and equity dimensions are increasingly central to environmental scholarship, extending the EPS policy cluster.

topSuggestions: Resolve the dangling EVSC 790A reference and fill the empty course descriptions (EVSC 622, ENE 630, ENE 661) to complete the entry.

Publish 3-5 doctoral learning outcomes describing original-research, interdisciplinary-breadth, and communication competencies.

ph-d-in-materials-science-and-engineerin.name: Ph.D. in Materials Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The page references 659 courses with requirementGroups=0 and no visible MTSE core; the sampled courses are entirely biomedical-engineering (BME 650 Clinical Physiology, BME 651 Tissue Engineering, BME 671 Biomechanics, BME 678/679 Orthopedic Implants), so the materials-science identity and any core-to-research progression are not discernible. The page reads as an undifferentiated cross-listed elective pool rather than a curated doctoral curriculum.
dimensions.suggestion: Define an MSE core (thermodynamics & kinetics, structure & characterization, computational materials) and curate the electives into named tracks instead of listing the entire graduate catalog.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph has genuine depth (deepestPrereqChain=5, e.g., the BME 677 -> BME 678 -> BME 679 orthopedic-implant chain), which is healthy, but missingCourseCount=108 (BIOL 201, BME 111/301/302/303, etc.) is very high; most are undergraduate prerequisites referenced by graduate courses and thus expected to dangle, yet the count also signals uncontrolled scope.
dimensions.suggestion: Prune the referenced set to the program's actual courses so the 108 dangling references drop, and confirm that legitimate undergraduate prerequisites resolve.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or doctoral competencies appear in the data; despite the enormous course list, the page states no outcomes, which is a real gap for a Ph.D. catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish Ph.D. learning outcomes and milestones (qualifying exam, candidacy, original-research and dissertation competencies).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=3.0 is implausible for a doctorate (a Ph.D. is typically 60+ post-bachelor credits), pointing to a data-entry/parse error, and with requirementGroups=0 and creditReconcileDelta=null there is no coursework/research/dissertation distribution -- a clear credit-transparency defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the total credit requirement (3.0 is clearly wrong) and publish the coursework, research, and dissertation credit split.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page lists 659 courses dominated by unrelated biomedical courses, shows statedTotalCredits=3.0, provides no plan of study, and surfaces no MTSE-coded core, so a reader cannot see what the materials-science doctorate actually requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Restructure the page around a concise core plus curated electives, fix the credit total, and surface the program's own MTSE courses.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The sprawling elective pool does span industry-relevant applied areas -- biomaterials (BME 672), tissue engineering (BME 651/652), medical imaging (BME 668), BioMEMS (BME 680), and medical-device development (BME 684) -- giving real applied breadth, but nothing is curated toward materials, so relevance is incidental rather than intentional.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a materials-for-industry track (energy materials, electronic/semiconductor materials, additive manufacturing) so the doctorate's industry relevance is legible.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled courses carry substantive, specific descriptions -- BME 651 explains scaffold-based tissue fabrication, BME 668 details medical-imaging physics and reconstruction, BME 676 covers computational-mechanics software workflows -- so description quality where present is strong and non-placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Individual prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently (BME 650: 'BME 111, BME 303, BME 382 or permission'; BME 676: 'BME 670 or equivalent'; the BME 677/678/679 chain), but the program-wide missingCourseCount=108 means many referenced prerequisites point to courses absent from the catalog, so overall clarity is undercut by unresolved references.

missingTopics.rationale: No MTSE core course is surfaced amid 659 references; phase equilibria, diffusion, and transformations are the foundation of a materials-science doctorate and should anchor the required core.
Electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, and spectroscopy are essential doctoral research skills, yet the sample skews entirely biomedical with no dedicated materials-characterization course visible.

DFT, molecular dynamics, and phase-field modeling are central to modern materials research; the biomedical-heavy sample shows computational biomechanics (BME 676) but no computational-materials course.

missingTopics.topic: Structure-property relationships in metals, ceramics, and semiconductors
missingTopics.rationale: Classical materials classes (metals, ceramics, electronic materials) are absent from the sample, leaving the doctorate's materials identity unrepresented behind a biomedical elective pool.

emergingTrends.trend: Materials informatics and machine learning for discovery
emergingTrends.rationale: The Materials Genome Initiative has made data-driven materials design central; an informatics/ML course would give the doctorate a modern, materials-specific research thrust.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing of metals, ceramics, and polymers
emergingTrends.rationale: Additive manufacturing is reshaping processing-structure-property research and could build on the program's existing biomaterials and device courses.
Solid-state batteries, photovoltaics, and catalysis materials are major R&D growth areas absent from the current biomedical-dominated list.
Graphene, transition-metal dichalcogenides, and topological materials are a research frontier a physics-housed MSE doctorate is well positioned to lead.

Curate electives into named tracks and prune the 108 dangling references so the page reflects the actual program.
Publish Ph.D. learning outcomes and milestones.
Add materials-frontier electives (materials informatics/ML, additive manufacturing, energy materials) to modernize the curriculum.

ph-d-in-mathematical-sciences-359.name: Ph.D. in Mathematical Sciences

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 20 referenced courses span three coherent doctoral tracks that build with real depth (deepestPrereqChain 3): a pure-analysis spine (MATH 645 Analysis I to MATH 745 Analysis II and MATH 768 measure-theoretic Probability Theory, plus MATH 656 Complex Variables), an applied-math track (MATH 613 Modeling to MATH 689 Advanced Applied Math II: ODEs, with MATH 614 Numerical Methods I and MATH 651), and a full statistics sequence (MATH 661/662 to MATH 665 Inference, feeding MATH 644 Regression, MATH 698 Sampling, MATH 699 Design of Experiments). Progression is well ordered; the main weakness is that no plan-of-study groups were parsed (requirementGroups 0), so qualifying-exam structure and the coursework/research split are not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit plan of study that groups courses into breadth areas (analysis, applied mathematics, statistics) and shows the qualifying-exam, coursework, and dissertation-research stages.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy and reaches a sensible doctoral depth of 3 (e.g., MATH 546 to MATH 645 to MATH 745/768 and to MATH 656), with statistics gated cleanly on MATH 662 to MATH 665 to the methods courses. Only one dangling reference exists (missingCourseCount 1, MATH 790A), and it is a dissertation-research registration course rather than a substantive gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Add MATH 790A (dissertation research) to the catalog so the last dangling reference resolves, and confirm each research course (725, 792B) is listed.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or doctoral educational objectives appear in the data; for a Ph.D. the expected competencies (breadth mastery, qualifying-exam standards, original-research capacity) are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish doctoral learning outcomes covering breadth-area mastery, qualifying/candidacy expectations, and dissertation research and teaching competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are both null and no plan groups were parsed, so the coursework-versus-research credit split is not visible; for a Ph.D. this total is inherently research-dominated and less fixed than for a taught degree, so the opacity is a transparency note rather than a hard defect. The course mix (coursework plus MATH 725 Independent Study, 791 Seminar, 792B Pre-Doctoral Research) implies a conventional coursework-then-research balance.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total post-baccalaureate credit requirement and the minimum coursework versus dissertation-research credits so prospective students can see the balance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for all sampled courses and titles clearly signal the analysis, applied, and statistics tracks, but the page shows no plan of study, no stated credit total, and no labeled stages, so the reader must infer the doctoral structure from prerequisites alone.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study table with labeled breadth areas and stages plus a credit summary to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects strong contemporary computational and data-oriented mathematics: numerical methods (MATH 614), modeling (MATH 613), and a deep applied-statistics stack (regression, design of experiments, sampling, statistical inference, measure-theoretic probability). It is comparatively light on optimization, machine/statistical learning, and applied stochastic processes, which are now central to both academic and industrial mathematical-sciences research.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or require a graduate course in optimization and one in statistical/machine learning so doctoral students match the computational-data frontier employers and funders now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific, articulating each course's scope (e.g., MATH 768 as a measure-theoretic axiomatic probability course, MATH 614 emphasizing accuracy and rigor in scientific computation) rather than placeholder text; excerpts are truncated but clearly full entries exist.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible sequencing and departmental-approval alternatives (e.g., MATH 665 requires MATH 662; MATH 745 requires MATH 645); only MATH 790A dangles and a few gates read 'or departmental approval' rather than a hard course.

missingTopics.rationale: The applied and numerical track (MATH 613/614/651/689) has no dedicated optimization course, yet optimization underpins modern applied mathematics, operations research, and machine learning.

Despite a strong classical-statistics stack (MATH 644/665/698/699), no course covers high-dimensional or statistical learning, a core competency for contemporary mathematical-sciences Ph.D. graduates.
MATH 768 is measure-theoretic probability but there is no applied stochastic-processes, Markov-chain, or stochastic-differential-equations course to support modeling and simulation.
MATH 689 covers boundary-value ODEs but no dedicated partial-differential-equations or finite-element/numerical-PDE course appears, a gap for an applied-mathematics doctorate.
The statistics track stops at frequentist regression/inference; MCMC, bootstrap, and Bayesian computation are absent and are now standard doctoral tools.

emergingTrends.trend: Scientific machine learning and physics-informed models
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven scientific computing (e.g., physics-informed neural networks) is reshaping applied mathematics; the MATH 613/614/689 sequence could add a scientific-ML component.
UQ links numerical methods and probability and is central to modern modeling; it would naturally extend MATH 614 and the probability courses.
Geometry- and topology-driven data methods are a fast-growing research frontier that the analysis and statistics tracks are well positioned to support.
MCMC and probabilistic modeling now dominate applied statistical research; adding them modernizes the MATH 662/665 sequence.
Large-scale simulation increasingly relies on parallel/GPU computing, an extension the numerical-methods track (MATH 614) should reflect.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study with breadth areas, qualifying-exam structure, and the coursework-versus-dissertation credit split, replacing the null statedTotalCredits.
State doctoral learning outcomes (breadth mastery, candidacy standards, original-research and teaching competencies).
Add graduate optimization and statistical/machine-learning courses to close the biggest currency gap versus the research frontier.
Introduce applied stochastic processes/SDEs and a numerical/advanced PDE course to round out the applied-mathematics track.
Resolve the dangling MATH 790A dissertation-research reference in the catalog.

ph-d-in-transportation-engineering-489.name: Ph.D. in Transportation Engineering

ph-d-in-urban-systems-173.name: Ph.D. in Urban Systems

However, the only parsed courses are research/independent-study placeholders; the substantive coursework core (theory seminars, research methods, urban-systems electives) that an interdisciplinary Urban Systems Ph.D. should require is not documented, so intellectual progression through coursework is invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required doctoral coursework, core seminars, research-methods sequence, and a designated concentration set, so the page shows more than research-credit placeholders.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: A flat prerequisite graph (deepestPrereqChain 0) is appropriate for a doctoral program gated by advisor permission and milestones rather than course chains, and the gates shown ('Permission of Program Director', proposal defense for USYS 790) are sensible. The one dangling reference (INTD 799, missingCourseCount 1) is a minor blemish, likely an interdisciplinary research course record that is not linked.

Still, the coursework portion of the 51 credits is not broken out, so a student cannot see how much is seminars/methods versus research.
dimensions.suggestion: Itemize the 51 credits into required coursework, elective/concentration credit, and dissertation research so the large reconcile gap is explained on the page.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity

There is no visible statement of coursework, qualifying exams, or dissertation timeline, so the page reads as a set of research placeholders.
dimensions.suggestion: Restructure the page into clear sections, coursework, research milestones, and dissertation, with a legible plan and non-boilerplate descriptions of the actual seminars.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Urban Systems is a highly relevant interdisciplinary field, and the college's ecosystem includes strongly current offerings (GIS, sustainable urbanism, the 'Augmented City'), but this program's own parsed content is limited to generic research courses that reveal nothing about methods currency such as spatial data science or urban informatics.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the concentration areas and their signature courses (e.g., urban informatics, environmental systems, health, transportation) so the doctorate's current research strengths are visible.

descriptionQuality.justification: The parsed course descriptions are administrative boilerplate ('This designation covers courses for Urban Systems students prescribed by a supervising faculty member'), which is inherent to independent-study and dissertation credits but leaves the page without informative descriptions of any substantive doctoral coursework.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clear in kind ('Permission of Program Director', and proposal defense for dissertation research) and appropriate for a doctorate, though non-specific, and one reference (INTD 799) is dangling, slightly reducing consistency.

emergingTrends.rationale: Large-scale mobility, imagery, and administrative data now drive urban inquiry, requiring dedicated computational and geospatial training.

emergingTrends.rationale: Machine-learning models for land use, mobility, and risk prediction are a fast-growing methodological frontier the program should position for.

pharmaceutical-management-458.name: Pharmaceutical Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight referenced courses coherently blend management method (EM 636 Project Management, EM 637 Project Control, IE 673 Total Quality Management) with a pharmaceutical-engineering core (PHEN 601 Principles, PHEN 604 Validation and Regulatory Issues, PHEN 605 Packaging Technology, PHEN 618 Pharmacokinetics and Drug Delivery), rounded out by PHEN 725 Independent Study. For a management-focused certificate this pairing of project/quality management with domain fundamentals is well conceived, though the required subset is not shown (requirementGroups 0).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish which courses are required versus elective (e.g., a management pair plus a pharma-domain pair) so the intended management-plus-domain balance is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean and appropriately shallow for a certificate: missingCourseCount is 0 and the deepest chain is 1 (EM 637 requires EM 636; PHEN 605 requires PHEN 601 and PHEN 603). No dangling references appear, and the management gateway courses (EM 636, IE 673, PHEN 601) sensibly carry no prerequisite.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface PHEN 603 in the course list (it is referenced as a PHEN 605 prerequisite but not shown) so the packaging pathway's dependency is visible to students.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are stated, so a prospective student cannot see what managerial and regulatory competencies the certificate certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 outcomes tied to concrete capabilities such as managing pharma projects to schedule/budget, applying quality systems, and navigating FDA regulatory processes, mapped to EM 636/637, IE 673, and PHEN 604.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a graduate certificate the null statedTotalCredits and null creditReconcileDelta are a transparency note rather than a defect; all constituent courses are 3 credits, implying a typical 12-credit/four-course credential, but the total is not stated. The management-versus-domain mix is well-focused for the credential's scope.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the exact required-course count (e.g., 12 credits / four courses) and clarify how many must come from the management versus pharma-domain groups.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and specific (e.g., PHEN 604 develops 'a working knowledge of the Federal Code of Regulations and its impact on the pharmaceutical and allied industries'; EM 637 covers work-breakdown, budgetary control, and earned-value analysis). The page lacks a stated total-credit figure and an explicit required-course list expected on a certificate page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a credential summary block (total credits, required courses, target audience) above the course list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The certificate is strongly aligned with pharma-industry practice: project management, total quality management, and validation/regulatory compliance (PHEN 604) are exactly the operational competencies employers value in New Jersey's pharmaceutical corridor, and PHEN 618 grounds managers in drug-delivery science. It is comparatively light on supply-chain, pharmacoeconomics, and digital/analytics management content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a pharmaceutical supply-chain or operations-analytics elective so the management track reflects the data-driven, globally distributed nature of modern pharma operations.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific rather than placeholder: EM 637 enumerates work-breakdown construction, budgetary control, and earned-value analysis, and PHEN 601 outlines drug discovery/development, FDA processes, and dosage forms. Full entries clearly exist for all eight courses despite truncated excerpts.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0): EM 637 requires EM 636 or MGMT 641 and PHEN 605 requires PHEN 601 and PHEN 603, while gateway courses correctly carry none. Only the reference to PHEN 603 without listing it slightly reduces completeness.

missingTopics.rationale: The certificate covers project, quality, and regulatory management but no course addresses global pharma supply chains, cold-chain logistics, or serialization/track-and-trace, a core management competency.
Management of pharmaceutical products increasingly hinges on pricing, value-based reimbursement, and market access, none of which appear in the current course set.
PHEN 604 covers U.S. CFR fundamentals, but there is no course on ICH harmonization, global submissions, or post-market surveillance that pharma managers need.

missingTopics.topic: Quality by Design and risk management (ICH Q8-Q10)
missingTopics.rationale: IE 673 teaches general TQM, but modern pharma quality management is organized around QbD and formal risk management, which are not explicitly represented.

missingTopics.topic: Pharmaceutical operations analytics and data-driven decision-making
missingTopics.rationale: There is no course on analytics for manufacturing/quality metrics, leaving a gap between management theory and the data pharma operations now generate.
