descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across all six courses (e.g., TRAN 625 transit vehicle/right-of-way characteristics and capacity), with no thin or placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly and are internally consistent, TRAN 640's TRAN 650/EM 602 requirement is satisfiable within the menu, with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0).

missingTopics.rationale: TRAN 603 introduces network analysis and assignment conceptually, but no course teaches four-step or activity-based demand modeling software used in practice.

missingTopics.topic: GIS and spatial analysis in transportation
missingTopics.rationale: Planning and transit courses would benefit from spatial-data skills, absent from the menu.

missingTopics.topic: Intelligent transportation systems and smart mobility
missingTopics.rationale: TRAN 752 traffic control is classical; connected/real-time operations and MaaS are not represented, sitting instead in a separate certificate.
No dedicated safety/Vision Zero course appears despite its prominence in current planning.
TRAN 610 economics is present but sustainable and equitable mobility planning is not addressed.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven travel behavior and big data
emergingTrends.rationale: Mobility data (GPS, transit smartcards) is transforming planning, extending TRAN 603's survey and assignment methods.
Transit and operations (TRAN 625) are being reshaped by EVs and shared modes the certificate could incorporate.
Transportation economics (TRAN 610) is broadening to include equity and decarbonization goals.
Traffic control (TRAN 752) and planning increasingly merge with real-time ITS operations.
TRAN 640 distribution logistics anticipates data-driven urban-freight and last-mile optimization.

topSuggestions: Add a travel-demand-modeling/GIS elective to give the certificate a modern quantitative skill.
Publish learning outcomes and state total credits / course count.
Introduce sustainability-and-equity and safety-analysis content to reflect current practice.
Add a certificate overview and recommend TRAN 603/TRAN 650 as the foundational course.
Cross-reference the ITS certificate so students can extend into smart-mobility topics.

ui-ux-digital-design-essentials-181.name: UI/UX Digital Design Essentials

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The parsed course list is incoherent with the stated credential: for a UI/UX essentials certificate the 174 referenced courses and every sampled course are bioinformatics (BNFO 601-726) and computer-science systems/security/theory courses (CS 608 Cryptography, CS 630 Operating System Design, CS 643 Cloud Computing, CS 647 Counter Hacking, CS 650 Computer Architecture). No UI, UX, interaction-design, or visual-design course appears at all, indicating the catalog page aggregated an unrelated program-wide course dump rather than the certificate's actual curriculum.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the entry with the genuine UI/UX course list (the DD 640 / IS 661 / DD 634 UX courses that exist elsewhere in this catalog) and remove the unrelated bioinformatics and CS systems courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 3, but missingCourseCount is 15 with dangling references to undergraduate CS and program courses (CS 114, CS 241, CS 280, CS 332, CS 350, CS 435, MATH 333, YWCC 590-592, several 790-level research courses); more fundamentally the prerequisite graph that exists belongs to a CS curriculum, not a UI/UX certificate, so its structure is not meaningful for this credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the correct UI/UX courses are loaded, rebuild the prerequisite graph from those courses and resolve the 15 dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present, and because the catalog data does not surface the actual UI/UX curriculum, a reader cannot even infer the intended competencies from the courses shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 UI/UX learning outcomes (user research, wireframing/prototyping, usability testing, accessible visual design) alongside the corrected course list.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 3.0, which is implausible for a certificate (essentials certificates are typically ~12 credits / 4 courses), while 174 courses are referenced and summedTermCredits is null; the stated total, the reference count, and the credential type are mutually inconsistent, so credit transparency is effectively broken.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the total to the real requirement (e.g., 12 credits / 4 courses) and scope the referenced courses to only those in the certificate.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page cannot orient a prospective student: it lists 174 unrelated graduate courses spanning bioinformatics, cryptography, cloud computing, and computer architecture instead of any UI/UX content, states only 3 total credits, and includes empty descriptions (e.g., BNFO 698 'ST:' with a blank excerpt). This is a broken entry rather than a thin one.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the aggregated course dump with a curated UI/UX plan of study, correct credit total, and a short credential overview.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Relevance to the stated UI/UX field cannot be demonstrated because no UI/UX course is surfaced; the courses that do appear (CS 634 Data Mining, CS 643 Cloud Computing, CS 645 Security) are individually current CS topics but irrelevant to a digital-design certificate, so the entry fails to evidence currency in its own discipline.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the certificate with current UI/UX practice courses (design systems, Figma prototyping, accessibility) so its industry relevance can actually be assessed.

descriptionQuality.justification: Among the sampled (wrong) courses many CS descriptions are actually substantive, but several are empty or placeholder (BNFO 698 'ST:' has a blank excerpt; BNFO 700B/701 are boilerplate project/thesis stubs), and critically none describes UI/UX content, so description quality for the stated certificate cannot be established.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite fields are unreliable: 15 dangling references exist, and some prerequisiteRaw values are malformed description fragments rather than prerequisites (e.g., CS 602 lists 'advanced Web-based programming with an emphasis on the Java language and platform' as its prerequisite), so prerequisites are neither unambiguous nor internally consistent.

missingTopics.rationale: A UI/UX essentials certificate must cover interaction principles and usability heuristics, yet no such course is surfaced in the parsed data.

missingTopics.topic: User research and usability testing methods
missingTopics.rationale: Core UX practice (interviews, personas, moderated/unmoderated usability testing) is absent from what the catalog shows, though UX courses like IS 661 exist elsewhere in this catalog.
Hands-on prototyping with industry tools is essential to a UI/UX credential and is not represented among the listed courses.
Accessible design is now a baseline professional and legal expectation for UI/UX work and should be an explicit topic.
UI fundamentals (grids, type, color, component/design systems) underpin the 'UI' half of the certificate and are missing from the surfaced content.

emergingTrends.rationale: Generative tools for ideation, copy, and layout (including AI features inside design tools) are reshaping UX workflows and should be introduced in an essentials certificate.
Scalable component libraries and design-to-code handoff are now standard practice that a modern UI/UX curriculum should teach.
Regulatory and ethical pressure is pushing accessibility from optional to foundational across UI/UX practice.
Immersive interfaces are a growth area the program could connect to XR design work already present elsewhere in this catalog (e.g., DD 650).
A/B testing, product analytics, and behavioral metrics increasingly guide UX decisions and would modernize the certificate.

topSuggestions: Fix the catalog data: the certificate currently lists 174 unrelated bioinformatics/CS courses and only 3 total credits; replace with the actual UI/UX course list (e.g., DD 640, IS 661, DD 634) and correct the credit total to ~12.
Resolve the 15 dangling course references and remove malformed prerequisite fields (e.g., CS 602's description-as-prerequisite) once the correct courses are loaded.
Publish 3-5 UI/UX learning outcomes covering user research, prototyping, usability testing, and accessible visual design.
Add explicit accessibility (WCAG) and design-systems/Figma prototyping content, baseline expectations the current entry does not show.
Add a plan-of-study block with required/elective structure so the credential is legible to prospective students and employers.

web-systems-development-245.name: Web Systems Development

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A coherent web spine exists with real depth (IS 601 Web Systems Development feeds IS 690 Web Services and Middleware, IS 634 Information Retrieval, and IS 631; IS 665 feeds IS 688 Web Mining), but the referenced pool balloons to 36 courses dominated by an unrelated data-science/AI suite (DS 669 Reinforcement Learning, DS 680 NLP, CS 677 Deep Learning) and doctoral research courses (DS 725/726, DS 792B, CS 726, DS 700B/701B thesis). This scope drift badly undermines coherence for a web-development certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Prune to a focused web-development pathway (IS 601, IS 690, IS 634, IS 688, CS 602 plus a modern front-end/full-stack course) and remove the unrelated data-science, AI, and doctoral-research courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 2 with sensible web chains (IS 601 to IS 690/IS 634; IS 665 to IS 688), but missingCourseCount is 5 and the danglers (CS 280, MATH 333, DS 790, DS 790A, INTD 799) are undergraduate prerequisites and doctoral research courses that should not be part of a web certificate at all; the danglers are a symptom of the over-broad course inclusion.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the doctoral research references (DS 790, DS 790A, INTD 799) and the undergraduate prerequisite codes so the certificate's graph reflects only its web-development courses.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, a real gap for a certificate whose graduates should be able to demonstrate specific web-development competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as building web systems, developing web services/middleware, and applying information retrieval and web mining.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null (expected for a certificate), but with a 36-course referenced pool and no stated total or required count, the credential is both opaque and far too broad to read as a focused ~12-credit certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits (e.g., 12) and restrict the listing to a coherent four-course web-development pathway.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page mixes 36 courses spanning web development, the full data-science/AI curriculum, and doctoral thesis/research placeholders, with five dangling references and no overview or outcomes; the mismatch between the certificate name and its content is a real presentation defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Rewrite the page to list only web-systems courses with a short overview, and remove the data-science, AI, and doctoral entries that do not belong.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuinely web-focused courses lean dated: IS 601 (open-source web development), IS 690 (web services/middleware/SOA), IS 634 (information retrieval), and IS 688 (web mining) reflect a 2000s-era stack, while the modern web stack (JavaScript/TypeScript SPA frameworks, Node.js, REST/GraphQL APIs, cloud/serverless deployment, containers, and web security) is absent; the bundled ML/AI courses are current but off-topic.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a modern full-stack course (JS/TS frameworks, REST/GraphQL, cloud deployment) and a web-security course (OWASP Top 10, OAuth) to replace the SOA-era emphasis.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled courses have present and specific descriptions (IS 690 middleware/interoperability; IS 634 crawling/indexing/vector-space IR; IS 688 web-hyperlink and usage mining), but several bundled research and independent-study entries are thin placeholders (DS 786 'Introduction to selected topics in data science'; DS 700B/701B/725/726 restriction-only stubs) that drag the aggregate down.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Many prerequisites are clear (IS 601 into IS 690/IS 634; DS 677 requires DS 675 or CS 675), but five references dangle, some prereq fields carry description prose (CS 602 and IS 601 'NONE Students will...'), and the out-of-scope courses muddy the graph's consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: IS 601 teaches open-source web development but no current client-side framework stack, the core of contemporary web development.
IS 690 covers SOA-era web services but not modern API-first, Node/REST/GraphQL development.
No course covers deploying web systems to the cloud, now a baseline professional skill.
Building web systems (IS 601) without an application-security course leaves a critical gap.

missingTopics.topic: Responsive, progressive, and accessible web apps
missingTopics.rationale: Modern front-end practice (responsive design, PWAs, accessibility) is absent from the web spine.

emergingTrends.rationale: Web systems increasingly deploy to serverless/edge platforms (Vercel, Cloudflare), which IS 601/IS 690 should incorporate.

emergingTrends.trend: AI-assisted and LLM-integrated web apps (RAG, chat UIs)
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 634 (information retrieval) and IS 688 (web mining) are natural bridges to retrieval-augmented, LLM-powered web applications.
Decoupled front-end/back-end architectures are now standard and should modernize IS 690's middleware focus.
Near-native browser performance via WebAssembly is expanding the web platform in ways the current courses ignore.
Interactive real-time web applications require event-driven patterns absent from the SOA-era curriculum.

topSuggestions: Prune the 36-course referenced pool to a coherent four-course web-development pathway and remove the unrelated data-science, AI, and doctoral entries.
Modernize the curriculum with a full-stack JS/TS plus cloud-deployment course and a web-security (OWASP) course.
State total credits and the required courses so the certificate reads as a focused credential.
Publish program learning outcomes describing the web-development competencies earned.
Remove the dangling doctoral and undergraduate course references so the prerequisite graph reflects only the certificate's courses.

wind-power-management-535.name: Wind Power Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The six courses form a coherent management-of-wind concentration: ECE 654 (US offshore renewable energy policy), ECE 670 (management strategies in the offshore wind industry), and ECE 671 (wind plant project development) cover the policy/business/project life-cycle spine, while CS 644 (big data) and MGMT 635 (data mining and analysis) supply analytics and ECE 725 offers an applied independent study. The set reads as a purpose-built managerial counterpart to the technical O&M certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Recommend a sequence that places policy/development courses (ECE 654/671) before ECE 670 management strategies so students understand the regulatory and project context before the strategy synthesis.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 with missingCourseCount=0, so there are no dangling references - appropriate for a short certificate of standalone electives. Only CS 644 states a prerequisite ('permission of the instructor'), while the ECE management courses list none, which is reasonable for policy/management content.
dimensions.suggestion: Confirm whether CS 644's 'permission of the instructor' implies a programming/data background and, if so, state it so non-technical management students can gauge fit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear, so the managerial competencies the certificate develops (e.g., navigating offshore permitting, structuring wind-project finance) are not stated for prospective students or employers.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 outcomes covering wind-project development, regulatory/permitting fluency, and data-informed asset management.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, so the total is not transparent, but six 3-credit courses imply a standard 12-credit / four-course certificate with modest choice, an appropriately focused size. creditReconcileDelta is null and non-critical for a certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: State the credit total and the number of required courses (e.g., choose four of six for 12 credits).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are specific and readable - ECE 654 outlines the statutory/regulatory framework for offshore leasing, ECE 671 covers project finance and site selection - but the page lacks a credit summary and stated outcomes, and descriptions are truncated in the source.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program overview with credit total and outcomes so the strong course descriptions sit within a complete page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The content is timely and regionally strategic: offshore-wind policy (ECE 654), industry management (ECE 670), and project development/finance (ECE 671) map directly onto the US/New Jersey offshore-wind buildout, and the analytics electives (CS 644, MGMT 635) reflect the sector's data-driven asset-management turn.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an energy-markets/PPA finance elective so students can quantify project economics alongside the policy and development coverage.

descriptionQuality.justification: The wind-specific descriptions are specific and informative - ECE 654 details permitting frameworks, ECE 670 covers the wind-farm life cycle, ECE 671 addresses financial/economic feasibility - though CS 644 and MGMT 635 are generic analytics descriptions shared with other programs.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Only CS 644 states a prerequisite ('permission of the instructor') and the remaining courses list none; there are no dangling references, but the near-absence of prerequisites leaves little to assess and the one condition is vague.

missingTopics.topic: Wind-project finance, PPAs, and energy markets
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 671 introduces project economics, but no dedicated finance/markets course covers power-purchase agreements, tax credits, and electricity-market revenue that drive wind investment decisions.
ECE 654 addresses policy, but community engagement, fisheries/wildlife mitigation, and environmental-justice processes for siting are largely unaddressed.

missingTopics.topic: Supply chain and construction logistics for offshore wind
missingTopics.rationale: The certificate omits the ports, vessels, and supply-chain management that are decisive constraints on offshore-wind project delivery.

missingTopics.topic: Health, safety, and marine operations management
missingTopics.rationale: Managing offshore operations requires HSE and marine-ops governance content that no current course covers.

missingTopics.topic: Risk management and insurance for wind assets
missingTopics.rationale: Large capital projects need risk-transfer and insurance literacy that would complement the development focus of ECE 671.

emergingTrends.trend: Floating offshore wind and next-generation lease auctions
emergingTrends.rationale: Federal lease auctions and floating platforms are reshaping project development, extending ECE 654/671's policy and development coverage.
Investors increasingly require ESG disclosure for wind assets, a managerial competency the certificate should incorporate alongside ECE 670 strategy.

emergingTrends.trend: Data analytics for asset and portfolio management
emergingTrends.rationale: CS 644 and MGMT 635 position the program to teach production forecasting and portfolio optimization now standard in wind asset management.

emergingTrends.trend: Community benefit agreements and environmental justice
emergingTrends.rationale: Equitable siting and community benefits are becoming decisive to project approval, a natural extension of the ECE 654 policy course.
As wind pairs with storage and faces curtailment, the economics of integration and hybridization are a rising management concern.

topSuggestions: Publish learning outcomes covering wind-project development, regulatory/permitting fluency, and data-informed asset management.
State the certificate's credit total and required course count (e.g., four of six courses / 12 credits).
Add a wind-finance/energy-markets elective (PPAs, tax credits, market revenue) to quantify the project economics ECE 671 introduces.
Introduce supply-chain, permitting/stakeholder, and HSE content to cover the operational realities of delivering offshore wind projects.
Clarify CS 644's 'permission of the instructor' condition so non-technical management applicants can gauge fit.

wind-power-system-operation-maintenance-.name: Wind Power System Operation & Maintenance

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The six referenced courses cohere tightly around wind-farm operation and maintenance: ECE 651 (wind power transmission and grid interconnection), ECE 652 (HVDC design, operation and maintenance), and ECE 656 (power system dynamics) supply the electrical-systems O&M spine, while CS 634 (data mining) and MGMT 635 (data mining and analysis) support condition monitoring and predictive maintenance, and ECE 725 provides an applied independent-study capstone. This is a focused, well-aligned credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short sequencing note recommending the power-systems courses (ECE 651/652/656) before the analytics electives so students build domain grounding before applying data-mining methods.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and every course is depth 0 with missingCourseCount=0, so there are no dangling references, which is appropriate for a short certificate that draws standalone graduate electives. The ECE courses gate through stated background restrictions (electrical-engineering or physical-science background) rather than formal course prerequisites.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the prose 'electrical engineering background' restrictions into a stated admission prerequisite so entry expectations are unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present, so the specific O&M competencies (e.g., diagnosing turbine/grid faults, applying predictive-maintenance analytics) that the certificate certifies are not stated for prospective students or employers.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-4 learning outcomes tied to wind O&M practice, such as interpreting SCADA/condition-monitoring data and planning maintenance for grid-connected wind assets.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, so the total is not transparent, but the six 3-credit courses imply a conventional 12-credit / four-course certificate with one course of choice - a focused, appropriately sized credential. creditReconcileDelta is null and non-critical for a certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: State the required credit total and how many of the six listed courses a student must complete (e.g., choose four for 12 credits).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The course descriptions are specific and on-topic (HVDC O&M in ECE 652, power-system dynamics analysis in ECE 656), but several ECE entries open with 'Restrictions:' boilerplate and are truncated, and the page lacks a credit summary and stated outcomes.
dimensions.suggestion: Lead each course description with its substantive content and move restriction/background notes to a separate prerequisites field.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The content is highly current and regionally strategic: HVDC (ECE 652), grid interconnection (ECE 651), and power-system dynamics (ECE 656) are exactly the electrical O&M skills the growing onshore/offshore wind sector needs, and pairing them with data-mining analytics (CS 634, MGMT 635) reflects the industry's shift to predictive maintenance.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit condition-monitoring/prognostics course or module so the analytics electives connect directly to turbine-health practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative - ECE 652 details HVDC design/operation/maintenance and ECE 656 describes power-system dynamics analysis - though the two data-mining courses (CS 634, MGMT 635) are generic and several entries lead with restriction boilerplate rather than content.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Formal prerequisites are null across all six courses and background expectations are expressed as prose 'Restrictions' (electrical-engineering or physical-science background); there are no dangling references, but reliance on restriction text rather than stated prerequisites leaves entry requirements semi-formal.

missingTopics.rationale: The certificate teaches data mining (CS 634, MGMT 635) but no course covers the turbine SCADA sensors and vibration/condition-monitoring systems that generate the O&M data.
No dedicated reliability/prognostics course connects the analytics to formal maintenance-planning frameworks central to wind O&M.

missingTopics.topic: Turbine mechanical systems and drivetrain/blade inspection
missingTopics.rationale: The courses are electrical/grid-focused (ECE 651/652/656) with no coverage of gearbox, drivetrain, or blade inspection, a major share of real turbine maintenance.

missingTopics.topic: Offshore access, marine logistics, and HSE
missingTopics.rationale: Given regional offshore wind, the certificate omits offshore-specific O&M logistics and safety that distinguish marine wind maintenance from onshore.

missingTopics.topic: Grid codes, power quality, and fault ride-through
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 651 covers interconnection, but explicit grid-code compliance and power-quality diagnostics would round out the operations skill set.

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 634 and MGMT 635 position the certificate to teach ML-driven failure prediction, now the industry standard for reducing turbine downtime.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins for turbine and farm health
emergingTrends.rationale: Physics-plus-data digital twins are becoming core O&M tools and are a natural extension of the program's dynamics (ECE 656) and analytics content.

emergingTrends.trend: Drone and robotic inspection with AI defect detection
emergingTrends.rationale: Automated blade/tower inspection is rapidly displacing manual methods and would extend the certificate's data-driven maintenance theme.
ECE 652's HVDC focus aligns directly with the offshore-wind transmission buildout, keeping the certificate on the sector's leading edge.
As wind pairs with storage and grid-forming converters, operations skills for these hybrid assets are an emerging demand the certificate can address.

topSuggestions: Publish learning outcomes tied to wind O&M practice (fault diagnosis, predictive-maintenance analytics, grid-connected asset maintenance).
State the certificate's credit total and how many of the six courses are required (e.g., four courses / 12 credits).
Add a condition-monitoring / prognostics course so the data-mining electives connect directly to turbine-health practice.
Introduce turbine mechanical and offshore-logistics content to balance the electrical/grid emphasis with real maintenance activities.
Reformat course descriptions to lead with substantive content and move 'Restrictions' text into a formal prerequisites field.
